# Learnings Log

## 2026-03-24
- Project initialized with learning loop. Portfolio has 4 projects: LexisNexis, Pulse, Ghost, GoteFigure.
- Major content swap: YouTube Community and Nexus Studio replaced with Pulse and Ghost.
- All images resized with `sips --resampleWidth` for web optimization.

## 2026-04-13 — Pre-launch fixes batch

**What I did:** Backup, about page photo prep, overlap fix, responsive intro animation, tagline swap, Los Gatos→San Jose meta fix, red flag word audit, self-improvement loop setup.

**What worked:**
- The intro animation was already ~80% responsive (name uses `clamp()`, icons use `vw/vh` CSS custom properties, JS recalculates at runtime). The key improvement was adding a tablet media query to scale down burst radius and making icon sizes use `clamp(22px, 4vw, 28px)` with CSS variable `--icon-size`.
- Reading `--ix`/`--iy` from `getComputedStyle()` in JS means the tablet CSS overrides automatically apply to the cursor glide path — no duplicate breakpoint logic in JS.
- Circle wipe changed from `150%` to `200vmax` to guarantee coverage on ultrawide monitors.
- `.about-photo` was a placeholder div with centered text. Removed placeholder styles (`display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-size; color`) and replaced with `overflow: hidden` + child `img` styles. Removed `aspect-ratio: 4/5` to let the real photo render at natural proportions.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The uploaded photo file was not found at `/mnt/user-data/uploads/` — the path referenced in the prompt doesn't exist on this system. Had to defer the file copy until user provides the actual path.
- The about page overlap "bug" was less severe than expected — CSS Grid with `1fr 1fr` auto-sizes rows to the tallest cell, so the text column shouldn't actually overflow. Increased `margin-bottom` from `64px` to `80px` as a safety margin, but the root cause may be a different viewport or content state than what I tested.
- `getComputedStyle().getPropertyValue('--ix')` returns the raw string (e.g., `"15vw"`) not the computed pixel value, so I needed a `parseVwVh()` helper to convert `vw`/`vh` strings to pixels at runtime.

**What I learned:**
- Always verify file paths exist before planning file copy operations. Don't assume `/mnt/user-data/uploads/` exists.
- When CSS animations use `transform` and custom properties for position (`--ix`, `--iy`), reading those properties from JS with `getComputedStyle` requires parsing the unit string manually — browsers don't resolve custom properties to computed values.
- The intro animation uses `margin-left`/`margin-top` negative half-width for centering (not `transform: translate(-50%, -50%)`) because `transform` is already used by the burst animation. This is an intentional pattern — don't refactor to transform-based centering.
- `about.html` is at project root, so image paths are `img/about/rotem.jpg` (no `../` prefix). Case study pages in `work/` use `../img/` prefix.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `about.html`, `styles.css`, `main.js`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-13 — Fix about photo grey box + intro icon/name alignment

**What I did:** Two targeted bug fixes: removed grey bar below about page photo, restructured intro animation so icons orbit the name via a shared `.name-anchor` container with em-based positioning.

**What worked:**
- Grey box fix was straightforward: removed `background: var(--bg-secondary)` from `.about-photo` and its dark mode override, added `align-self: start` to prevent grid row stretching.
- Wrapping icons + name inside `.name-anchor` and making `.name-intro-inner` a flex container (`position: absolute; inset: 0; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center`) preserved the existing centering behavior while giving icons a shared coordinate origin.
- Setting `font-size: clamp(72px, 15vw, 150px)` on `.intro-icons` (matching ROTEM's font-size) means em-based icon positions scale proportionally with the name at every viewport size — no separate tablet/mobile breakpoint overrides needed.
- `getBoundingClientRect()` on `.name-anchor` (a static, non-animated container) is safe for calculating click position — only animated elements are unreliable for rect measurement.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- There were tablet and mobile media query overrides for icon positions (lines 320-340) using vw/vh that also needed updating. The em-based system made these redundant — they could be removed entirely since clamp() handles the scaling.
- The JS `parseVwVh()` helper needed replacing with `parseEmOrPx()`. The approach of reading `--ix`/`--iy` from `getComputedStyle()` still works — just the unit parsing logic changed.
- Click position calculation changed from `window.innerWidth / 2 + offset` (viewport-center-relative) to `anchorRect center + offset` (name-center-relative). This is the core alignment fix.

**What I learned:**
- When two visual elements must orbit around a shared center, wrap them in the same positioned parent and use relative units (em, %). Never position them independently with absolute viewport units (vw, vh) and hope they align.
- A background color on a parent div shows through when the child image doesn't fill the full parent dimensions. Fix: remove background, add `align-self: start` to prevent grid stretching.
- em-based positioning with a clamp() font-size eliminates the need for separate breakpoint overrides — the positions scale proportionally with the reference element.
- When converting vw/vh to em, calibrate at a reference viewport (e.g., 1440x900) and compute: `em_value = vw_value * viewport_width / (100 * font_size_px)`.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `styles.css`, `main.js`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-13 — Revert broken intro + fixed-pixel conversion

**What I did:** Reverted index.html, styles.css, main.js from `_backup-anim-and-photo-fix-2026-04-13/` (the `.name-anchor` rewrite broke the intro). Converted all intro animation responsive units (vw/vh/clamp) to fixed pixel values. Added 900px viewport cutoff to hide the intro entirely on narrow screens.

**What worked:**
- Reverting from backup was clean — the backup had the last known-good state before the `.name-anchor` architectural rewrite.
- Fixed pixel values calculated from 1440x900 reference viewport (e.g., `15vw` at 1440px = 216px ≈ 220px). Icons form a consistent orbit regardless of viewport width.
- The 900px cutoff in both CSS (`display: none !important`) and JS (early return in `playIntroAnimation()`) cleanly prevents the intro from running on viewports too narrow for the fixed layout.
- Removing the old `isMobile < 768` JS path and the tablet/mobile CSS media query overrides simplified the code — one guard at 900px replaces three separate breakpoint systems.
- Kept `vmax` in the circle wipe `clip-path` since it needs to cover the viewport.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The previous session's `.name-anchor` + em-based positioning approach was architecturally sound in theory but broke in practice — it changed the coordinate system for click position calculation and required extensive rewrites to JS timing/glide code. The simpler fix was fixed pixels + hide below threshold.
- The about.html grey box fix was in the current state, not the backup. Had to be careful to only revert the 3 specified files.

**What I learned:**
- When the user says "I want it consistent across sizes," fixed-pixel + hide-below-threshold beats fluid-scaling. Don't over-engineer responsive behavior for a decorative animation.
- Always backup the broken state BEFORE reverting — `_backup-broken-anim-2026-04-13/` preserved the failed approach for reference.
- An architectural rewrite (wrapping elements in new containers, changing coordinate systems) is high-risk for animation code with tight JS/CSS coupling. Prefer targeted property changes (vw→px) over structural refactors.
- When reverting from backup, verify which files to NOT revert — other fixes may have been applied to files in the current state that aren't in the backup.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `styles.css`, `main.js`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-13 — Rebuild icon orbit positioning from scratch

**What I did:** Deleted all existing `--ix`/`--iy` icon position values and replaced them with a clean set of fixed-pixel ellipse coordinates (rx=420, ry=200, 8 evenly-spaced angles). No other animation code was touched.

**What worked:**
- Surgical replacement: only the `--ix`/`--iy` values on lines 203-212 needed changing. The animation declarations (`animation: iconBurstAndFloat ...`) on the same lines were preserved as-is.
- The existing margin-based centering (`margin-left/margin-top: calc(--icon-size / -2)`) works correctly with the keyframes — no need to switch to `translate(-50%, -50%)`.
- No media query overrides needed — the 1000px breakpoint already hides all icons with `display: none !important`, so desktop icon positions only need to exist once.
- Backup before touching anything prevented risk.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- There was only ONE set of `--ix`/`--iy` rules (lines 203-212), not multiple overlapping sets from patching. The previous session's revert had cleaned up the duplicates.
- The proposed plan included switching to `translate(calc(-50% + var(--ix)))` centering, but this would have broken ALL keyframe transforms (they override transform entirely and don't include `-50%`). Flagging the conflict before executing saved a cascade of breakage.

**What I learned:**
- When a CSS system has been patched multiple times and behaves unpredictably, rebuild the affected section from scratch rather than patching further.
- Always delete stale rules before adding new ones — duplicate `--ix`/`--iy` declarations override each other silently.
- Before changing a centering strategy (margin-based vs transform-based), check whether keyframes override the same transform property. If they do, every keyframe must also be updated — or keep the existing centering approach.
- New orbit math: ellipse with rx=420px, ry ~173-200px. 8 icons at roughly even angular spacing. Cursor at (-420, 0), Figma at (364, -100), Code at (420, 0). Fixed pixels, no responsive variants.
- Capture Playwright screenshots at ~1.2s to see icons during burst phase; 3.5s is too late (circle wipe has already revealed homepage).

**Files touched:** `styles.css`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — LexisNexis case study content update (image processing + HTML mapping)

**What I did:** Processed 7 images from source screenshots (cropping, retina scaling, stitching, redaction), then updated work/lexisnexis.html with 4 image swaps and 2 new image insertions.

**What worked:**
- Pillow (PIL) for all image processing — crop, resize, GaussianBlur for face redaction, ImageDraw for text redaction with placeholder tags. No ImageMagick needed.
- Pixel scanning with `getpixel()` to find precise tag/text coordinates before applying redaction rectangles. Scanning for colored backgrounds (teal/blue) was more reliable than scanning for dark text.
- Using `glob.glob()` to find files by pattern rather than hardcoding filenames — macOS screenshots use Unicode narrow no-break space U+202F before AM/PM, which looks identical to a regular space but breaks `open()`.
- Backup before any processing: `_backup-lexisnexis-content-2026-04-14/` (folder + zip).
- Verifying all `img src` paths resolve to real files with a Python script after HTML edits.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- macOS screenshot filenames contain U+202F (narrow no-break space) before "AM"/"PM", not a regular ASCII space. `os.path.join()` and direct `open()` fail silently. Always use `glob.glob()` for screenshot filenames.
- First redaction attempt missed all targets — coordinate estimates from visual inspection of thumbnail-sized images were wildly off. Needed systematic pixel scanning to find actual positions.
- `ImageFont` import was missing from the `from PIL import ...` line in one script iteration, causing a `NameError`. Always import all four: `Image, ImageFilter, ImageDraw, ImageFont`.

**What I learned:**
- macOS screenshot filenames use U+202F not U+0020 before AM/PM. Always use glob patterns, never hardcode these filenames.
- For automated redaction: scan for colored backgrounds (tag pill colors) rather than text pixels — background colors are uniform and easier to detect. Then cover the entire tag region, not just the text.
- Automated coordinate-based redaction is inherently imprecise for final shipped versions. The draft redactions here are functional but manual touch-up in Preview/Figma would be more reliable for production.
- Source LexisNexis screenshots live in `img/lexisnexis/` as `Screenshot 2025-04-25 at *.png` files.
- Always read the current HTML structure and verify exact line content before proposing image slot mappings. Don't assume which slots exist.
- When adding images inside a `<section>` (like Outcome), the container div needs consistent class naming with existing image containers for styling to apply.

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html`, `img/lexisnexis/new/` (7 new images), `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — LexisNexis V2 image swap (4 replacements)

**What I did:** Replaced 4 case study images with user-provided V2 versions (Hero, overview, research insight, tag taxonomy). Converted img2.pdf→PNG via sips. Used -v2 filename suffix to preserve old files as fallback.

**What worked:**
- `sips -s format png input.pdf --out output.png` handles PDF→PNG conversion on macOS without additional dependencies. Output was 800x454 — small but functional.
- Using `-v2` suffix (`hero-v2.png`, `overview-v2.png`, etc.) keeps old files intact as fallback without cluttering the directory.
- Verifying old filenames return 0 grep matches after edits confirms clean swap.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- Nothing significant — straightforward swap since the previous session established the HTML structure and image slot positions.

**What I learned:**
- `sips -s format png` converts PDF to PNG on macOS natively. No need for ImageMagick or Pillow for simple format conversion.
- When replacing images iteratively, use versioned suffixes (-v2, -v3) rather than overwriting. This preserves rollback paths and makes it easy to diff between versions.
- V2 source files live in `img/lexisnexis/new/V2/` (Hero.png, img1.png, img2.pdf/png, img3.png).

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html`, `img/lexisnexis/` (4 new -v2 files), `img/lexisnexis/new/V2/img2.png` (converted from PDF), `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — Re-copy V2 images without processing

**What I did:** Re-copied 4 V2 images from source using plain `cp` — no Pillow, no sips, no cropping. Previous copy may have been processed; this restores pixel-perfect fidelity with the Gemini Nano Banana originals.

**What worked:** Plain `cp` + dimension verification via Pillow (read-only) to confirm source == destination.

**What I learned:**
- V2 images from Gemini Nano Banana are finished art. Do NOT apply any cropping, trimming, resizing, or Pillow processing to them — use plain `cp` only. The previous session's 10px edge trim was applied inappropriately and cropped content.
- When the user provides finished art from an external tool, always copy verbatim. Only process images when working with raw screenshots that explicitly need cropping/redaction.
- Cropped copies backed up to `_backup-lexisnexis-cropped-v2/` for reference.

**Files touched:** `img/lexisnexis/hero-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/overview-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/research-insight-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/tag-taxonomy-v2.png`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — V3 images from multi-page PDF

**What I did:** Extracted 4 pages from `img/lexisnexis/new/V3/V3imgs.pdf` at 300 DPI using PyMuPDF, then copied to active image locations via plain `cp`.

**What worked:**
- PyMuPDF (`fitz`) extracts PDF pages as high-res PNGs with `page.get_pixmap(dpi=300)`. Output was 10667x6667 per page.
- All 4 pages exported at exactly 16:10 ratio (1.600), matching the `.case-image { aspect-ratio: 16 / 10 }` CSS containers — zero cropping in browser.
- `pdftoppm` was not available on this macOS install; PyMuPDF was already installed and worked immediately.

**What I learned:**
- PDF-as-asset-container is a viable workflow when multiple images need to be delivered together at matching dimensions. One PDF with 4 pages is cleaner than 4 separate files.
- PyMuPDF at 300 DPI produces very large files (~10K px wide). For web use this is overkill but preserves maximum quality. Could downsample to ~2x retina (3200px wide) if file size becomes an issue.
- Always confirm page order with the user before copying — PDF page order may not match case study section order.

**Files touched:** `img/lexisnexis/hero-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/overview-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/research-insight-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/tag-taxonomy-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/new/V3/page-{1..4}.png`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — Archive cleanup

**What I did:** Moved 29 backup items (21 folders, 7 zips, 1 legacy folder, ~98 MB total) from project root into `_archive/`. Nothing deleted.

**What I learned:**
- Archived backup folders to `_archive/` inside the project root. Preserved for rollback. Project root is now cleaner to navigate in Finder.
- Future backups should be created directly inside `_archive/` to avoid root clutter.

**Files touched:** Moved 29 items into `_archive/`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-14 — Downsample V3 images for web

**What I did:** Downsampled 4 LexisNexis images from 10667x6667 (300 DPI PDF extraction) to 2560x1600 (2x retina for 1280px display) using Pillow Lanczos resampling.

**What I learned:**
- High-DPI PDF page extraction produces images far larger than needed for web. Always downsample to ~2x the intended display width (2560px for 1040px container) with Lanczos resampling.
- PNG `optimize=True` helps but images with complex gradients/detail (hero, overview) still land at 2-3MB. JPG at 85-90 quality would drop size by 60-70% with minimal visible loss for photo-heavy images.
- Simple diagram/taxonomy images (tag-taxonomy) compress well to under 1MB as PNG.
- Backup created directly in `_archive/` per the new convention.

**Files touched:** `img/lexisnexis/hero-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/overview-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/research-insight-v2.png`, `img/lexisnexis/tag-taxonomy-v2.png`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-20 — Mobile intro name clipping fix

**What I did:** Added one line to the existing `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block in `styles.css` to scale the ROTEM font-size on narrow viewports: `.name-line-first .name-char { font-size: clamp(56px, 17vw, 128px); }`.

**What worked:**
- Single-line fix inside the existing mobile media query. No JS changes, no new selectors, no timing changes.
- `clamp(56px, 17vw, 128px)` returns 128px at viewports ≥753px (so iPad + desktop behavior is preserved exactly), and scales down on phones: ~64px at 375px (iPhone SE), ~73px at 430px (iPhone Pro Max).
- Added the rule only inside the `max-width: 1000px` block, so desktop animation (≥1001px) is untouched per constraint.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The 128px `.name-line-first .name-char` rule had NO responsive override anywhere — the existing mobile media query only hid the icons/burst/reveal layers but left the name font-size alone. Easy to miss because the rest of the mobile intro flow (via `playNameOnlyIntro()` in `main.js`) is otherwise well-scoped.
- Letter-spacing (-0.04em) and transforms (vertical-only `translateY`) were ruled out as contributors — font-size alone was the overflow source. At Satoshi 900-weight, "ROTEM" at 128px renders ~440-460px wide, overflowing every iPhone viewport (375-430px). `.name-intro { overflow: hidden }` then clipped the R and M edges.

**What I learned:**
- For hardcoded desktop font-sizes that only render inside an already-constrained mobile media query, add a `clamp()` rule *inside that same query* — it keeps the desktop value unchanged (clamp returns the max when vw is large) while scaling on narrow viewports.
- When the mobile intro path (`playNameOnlyIntro()`) is functionally separate from desktop but shares the same HTML/CSS selectors, responsive overrides belong in the existing media query, not as new media queries.
- For word-sized glyph runs, estimate total width as `char_count × font_size × ~0.7` for 900-weight uppercase. ROTEM at 128px → ~448px. Compare against target viewport before assuming a responsive value "should be fine."

**Files touched:** `styles.css`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-21 — Copy pass for 2026 AI-native positioning

**What I did:** Ten surgical copy edits across the 6 live HTML files to reposition the portfolio for a dev-tools / AI-native startup job search. Repositioned the homepage subtitle and About opener toward "product designer building AI-native prototypes and the tools I wish existed." Added a post-LexisNexis bridge paragraph on About that frames the July 2025–present gap as active building (Pulse, Ghost, VR golf, daily art, apparel relaunch) rather than a job-hunt gap. Added a Skills & Tools section on About listing concrete tooling (Figma variables/Dev Mode, Storybook, Next.js, Tailwind, Framer Motion, Claude Code, Unity, OpenXR) and practices (design systems, design-to-code pipelines, WCAG AA, AI-augmented prototyping). Swapped the footer quote on all 6 pages from "Designing is just problem-solving..." to "Design infrastructure is leverage." Integrated the same leverage thesis into the LexisNexis overview as a standalone sentence between the inherited-system paragraph and the seven-months paragraph. Condensed the three-paragraph Phase 1 self-narrative on LexisNexis into a single tighter paragraph. Reframed Pulse and Ghost outcome footers to stop apologizing for mock data and instead name the next concrete build target (Pulse: Claude integration for real screenshot analysis; Ghost: Figma API wiring for one-click PRs from deviations).

**What worked:**
- The `about-section-heading` class (not `experience-heading`) is the standard for most About h2s — `Currently building`, `Outside design`, `Reach me` all use it. `experience-heading` is specific to the Experience section. Used `about-section-heading` on the new Skills & Tools h2.
- Wrapping the new Skills & Tools section in `<section class="about-aside reveal">` matches the `Outside design` section structure — heading plus prose paragraphs, no grid. The existing `.reveal` IntersectionObserver picks it up automatically with no JS change.
- The unique anchor strings in the user's find/replace blocks made every Edit call a one-shot match. No collisions between the old and new footer quotes because the Edit tool ran on each file separately.
- Batched 9 of 10 edits in a single parallel tool-use block (Change 6 ran in a second batch because `gotefigure.html` needed a fresh Read first). Dropping from 10 sequential calls to 2 batches was the right grain.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- My earlier grep using `"Built with: Next.js 14` (with the literal quote char) missed because the source uses `<strong>Built with:</strong>` — the colon lives inside the `<strong>` tag. Re-grepped for `Built with` (no quote, no version) and both files surfaced. Always grep with the minimum distinguishing fragment, not the full human-readable sentence.
- The existing About prose uses `&amp;` for ampersands (`UX Designer &amp; Researcher`). Wrote the new heading as `Skills &amp; Tools`, not `Skills & Tools`, to match. The user's brief used the plain `&` character — deferred to the codebase convention.

**What I learned:**
- Before adding a new section to an existing page, grep all existing `<h2 class=...>` occurrences on that page to see which heading class is standard. There may be more than one and only one is the default.
- For copy passes with many changes, draft all the unique-anchor Edit calls first, then batch them in parallel. Avoid reading the files a second time between edits — one Read per file is enough when the anchor strings are already quoted verbatim in the brief.
- Swapping the same footer quote across 6 files is 6 Edit calls, not `replace_all: true` on a single call — each file is independent. But the anchor string is identical, so it can be copy-pasted.
- Preserving HTML entity conventions (`&amp;`, `<em>`, `<strong>`) from surrounding content is more important than literal fidelity to the user's plain-text brief. When in doubt, match the codebase.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `about.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-21 — Comprehensive rebrand pass (31 changes across 6 HTML files)

**What I did:** Full copy/positioning pass targeting Founding Designer / Series A dev-tools and AI-native startup roles. New brand line: "product designer who ships systems, products, and prototypes in between." Reordered the homepage bento (Ghost moved to position 3, GoteFigure to position 4) and rewrote all 4 card descriptions to be sharper. Rewrote about-page intro paragraphs (UCSD, LexisNexis, thesis), Currently Building blurbs, Outside Design, Experience descriptions for LexisNexis and GoteFigure, and the Reach Me paragraph. Added a new "About this site" section explaining the build (vanilla HTML/CSS/JS, no framework, trigonometric orbit math for the intro). On all four case studies: changed every role title to "Product Designer" (dropping "& Developer" on Pulse/Ghost, swapping "UX Designer & Researcher" → "Product Designer & Researcher" on LexisNexis), added Figma to the tools list on Pulse and Ghost, removed all anonymous "expert feedback" quotes in favor of self-critique framing, rewrote Pulse + Ghost overviews to be shorter and more confident, condensed Ghost's Design Process from 5 iterations to 3 pivots, and tightened both outcome sections.

**What worked:**
- Reading all 6 HTML files in one parallel batch up front was much faster than reading-then-editing-then-reading-the-next-file. Single Read pass per file, all anchor strings already quoted in the brief, then batched Edit calls per file.
- Per-file Edit batching: about.html had 10 edits batched in one parallel block, ghost.html had 10, lexisnexis.html had 6, pulse.html had 6. All matched first try because the brief's find/replace strings were quoted verbatim from the source.
- Matched HTML entity conventions from existing prose: kept `&amp;` in role labels, used `<strong>` (not `<em>`) for the "design infrastructure is leverage" emphasis per the brief's explicit instruction.
- For the About "About this site" section, used `<section class="about-aside reveal">` to match the existing Outside Design / Skills & Tools pattern. The IntersectionObserver picks it up automatically with no JS change.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The bento reorder (Change 3) introduced a CSS layout side effect. `bento-row-bottom` uses `grid-template-columns: 1fr 1.35fr` — meaning the FIRST card in the row gets the narrow slot. The cards were swapped position-only (per "preserve HTML structure within each card — only swap card order"), so the bento-card-c / bento-card-d classes stayed with the slot. Result: Ghost now sits in the 1fr (narrow) slot and GoteFigure in the 1.35fr (wide) slot — flipped from the original. Flagged in the report so the user can choose whether to keep this or flip the column proportions to preserve the original visual weight of each project.
- Change 19 ("I created" → "I built") tried to find a fragment ending at "appearance" but the actual line in the source continues with " — so 'surface-elevated' and 'feedback-error' replaced ambiguous names like 'gray-200' or 'red-3.'" — had to expand the find string to include the full sentence and rewrite the leading clause and "not just appearance" → "not appearance" in the same Edit. The brief's quoted find string was a substring, not a full match.

**What I learned:**
- Before swapping the position of grid items in a layout that uses `grid-template-columns` with asymmetric fractional widths (1fr 1.35fr, etc.), check the CSS to understand whether the visual weight is tied to the slot (DOM position) or to the card (class). For position-tied layouts, "swap order" changes which project gets the bigger card — flag this side effect explicitly.
- When the brief quotes a find string that doesn't include the full sentence/line, search the source for the longest distinguishing fragment and rewrite the entire matched span — don't try to do a partial replace and assume the rest of the line stays attached cleanly.
- For multi-paragraph rewrites where the H2 heading stays but the body changes, include the H2 line in the Edit's `old_string` to anchor the match unambiguously, then put the same H2 back in `new_string` followed by the new paragraphs.
- Self-critique framing is stronger than appeals to outside authority. Removed all "Expert feedback was blunt..." and "The reviewer used the word 'brilliant'" quotes — replaced with "I looked at it and realized I'd built a well-designed template, not something interesting." Reads as judgment, not validation-seeking.
- "Product Designer" is the title for dev-tools / AI-native job hunting. "UX Designer" and "Product Designer & Developer" both signal the wrong tier — the first sounds junior, the second sounds like the candidate is unsure which lane they're in.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `about.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-21 — Bento bottom-row proportion flip (post-rebrand)

Flipped `.bento-row-bottom` `grid-template-columns` from `1fr 1.35fr` to `1.35fr 1fr` ([styles.css:634](styles.css#L634)) so Ghost (now position 3) gets the wider slot and GoteFigure (position 4) gets the narrower one — matches the post-rebrand project priority. Top row unchanged.

## 2026-04-21 — About photo grey box regression (2nd occurrence)

**What I did:** Re-applied the exact fix from the 2026-04-13 session: removed `background: var(--bg-secondary)` from `.about-photo` (light mode, line ~1087) and from the `[data-theme="dark"] .about-photo` rule (line 51), and added `align-self: start` to `.about-photo` so the grid cell sizes to the image rather than stretching to match the taller text column.

**What failed or surprised me:** This is the SECOND time this exact bug has appeared and been fixed. The 2026-04-13 entry records the first fix (same root cause, same remedy). Some later rebuild or refactor re-introduced both background declarations and dropped the `align-self: start`. Neither change-log nor prior learnings prevented the regression.

**What I learned:**
- **Protect these two invariants on `.about-photo`:** (1) no `background` property in light or dark mode, (2) always `align-self: start` in the `.about-content` grid. Any future refactor that touches the About hero must preserve both.
- The failure pattern is a grid cell with `stretch` align-self (the default) plus a background color plus a child image that doesn't fill the cell vertically. The image sizes by its intrinsic aspect ratio while the cell stretches to the tallest sibling — the background fills the gap. Spot this anywhere the pattern `parent grid cell + bg color + child img with height:auto` appears.
- **If this regresses a third time, promote to a documented component.** Extract the `.about-photo` pattern (or a generic `.media-frame`) with inline documentation (`/* REGRESSION-PRONE: no background, align-self: start */`) or move to a dedicated partial. At that point the cost of the extraction is lower than the cost of fixing the same three-line bug again.

**Files touched:** `styles.css`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-21 — About page restructure (split "How I got here" into two sections)

**What I did:** Split the single 4-paragraph "How I got here" block into two sections: kept UCSD / LexisNexis / thesis paragraphs under "How I got here" (inside `.about-content` with the photo), and moved the "Since leaving LexisNexis..." bridge paragraph into a new standalone `<section class="about-aside reveal">` titled "What I'm building now", inserted between `about-content` and `currently-building`. Also bumped `.about-content` grid from `1fr 1fr` to `1.1fr 1fr` so the photo column gets ~10% more width now that the text column is shorter, improving visual balance.

**What worked:**
- Reusing the existing `about-aside` pattern for the new section meant zero new CSS — `.about-aside` already defines the 72px top margin, 17px body type, and 640px paragraph max-width. Same pattern as Outside Design, Skills & Tools, About this site.
- Adjacent-sibling margin collapse: `.about-content` (margin-bottom: 80px) + new `.about-aside` (margin-top: 72px) collapse to 80px, matching the page's existing section rhythm. No custom spacing needed.
- The mobile override at [styles.css:1314](styles.css#L1314) stacks `.about-content` to `grid-template-columns: 1fr` regardless, so the 1.1fr bump is desktop-only by construction — no mobile tweak needed.

**What I learned:**
- Before adding a new one-off class for a single-column text section on the About page, check whether `about-aside` already matches. It is the canonical single-column pattern and should be preferred any time a new section is an H2 + prose with no special layout.
- When shortening one column in a two-column grid, a small `fr` bump (1.1fr 1fr or similar) is a lighter-weight rebalance than changing gap, padding, or aspect ratio. Keeps the rest of the rhythm intact.
- For narrative restructures (splitting one section into two), the story arc is the constraint — in this case, "How I got here" (past) and "What I'm building now" (present/future) read as a cleaner two-beat arc than a single dense block. Worth doing when one section has stretched past the photo or another visual anchor.

**Files touched:** `about.html`, `styles.css`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-23 — LexisNexis thumbnail + outcome footer image swap

**What I did:** Shipped two new LexisNexis images. (a) Replaced the bento-grid thumbnail with a typographic token-row + metric treatment, renaming the source `image (1) 2 [Vectorized].png` (95 KB) to `thumbnail.png`. (b) Replaced the outcome-section footer image (above "What I'd do differently") with a Before/After pipeline diagram featuring Figma/Storybook icons and a rust accent on the automated flow, renaming source `Frame 2.png` (1.08 MB) to `footer_apr23.png` and updating [work/lexisnexis.html:190](work/lexisnexis.html#L190) to reference the new path with fresh alt text. Both replace Mar/Apr-era versions.

**What worked:**
- Dated filename convention (`footer_apr23.png` in top-level `img/lexisnexis/`) keeps the new asset out of the `new/` legacy staging folder where the previous pipeline-comparison.png lived. Cleaner separation between historical batches and current live assets.
- Updating the `<img src>` instead of forcing the new file into the old filename (`pipeline-comparison.png`) meant the alt text could be updated in the same Edit call — both changes are semantically related anyway (image content changed ⇒ alt description must change).
- Three-way grep verification (old filename = 0 matches, new filename = exactly 1 live match, shared `lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` = 3 matches across index + ghost/pulse Other Work cards) caught all call sites automatically.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The task's literal instruction was to rename the current `thumbnail.png` to `thumbnail-palette-old.png`, but that name was already in use (from the previous task's backup of the true Mar 24 palette-version thumbnail). Blindly running `mv` would have silently overwritten the genuine palette backup. Fix: used `thumbnail-apr23-v1-old.png` instead and flagged the collision in the report. General pattern: before `mv`ing to a "backup" name, verify the target doesn't already exist.
- `lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` is referenced in THREE places, not one — homepage bento ([index.html:163](index.html#L163)) plus two "Other Work" cards ([work/ghost.html:186](work/ghost.html#L186), [work/pulse.html:184](work/pulse.html#L184)). Keeping the canonical filename unchanged means all three auto-update. If I had renamed the file instead of the source, I'd have needed to touch 3 HTML files.

**What I learned:**
- When backing up an existing file, always check whether the proposed backup name collides. Naming convention for repeat backups: add a version suffix (`-v1-old`, `-v2-old`) or a date (`-apr23-old`) rather than overwriting an existing backup silently.
- Cross-project thumbnail references: a project's thumbnail is referenced from its own page, the homepage bento, AND every other case study's "Other Work" grid. Filename-stable swaps are strictly better than path rewrites when the goal is a visual-only change.
- The `new/` subfolder inside `img/lexisnexis/` is legacy staging from the Apr 14 content batch, not where fresh top-level images belong. Drop new assets at `img/lexisnexis/` root; reserve `new/` for historical reference.
- Footer source file at 1.08 MB is acceptable as-is; the 5.3 MB Frame-2 precursor (aborted, deleted by user) would have warranted downsampling per prior learning. Rule of thumb: downsample any LexisNexis image >2 MB; leave smaller ones alone.

**Files touched:** `img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` (new, renamed from `image (1) 2 [Vectorized].png`), `img/lexisnexis/footer_apr23.png` (new, renamed from `Frame 2.png`), `img/lexisnexis/thumbnail-apr23-v1-old.png` (backup), `img/lexisnexis/new/pipeline-comparison-old.png` (backup), `work/lexisnexis.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`

## 2026-04-23 — LexisNexis hero image swap + img2 deferred

**What I did:** Shipped a new hero image on the LexisNexis case study. Moved source file `img/lexisnexis/new/Hero_lexis.png` (883 KB) up to `img/lexisnexis/hero_apr23.png`, backed up the previous V2-batch hero as `hero-v2-old-apr23.png`, and updated [work/lexisnexis.html:70](work/lexisnexis.html#L70) src + alt text to describe the Before/After four-step manual handoff → one automated JSON export. Held `img2_lexis.png` (1.34 MB, still in `new/`) unchanged — deferred to tomorrow for rebuild using a real Figma screenshot instead of an AI-generated variables panel.

**What worked:**
- Combined `mv new/Hero_lexis.png hero_apr23.png` in one shot — it renames and promotes the file out of the legacy `new/` staging folder in a single operation. Matches the top-level placement convention the earlier `footer_apr23.png` swap established today.
- A single `Edit` call covered both src and alt changes on the same `<img>` line (anchor was the full tag with old attribute values), avoiding a second touch.
- Pre-checking the backup name (`ls hero-v2-old-apr23.png` → "No such file or directory") before `mv`ing to it prevented the silent-overwrite failure mode that bit today's thumbnail task.
- Grep scope was tiny: `hero-v2.png` was referenced in exactly one live file. Confirmed up front so the recommendation (approach b) was grounded in data, not reflex.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The task brief said the source file was in `img/lexisnexis/`, but it was actually at `img/lexisnexis/new/Hero_lexis.png` (alongside `new/img2_lexis.png`). The `new/` subfolder keeps being a magnet for fresh exports even though today's convention moved live assets up to the top level. Finding the files required an `iname` find across 5 levels.
- First-draft alt text said "five-step manual handoff" matching the brief, but the parenthetical only listed four steps (Notify Engineer, Wait for Resource, Manual Code Updates, Code Review). Flagged the mismatch before committing; user confirmed the image shows four steps and corrected to "four-step." Catching this in the STEP-2 report saved a silent-error alt text from shipping.

**What I learned:**
- When a task brief says "file X is in folder Y," always `find` across 3–5 levels deep first rather than trusting the path literally — `new/`, `V2/`, `V3/` nested staging folders are common here.
- For numbered/labeled elements in diagrams, cross-check the count in alt text against the examples listed in parens. "a five-step handoff (A, B, C, D)" is internally inconsistent and a sign the description was drafted without an exact count.
- LexisNexis hero-batch filenames on live files now follow a dated convention: `hero_apr23.png`, `footer_apr23.png`, `thumbnail.png` (stable name). Backup suffix convention stabilizing as `-v1-old`, `-old-apr23`, or `-old-<date>` depending on what distinguishes the revision.
- Held assets should stay exactly where they were placed (do NOT pre-promote `img2_lexis.png` out of `new/`) — rebuilding from a different source file tomorrow may need the working-directory context of the `new/` staging folder.

**Files touched:** `img/lexisnexis/hero_apr23.png` (new, moved+renamed from `new/Hero_lexis.png`), `img/lexisnexis/hero-v2-old-apr23.png` (backup of previous hero), `work/lexisnexis.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`. Untouched (intentionally deferred): `img/lexisnexis/new/img2_lexis.png`.

## 2026-04-23 — LexisNexis hero framing: per-page object-position override

**What I did:** Added `style="object-position: center 15%;"` inline on the LexisNexis hero `<img>` at [work/lexisnexis.html:70](work/lexisnexis.html#L70) to fix bottom-edge cropping of the new `hero_apr23.png`. The global rule `.case-hero-image img { object-position: center top; }` at [styles.css:855](styles.css#L855) was left untouched so Pulse and Ghost heroes keep their top-anchored framing.

**What worked:**
- Inline style beat the `.case-hero-image img` selector by specificity without needing `!important` — clean override.
- Math-grounded value choice: image is 2560×1443 (≈16:9), container is 16:7; overflow at typical viewport is ~162px, and `center 15%` shifts the viewport down by ~24px — squarely in the user's requested 10–30px range. Avoided defaulting to the user's proposed 30% (~49px shift, past the upper bound) by running the numbers first.
- Single-line Edit with the full `<img>` tag as anchor — src, new style, and alt text all visible in one read. Zero risk of the alt-text reverting.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- There's already precedent for inline `object-position` in this project: [work/gotefigure.html:102, 113, 116, 119](work/gotefigure.html#L102) use inline `object-position: center` on gallery (non-hero) images. None are on `.case-hero-image` containers, so the new usage doesn't conflict — but it means the "inline style for per-image framing" pattern is already established in the codebase, not a novel approach.

**What I learned:**
- **Per-page `object-position` override pattern for `.case-hero-image`:** use inline `style="object-position: center N%"` on the single `<img>` tag. Acceptable for one-off per-page framing. If a second case study needs a non-default hero framing, *promote to a reusable class* (e.g. `.case-hero-image img.shift-15`, or a CSS custom property like `--hero-focal-y` on the container) rather than accumulating inline overrides. Two data points = class; one = inline.
- For `object-fit: cover` framing math: given image aspect ratio `r_img` and container aspect `r_cont` with `r_cont > r_img`, the vertical overflow fraction is `1 - (r_img / r_cont)`. For 16:9 image in 16:7 container: overflow ≈ 22.4% of image height. Each 1% of `object-position` Y ≈ overflow_px / 100. Use this to convert pixel intent → percentage without guessing.
- The project has 4 `object-position` rules in `styles.css` (lines 574, 855, 947, 1005). Before proposing ANY global change, grep for all of them and identify which containers they're scoped to — a single `object-position` change in styles.css can touch bento thumbnails, hero images, and case-body images all at once.

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, all other case study pages.

## 2026-04-23 — Bento offset revert + CLAUDE.md doc-reality sync

**What I did:** Corrective revert of the 2026-04-21 rebrand's unintended bento side effect. Restored the CLAUDE.md-documented 2x2 offset zigzag pattern (row 1 heavy-left, row 2 heavy-right): swapped the two `<a>` blocks in [index.html:186-206](index.html#L186-L206) so GoteFigure occupies `bento-card-c` and Ghost occupies `bento-card-d`, and flipped [styles.css:633](styles.css#L633) `.bento-row-bottom` `grid-template-columns` from `1.35fr 1fr` back to `1fr 1.35fr`. Also synced two stale claims in CLAUDE.md to match current code: the aspect-ratio note ("Different aspect ratios per card" → "Uniform 16:9 aspect ratio across all four cards", verified against lines 637–640 which all specify `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9`) and the hero-text quote (pre-rebrand "I design systems that make complex workflows feel simple." → current "Product designer who ships systems, products, and the prototypes in between."). Hero styling note "40px/700 weight" verified against `.hero-text` rule at [styles.css:506-508](styles.css#L506-L508) — still accurate, kept as-is.

**What worked:**
- Diagnostic-first workflow before the revert meant the exact scope (one HTML block swap + one CSS line + two CLAUDE.md fixes) was known before touching anything. The diagnostic turn's diff identified exactly 4 CSS deltas since the rebrand backup, and only one of them was bento-related — so the revert could be surgical.
- Whole-`<a>`-block swap in HTML rather than content-within-fixed-wrappers matches the pre-rebrand DOM literally (GoteFigure's `<a class="bento-card-c">`, Ghost's `<a class="bento-card-d">`). Visually equivalent to a content-only swap given lines 637–640 treat c/d identically, but the literal DOM restoration is cleaner to reason about later.
- Triple backup (index.html + styles.css + CLAUDE.md) into `_archive/_backup-bento-offset-revert-2026-04-23/` before any edit — in line with the project's standing "backup before touching anything" pattern.
- Flagging the HTML-content staleness (hero text) in the proposal even though the user's explicit instruction was scoped to CSS reality. User chose to include the fix in the same pass, avoiding a second CLAUDE.md-sync session later.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- First attempt to Edit CLAUDE.md failed with "File has not been read yet." Even though I had grep'd lines 70-71 content via Bash earlier in the session, the Edit tool requires an explicit Read call in the current session before allowing writes. Recovered by reading a 6-line window at offset 68 and retrying.
- The `_backup-rebrand-2026-04-21` folder name is slightly misleading — it's a backup of the state *before* the rebrand, not the rebrand itself. Naming convention should be `_backup-pre-X-YYYY-MM-DD` when the intent is "state before change X." Kept the existing name as-is (renaming the folder would be gratuitous), but flagging.

**What I learned:**
- **New protected invariant (per CLAUDE.md):** bento bottom-row uses `grid-template-columns: 1fr 1.35fr` (heavy-right), not matching the top row. The zigzag offset is intentional design. Future refactors that touch `.bento-row-bottom` must preserve this — any change that makes top and bottom rows identical has broken the offset pattern.
- When changing the DOM order of items in a grid with position-tied column weights, check whether any CSS rule ties per-card styling to the slot class (e.g. `.bento-card-c` vs `.bento-card-d` specific rules). Currently c and d are styled identically, so content-swap vs block-swap are visually equivalent — but that could change if per-card rules are ever added.
- Doc-reality sync passes should happen at the end of any session that changes layout values documented in CLAUDE.md. The 2026-04-21 rebrand session changed bento proportions but left CLAUDE.md claiming the pre-rebrand state; that drift fueled 48 hours of "is this a regression?" perception. Rule: when a learnings entry documents a CSS value change, grep CLAUDE.md for the old value in the same session and update it if present.
- The Edit tool requires an in-session Read of the target file before it will accept writes, even if the file has been grep'd or otherwise inspected via Bash. Always Read before Edit in a new file, even for a small change.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `styles.css`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/learnings.md`, `_archive/_backup-bento-offset-revert-2026-04-23/` (new backup folder with pre-change copies of index.html, styles.css, CLAUDE.md). Explicitly untouched: `about.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`, all 4 case study pages in `work/`, all images, every other CSS rule, every other line of CLAUDE.md.

## 2026-04-24 — LexisNexis bento thumbnail framing: per-card object-position override

**What I did:** Added `style="object-position: center bottom;"` inline on the LexisNexis bento thumbnail `<img>` at [index.html:163](index.html#L163) to rebalance its framing in the `.bento-card-a .bento-card-thumb` 16:9 container. The global `.bento-card-thumb img` rule at [styles.css:574](styles.css#L574) (`object-position: center`) was left untouched — Pulse, Ghost, and GoteFigure thumbnails continue to use the default center framing.

**What worked:**
- Inline style beats the global `.bento-card-thumb img` selector by specificity without `!important` — same per-card override pattern established for the case hero image yesterday ([work/lexisnexis.html:70](work/lexisnexis.html#L70) uses inline `object-position: center 15%`).
- Diagnostic-first approach surfaced the non-obvious fact that the user's originally-proposed value (`object-position: center;`) would have been a no-op because it matched the existing cascade. Catching this before executing avoided shipping a zero-effect "fix" that would have looked right in the diff but done nothing.
- Image composition analysis via Read-on-PNG (content lives roughly in rows 22–95% of the 2560×1593 source, with the largest whitespace region at the top of the image) grounded the `center bottom` recommendation in the source's actual composition, not guesswork.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- The user's initial description ("anchored to the top of its thumbnail frame, with visible empty space below the content") read as the opposite of what the image composition actually showed — whitespace was heavily top-biased in the source. Flagged the contradiction before executing; user confirmed the intended direction was to shift the image DOWN (i.e., crop the top whitespace more aggressively), which means `center bottom` was the right direction regardless of which way the perception had gone. Lesson: when a framing-issue description is ambiguous, present both directions (up vs down) explicitly so the user can confirm the intent rather than guessing from the English phrasing.

**What I learned:**
- **Per-card `object-position` override pattern for `.bento-card-thumb img`:** inline `style="object-position: …"` on the specific `<img>` tag. Same shape as the per-page case-hero override. Keep it inline until a second card needs a non-default framing — then promote to a reusable class (e.g. `.bento-thumb-shift-down`) or a CSS custom property on the card (e.g. `--thumb-focal-y`).
- Before recommending an `object-position` value, **always confirm the current cascade value** — `object-position: center` is the most common default in this codebase and a proposed inline of `center` would be a no-op. Grep `object-position` in `styles.css` first.
- When `object-fit: cover` content appears "off-center" within its frame, the root cause is often **asymmetric whitespace in the source image**, not a CSS bug. `object-position` can compensate for moderate asymmetry, but heavily lopsided compositions (e.g. >15–20% whitespace on one side only) are better fixed by re-exporting the source than by patching with CSS. Rule of thumb: if the whitespace ratio top:bottom is worse than ~3:1, re-export; otherwise `object-position` is the right tool.
- Two live per-image framing overrides now exist in the codebase: case hero ([work/lexisnexis.html:70](work/lexisnexis.html#L70), `object-position: center 15%`) and bento thumbnail ([index.html:163](index.html#L163), `object-position: center bottom`). **If a third appears, promote to a class** — two is the rule-of-two threshold stated in yesterday's hero-framing learning.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, all other bento cards (Pulse, Ghost, GoteFigure), all case study pages, the source PNG at `img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` (unchanged).

## 2026-04-24 — GoteFigure thumbnail + Overview image swap (with folder-convention alignment)

**What I did:** Shipped two new GoteFigure images. (a) Replaced the homepage bento thumbnail with a new lifestyle split-panel photo (two people in graphic tees on a coastal trail / back-of-tee at lake sunset), renaming source `gotefigure_thumbnailv1.png` → `thumbnail.png` after downsampling 2560×1327 → 2080×1078 with Lanczos + RGB conversion (unused alpha dropped) + PNG optimize. Final 2.62 MB — overshoots the 500KB-1MB ask but respects the explicit PNG format instruction (photographic content does not compress below ~2.5 MB in PNG at that resolution). Renamed previous `thumbnail.jpg` → `thumbnail-v1-old.jpg` (verified name did not collide). Updated [index.html:188](index.html#L188) src `thumbnail.jpg` → `thumbnail.png` and alt text to reflect the new lifestyle content. (b) Swapped the case study Overview image: updated [work/gotefigure.html:102](work/gotefigure.html#L102) src from `../images/gotefigure/model-hoodie.jpg` → `../img/gotefigure/overview.jpg`, aligning GoteFigure's Overview to the canonical `../img/<project>/` path convention used by Pulse/Ghost/LexisNexis (GoteFigure is the legacy outlier with 9 other `../images/gotefigure/` refs, untouched this session per user scope).

**What worked:**
- Diagnostic-first inventory report surfaced five things not in the brief — two of which changed the plan: (1) `model-hoodie.jpg` didn't exist on disk (the Overview ref was already broken), so the "rename old file as backup" step was moot; (2) the `images/` vs `img/` folder split meant the Overview target folder was genuinely ambiguous. Reporting these before file ops let the user make an informed option-A-vs-B call.
- Source-image preview via `Read` on the JPG confirmed the new thumbnail's subject had shifted (hand-drawn illustrations → lifestyle apparel photos), triggering an alt-text update the brief hadn't explicitly asked for. Reading the preview before swapping > hoping alt text stays correct.
- `Image.getextrema()` on the alpha channel (returns `(255, 255)`) confirmed the PNG's alpha was unused — safe to drop to RGB and save ~400 KB. This is the right pre-check before dropping alpha, vs assuming transparency is meaningful.
- Pillow `resize((w, h), Image.LANCZOS)` + `save(..., 'PNG', optimize=True)` produced the highest-quality PNG at the smallest size the format can achieve for the content. No shortcut available without format switch.
- Single-call Edit on each HTML anchor (the full `<img>` tag) covered src + alt simultaneously. Avoids the two-touch pattern (fix src, then re-find the tag to fix alt).
- Whole-folder backup (`cp -R img/gotefigure _archive/.../img-gotefigure`) captured the full pre-change state including the `not.jpg` mystery file and the ~4.6 MB pre-downsample source.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- PNG + photographic content + 2080px wide + "500KB-1MB target" is physically impossible on this source. Final file is 2.62 MB — >2.5× the high end of the size range. Downsampling further (1600px wide, etc.) would break the 2x-retina-for-1040px-display heuristic. JPG at q85 would have hit ~500 KB easily, but the user explicitly specified PNG — not swapping format without a nod.
- The `model-hoodie.jpg` referenced at `work/gotefigure.html:102` didn't exist on disk. Silently broken for an unknown period. Means `images/gotefigure/` has TWO dead refs live right now (`model-hoodie.jpg` at the Overview section AND `tees-collection.jpg` at line 138). Not in scope this session — worth a follow-up.
- Separately, `img/gotefigure/product-grid.jpg` exists on disk but isn't referenced anywhere (orphan, 55 KB). Not in scope.
- First Edit on index.html failed with "File has not been read yet" despite grep'ing the line — the Edit tool's in-session Read requirement from the 2026-04-23 bento-offset learning applied again. Recovered with a 5-line Read window and retried.

**What I learned:**
- **PNG is the wrong format for photographic content at 2000px+ widths.** When a user requests PNG for a lifestyle/photo thumbnail with an aggressive size target, flag the format-vs-size conflict before saving, rather than shipping 2-3× over the size target silently. Rule: photo-content PNGs at 2080px cost ~2-3 MB minimum with max optimization; only line art / illustrations / logos achieve <1 MB at this size.
- **Always `getextrema()` the alpha channel before assuming transparency is meaningful.** Many PNGs exported from modern tools include a full-opacity alpha channel that adds ~15-20% file size with zero visual benefit. Drop to RGB if extrema == (255, 255).
- **Pre-verify backup filename collisions before `mv`.** Continued the 2026-04-23 pattern — ran `ls <backup-target>` before `mv src backup-target`. No collision here; `thumbnail-v1-old.jpg` was safe.
- **Project has a folder-naming outlier:** GoteFigure's case study page uses `../images/gotefigure/` (9 refs) while every other project uses the canonical `../img/<project>/`. This session aligned ONE ref (Overview) to the canonical path — didn't consolidate the other 8 per user scope. Future rule: do NOT migrate the remaining 8 refs in a single batch unless the user explicitly asks — each migration requires verifying the target file actually exists in `img/gotefigure/` (vs `images/gotefigure/`).
- **`img/gotefigure/` has two live refs after this session** (`thumbnail.png` from index.html + `overview.jpg` from work/gotefigure.html). `images/gotefigure/` still serves 8 case-study refs. Both folders will coexist until an explicit migration pass.
- **GoteFigure case study currently has ~2 dead `img src` refs live** (`model-hoodie.jpg`, `tees-collection.jpg` — verified via ls). Deferred but flagged.

**Files touched:** `index.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `img/gotefigure/thumbnail.png` (new, renamed + downsampled from `gotefigure_thumbnailv1.png`), `img/gotefigure/thumbnail-v1-old.jpg` (backup of previous thumbnail), `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-thumbnail-overview-2026-04-24/` (backup folder with index.html + work/gotefigure.html + styles.css + full img/gotefigure/ snapshot), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, `img/gotefigure/not.jpg`, `img/gotefigure/overview.jpg` (left in place per canonical-path alignment), `images/gotefigure/` (entire folder, 8 other case-study refs), all other case study pages.

## 2026-04-24 — GoteFigure Overview V2 re-export (resolves Step 4 whitespace diagnostic)

**What I did:** Single-line src swap on [work/gotefigure.html:102](work/gotefigure.html#L102) — `../img/gotefigure/overview.jpg` → `../img/gotefigure/gotefigure_overviewv2.png`. This is the re-export that resolves the Step 4 whitespace diagnostic from earlier today. Alt text kept verbatim ("Model wearing GoteFigure OG Rabbit lavender hoodie") — already matched the new brief, so the alt Edit was a no-op beyond the src. Inline `style="object-position: center;"` preserved per instruction. Backup created at `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-overview-v2-2026-04-24/gotefigure.html`.

**What worked:**
- **Re-export beat CSS tuning.** The morning's Step 4 diagnostic offered three fixes — (a) re-export, (b) CSS zoom+position stopgap, (c) aspect-ratio container tweak. Rotem went with (a). The V2 source (2560×2552, roughly square, model ~90% of frame) completely eliminates the whitespace halo that made V1 render small inside the 16:10 `.case-image` container. Zero CSS changes needed. Confirms the morning's learning — "heavy internal whitespace → re-export" was the right call over CSS patches.
- **Pre-swap composition preview via `Read` on the PNG** surfaced the new aspect (1:1 square) + confirmed the whitespace was gone before writing. Same check from the thumbnail swaps today; the pattern holds: always eyeball a new image asset before swapping src, not after.
- **Filename-versioned src (gotefigure_overviewv2.png) instead of filename-stable (overview.jpg)** is consistent with Rotem's explicit brief this time (he wrote the literal filename). Contrasts with the thumbnail swaps, which used filename-stable `thumbnail.png` / `thumbnail.jpg` to avoid updating multiple HTML refs. Rule: follow the exact filename if the user writes it verbatim in the brief.
- **Alt text no-op awareness:** confirmed before editing that the new brief's alt matched what was already on line 102. Flagging the no-op in the pre-execution report meant no wasted Edit call AND no surprise if Rotem expected visible alt changes.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **V2 source is 7.64 MB PNG** — largest single image asset in the project by ~2×. Morning's learning ("photo-content PNG at 2080px = 2-3 MB minimum") still holds: at 2560×2552 the ceiling is higher. Rotem didn't ask for downsample; flagged in the report and moved on. Will likely need a downsample pass later to keep page load reasonable.
- **New aspect ratio may clip head and feet.** Source 1.00:1 in `.case-image` container 16:10 (1.60:1) with cover → ~386px of vertical image overflow. At `object-position: center` (the inline value), top and bottom 193px each get cropped. Model's head starts near the top edge of the source and feet near the bottom → both are at risk of being clipped in the rendered frame. Flagged as a visual-check item, did not change the object-position per "keep the existing inline style" instruction.

**What I learned:**
- **"Re-export" as Step-4 option (a) is the canonical fix for whitespace-halo images.** When a source has heavy internal padding around the subject, a new export with the subject filling 80-90% of frame is strictly better than any CSS-side zoom or aspect tweak. Keep this near the top of the options list in future whitespace diagnostics.
- **Three live refs in `img/gotefigure/` after today's sessions:** `thumbnail.png` (from index.html:188), `overview.jpg` (left in place but ORPHANED after this swap — no longer referenced from live HTML), and `gotefigure_overviewv2.png` (new, from work/gotefigure.html:102). **`overview.jpg` is now an orphan — candidate for cleanup in a future sweep.** Also: `not.jpg` remains orphaned per prior sessions.
- **For one-off versioned-filename swaps, the cost of a future re-swap is one-line-of-HTML per version.** The "filename-stable" argument only dominates when the image is referenced from 3+ call sites (like `lexisnexis/thumbnail.png`). For a single-ref Overview image, the user's explicit versioned filename is fine.
- **Aspect-ratio shift between image versions can silently break framing.** V1 overview = 1.20:1, V2 overview = 1.00:1 → same CSS container crops differently for each. When swapping an image for another with a materially different aspect, compute the new crop behavior and flag — don't assume the prior `object-position` value still makes sense.

**Files touched:** `work/gotefigure.html`, `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-overview-v2-2026-04-24/gotefigure.html` (backup), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, `index.html`, `img/gotefigure/gotefigure_overviewv2.png` (no downsample, no processing — shipped as Rotem delivered), `img/gotefigure/overview.jpg` (now orphaned but left in place), all other case study pages.

## 2026-04-24 — GoteFigure Overview V3 re-export (aspect-aligned to container)

**What I did:** One-line src swap on [work/gotefigure.html:102](work/gotefigure.html#L102) — `gotefigure_overviewv2.png` → `gotefigure_overviewv3.png`. Alt text and inline `style="object-position: center;"` preserved per instruction. Backup at `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-overview-v3-2026-04-24/gotefigure.html`.

**What worked:**
- **V3 aspect (2560×1593 = 1.607:1) matches the `.case-image` container (16:10 = 1.600:1) to within 0.4%.** Cover-crop becomes effectively a noop — what's in the source file renders pixel-identical on the page. This eliminates the aspect-mismatch risk I flagged on the V2 swap (head/feet clipping due to 1:1 source in a 16:10 container). V3 is the *correct* delivery aspect for this container.
- **File size dropped from 7.64 MB (V2) to 3.53 MB (V3) at same width (2560)** — tighter crop of head-and-shoulders composition has less scene detail, so PNG compression behaves better. Still oversized for web, but the morning's "downsample to 2080 + optimize" pipeline would now likely land ~2 MB, a reasonable range for a case-study page image.
- **Pre-swap composition Read** confirmed the subject was preserved (same model, same hoodie, same outdoor forest) and the aspect alignment before touching HTML. Same cheap-insurance pattern as the three prior swaps this session.
- **One-line Edit, anchor was the full `<img>` tag** — src changed, alt and inline style preserved without a second pass. Same pattern as V2.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- Nothing. This is the third V-rev swap on the same line today (V1 → V2 → V3), and the pattern is now fully internalized: Read → backup → Edit → verify → learnings. Each swap took under 2 minutes of tool calls.

**What I learned:**
- **Aspect-alignment between source and container is the quality multiplier, not megapixel count.** V2 at 2560×2552 (1:1) was technically higher-res than V3 at 2560×1593 (16:10), but V3 renders better in the target container because no information is lost to cover-crop. Rule: always match source aspect to container aspect when you have editorial control over the export.
- **Three-rev pace on a single image in a single afternoon is a normal part of getting a case-study image right.** The workflow of "Rotem re-exports → Claude swaps src + backs up + logs → repeat" is tight enough to iterate on composition without friction. No reason to push back on additional revs.
- **`img/gotefigure/` orphan inventory is now at THREE files:** `overview.jpg` (V1, orphaned at 2026-04-24 V2 swap), `gotefigure_overviewv2.png` (V2, orphaned at this swap), `not.jpg` (orphaned from prior sessions). Total orphan mass: ~7.8 MB. Worth a cleanup sweep when the final V-rev is locked in.

**Files touched:** `work/gotefigure.html`, `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-overview-v3-2026-04-24/gotefigure.html` (backup), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, `index.html`, `img/gotefigure/gotefigure_overviewv3.png` (no processing — shipped as-delivered), `img/gotefigure/gotefigure_overviewv2.png` (now orphaned, left in place), `img/gotefigure/overview.jpg` (orphan from earlier today, left in place), all other case study pages.

## 2026-04-24 — Hover-to-enlarge on case-study small images + Currently Building cards

**What I did:** Added two scoped hover treatments and two external YouTube links. (A) Subtle scale(1.03) + layered shadow lift on `.case-image-sm img` and `.case-image-trio img` — applies globally across all 4 case study pages without touching per-page HTML. (B) Same hover treatment on `.currently-building .building-card`, plus converted the GoteFigure and Rotems Adventures cards on `about.html` from `<div class="building-card">` to `<a class="building-card" href="..." target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">`, wrapping the full card (image + title + description). Gote Golf VR card left as a `<div>` (not clickable). Hover CSS wrapped in `@media (hover: hover)` so touch devices don't get post-tap persistent-scale bugs. All three new rules use existing `var(--ease-smooth)` — no new cubic-bezier values introduced.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic-first approach before CSS additions.** Reported (1) exact HTML of the three Currently Building cards, (2) the class name (`.building-card`, scoped under `.currently-building`), (3) existing ease variables (`--ease-smooth` at line 20, `--ease-out-expo` at line 21), (4) the exact CSS I'd add, and flagged that the brief's proposed `cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1)` didn't match any project variable — asked the user to confirm the `var(--ease-smooth)` substitution before executing. Caught the "do I invent a new ease curve or reuse the existing one" decision at plan time, not after code was written.
- **Scoping hover via class selectors instead of adding per-case-study markup.** One block of CSS at [styles.css:1009](styles.css#L1009) covers all `.case-image-sm img` and `.case-image-trio img` across all 4 work pages — zero HTML edits needed on any case study file. Applied globally via the existing class contract.
- **Parent-scoped hover selector for the building cards** (`.currently-building .building-card:hover`, not `.building-card:hover`) prevents the rule from bleeding into any future `.building-card`-named element elsewhere on the site. Same defensive scoping pattern as other section-scoped rules in this codebase.
- **Anchor reset pattern for card-wrapping links** (`display: block; color: inherit; text-decoration: none` on `.currently-building a.building-card`) is the minimum viable reset for making a block-level element with typographic children clickable without visual side effects. `display: block` was the load-bearing one — without it `transform: scale()` on an inline anchor behaves unpredictably.
- **`@media (hover: hover) { ... }` wrapper** for both hover rules is the canonical iOS/touch fix for the "scale sticks after tap" bug. Applying scale-on-hover without this media query causes scaled-up cards on touch devices after the user taps once. Should become a permanent pattern for any hover-only interactions in this project.
- **Existing `--ease-smooth` variable already drives hover timing elsewhere** in the codebase ([styles.css:544, 567, 575, 587](styles.css#L544)) — reusing it is strictly correct per the "reuse project conventions" rule rather than introducing the brief's `cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1)` (which is the standard CSS `ease` curve, slightly different character).
- **Six-file backup before touching anything** (`styles.css`, `about.html`, all 4 case study HTML files) into `_archive/_backup-hover-features-2026-04-24/` — standard pattern, cheap insurance. Case study files were backed up even though they weren't modified this session; the hover rule applies to them at runtime so they're in the blast radius.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **No existing `:hover`/`transform`/`transition` rules on `.building-card`** — the card had a clean slate, so the new hover block didn't need to coexist with or override any prior styles. Unusual for a card that's been in the design since the rebrand; worth noting that hover treatments hadn't been applied to the Currently Building section at all until now.
- **The brief's proposed ease curve** (`cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1)`, which is the standard CSS `ease`) is materially different from `--ease-smooth` (`cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94)` — "ease-in-out-quad"-ish). The project's smooth curve has a slower middle and harder acceleration at both ends vs. the brief's curve which has more linear motion. For a 400ms 3% scale the difference is visually subtle but not zero. User confirmed `--ease-smooth` before I proceeded — resolved via the diagnostic-first pattern.

**What I learned:**
- **`@media (hover: hover)` is the fix for the "scale sticks after tap" mobile bug.** Any `transform: scale()` hover rule on this site should be inside this media query from the start. Touch devices fire `:hover` on tap and the hover state persists until the next tap elsewhere — wrapping the rule in `(hover: hover)` restricts it to devices with true pointer hover (mouse/trackpad). Promote to a standing rule: **all future hover-based transforms wrap in `@media (hover: hover)`.**
- **Anchor reset for card-wrapping links** is a 3-line block: `display: block; color: inherit; text-decoration: none;`. If the card has its own background color / border, also verify the anchor doesn't inherit a `:visited` color — on this card it doesn't matter because `color: inherit` propagates to children via cascade, but it could matter on a future card with text at the root level.
- **Class-scoped hover selectors are preferable to DOM-scoped** — `.case-image-sm img:hover` rather than "every image in a case study page." The former is explicit about which images qualify; the latter is fragile to DOM restructures.
- **Diagnostic-first has become the default pattern for any multi-file styling task in this project.** Report the exact markup, exact class names, exact CSS, and flag any deviation from the brief (ease curve substitution, class name mismatches) before executing. The user consistently responds faster to a clean diagnostic than to a finished change that might need partial revert. Keep doing this.
- **Two live per-element hover patterns now exist in the codebase:** (1) magnetic tilt on bento cards (JS-driven, desktop-only, existing), (2) scale+shadow on case-study small images + Currently Building cards (CSS-only, `@media (hover: hover)` gated, new). If a third hover pattern appears, document all three together as a hover-interactions reference block in CLAUDE.md — two is still below the rule-of-two threshold for extraction.
- **Card-wrapping anchors in vanilla HTML should go on the outermost element of the card**, not inside. Wrapping the whole `<div class="building-card">` → `<a class="building-card" ...>` turns the entire click target into the link, which matches user expectation for "click anywhere on the card to navigate." Inner-only links (link on title, not image) create confusing click boundaries.

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (hover CSS for case-study small images + building cards, anchor reset), `about.html` (two `<div class="building-card">` → `<a class="building-card" href="..." target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">` conversions for GoteFigure and Rotems Adventures), `_archive/_backup-hover-features-2026-04-24/` (backup folder with pre-change copies of `styles.css`, `about.html`, and all 4 case study pages), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` (hover rule applies via class selectors; no per-page HTML edits), `index.html` (bento cards keep their existing JS magnetic tilt hover), `main.js`, `game.js`.

## 2026-04-24 — Currently Building cards: video wiring + VR static image (three-card hybrid)

**What I did:** Replaced all three placeholder divs in the Currently Building section on `about.html` with real content. (A) GoteFigure and Rotems Adventures cards (anchor-wrapped from tonight's earlier session) got `<video autoplay muted loop playsinline preload="metadata" aria-label="...">` elements pointing at the 960×540 transcodes shipped earlier today (`img/about/videos/gotefigure.mp4`, `rotems-adventures.mp4`) with JPG posters as fallback. Videos were pre-processed in a separate session: center-cropped from 720×1280 portrait to 720×405 16:9, scaled to 960×540 (per "Option 3" after 1280×720 came in over 1.2 MB), H.264 CRF 28, audio stripped with `-an`. Final sizes 0.92 MB + 0.88 MB — both under the 1 MB target. (B) Gote Golf VR card kept as a non-anchor `<div class="building-card">` with a static `<img src="img/about/gote-golf-vr.png" alt="..." loading="lazy">` — no YouTube link because the game isn't public yet. (C) Moved the VR screenshot from a kebab-case-unsafe path (`img/untitled folder/VrgolfGame.png`, which had both a space in the folder name and CamelCase filename) to `img/about/gote-golf-vr.png`, then removed the now-empty `untitled folder` via `rmdir`. (D) Added a single 6-line CSS rule `.building-card-image video { width: 100%; height: 100%; object-fit: cover; display: block; }` — mirrors the existing `.building-card-image img` rule so video and img children size identically inside the 16:10 container.

**What worked:**
- **Pre-flight ffmpeg pipeline shipped the right source files before HTML wiring started.** The two videos had already been transcoded (portrait → landscape crop, scale 960×540, CRF 28, no audio, faststart) and verified under 1 MB in a prior session — so the wiring step was just HTML + one CSS rule + one file move. Keeping the asset-processing step and the HTML-wiring step as separate approvals was right: if compression had come in bad, we'd have re-encoded without touching any HTML. Rule: **never wire an asset into HTML in the same turn that generates/transcodes it.** Two turns with a visual-check gate in between is cheaper than any "fix it all after" pass.
- **`autoplay muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"` is the canonical iOS Safari autoplay combo.** All four attributes are load-bearing: `muted` is the iOS 10+ autoplay unlock (iOS will NOT autoplay unmuted video regardless of other attributes), `playsinline` prevents iOS Safari from hijacking the video into fullscreen mode on play, `loop` makes it seamless for short B-roll, `preload="metadata"` fetches enough bytes to render poster + first frame without committing to the full video download. Dropping `playsinline` is the most common mistake — the video will play but iOS yanks it fullscreen, breaking the page. Drop `muted` and iOS won't autoplay at all.
- **Poster attribute + `preload="metadata"` combo** eliminates first-frame flash. Browsers render the poster immediately on HTML parse (no download wait for poster since it's a normal image), then cross-fade to the first video frame when autoplay fires — both iOS Safari and Chromium handle this cleanly with no visible flicker.
- **Anchor-wrapping-video is valid HTML5 and works without `pointer-events: none`.** `<a><video></video></a>` is flow-content-wrapping-flow-content, per spec. Click on the video forwards to the anchor because `<video>` without `controls` has no default click handler that intercepts. No `pointer-events: none` or z-index tricks needed. Videos without `controls` are passive targets.
- **File move before HTML edit** avoided the race where the HTML would reference a moved-but-not-yet-present image. `mv` + `rmdir` in one command (joined with `&&`) means rmdir only runs if the move succeeded, avoiding a dangling empty folder if the move fails.
- **Single-file batched backup of `img/` subtree** (via `cp -R`) captured both `img/about/` and `img/untitled folder/` into `_archive/_backup-video-wiring-2026-04-24/img/` — so both the pre-move source AND the pre-change about/ state are preserved. If the move had been wrong we'd have had full restoration data.
- **`.building-card-image` parent already enforced aspect-ratio + overflow:hidden** — the new child (video or img) inherits its sizing contract from the parent. One new CSS rule mirrors the existing img rule; zero changes to `.building-card` or the parent `.building-card-image`.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The brief's proposed HTML path used `../img/...`** (the `work/` convention), but `about.html` is at project root, so the correct path is `img/...` with no prefix. Caught this in diagnostic by grepping existing `src=` refs in about.html — the single pre-existing image at `img/about/rotem.png` confirmed the no-prefix convention. The brief even flagged the ambiguity and asked me to verify; the diagnostic step paid for itself here.
- **`rmdir` without any failure output means success.** I briefly wondered if `rmdir` had succeeded silently vs. just failed with no output. Confirmed via the subsequent `ls img/ | grep -i untitled` returning zero matches. Rule: after any silent file operation, verify the expected post-state with a separate command rather than trusting the absence of an error.
