- **mdls 962×540 quirk** (reported as 962 not 960 in Spotlight metadata) carried over from the 1283×720 quirk noted in the earlier transcode session. Same SAR/DAR rounding issue with libx264 output. Actual encoded frame is 960×540 — confirmed by the fact that `object-fit: cover` math works correctly at that dimension. Cosmetic, not a real issue.

**What I learned:**
- **Video wiring pattern for this codebase:** `<video src="..." poster="..." autoplay muted loop playsinline preload="metadata" aria-label="...">`. All seven attributes. No `controls`, no `preload="auto"`, no `preload="none"`. Document this as the canonical pattern for any future silent-B-roll video on the site. If you ever want to add controls or default-paused behavior, that's a deviation and needs its own design decision — don't quietly drop `muted`/`autoplay`.
- **iOS Safari autoplay rules (Jan 2026):** `muted` is REQUIRED for autoplay on iOS. `playsinline` is REQUIRED to prevent fullscreen hijack. Both in current mobile Safari 17+. Desktop Chromium autoplays muted video by default (unmuted video requires user gesture or site engagement score); desktop Safari is stricter than Chromium but lenient with `muted`.
- **Kebab-case normalization should happen AT the file move, not later.** `VrgolfGame.png` → `gote-golf-vr.png` is both a path fix (moving out of `untitled folder`) and a naming convention fix (camelCase → kebab-case). Doing both in one `mv` is correct — doing the path fix first and kebab-casing later would leave a temporary CamelCase file referenced by HTML for however long the session takes. One move, one rename, one HTML edit — atomic.
- **Three-card hybrid pattern** (2 anchor-wrapped video cards + 1 static-image div card): the differentiator is whether an external destination exists. Cards with a public link → anchor-wrap with video. Cards without a public destination → `<div>` with static image or placeholder. Mixing the two patterns in the same grid works because the hover treatment is on the parent `.currently-building .building-card` class — it applies uniformly to both `<a>` and `<div>` variants. Only the cursor changes (pointer on anchored cards, default on div cards) — the visual scale/shadow hover is identical.
- **Transcoded video lives under `img/` not in a new top-level `video/` folder.** Established convention this session: `img/about/videos/*.mp4`. Posters in the same folder as JPGs. Keeps asset paths aligned with the existing `img/<section>/` project structure rather than introducing a new top-level asset root. If video appears under other sections (work pages, homepage), mirror this: `img/<section>/videos/`.
- **Four live per-element hover patterns in the codebase now:** (1) magnetic tilt on bento cards (JS, existing), (2) scale+shadow on `.case-image-sm` and `.case-image-trio` images (CSS, tonight), (3) scale+shadow on `.currently-building .building-card` (CSS, tonight — now applies to cards containing videos and images), (4) anchor cursor-pointer on two of the three Currently Building cards. Two is the rule-of-two threshold from prior learnings — at four, the hover-interactions reference block in CLAUDE.md is becoming overdue. Candidate doc target: a "Hover & interaction inventory" section near the motion rules.
- **`cp -R` on folders with spaces in their names works fine with quoted paths** — `cp -R "img/untitled folder" ...` copies without issue. The space hazard is really for filename globbing and shell-script portability, not single-command cp/mv with proper quoting. That said, the underlying lesson stands: don't ship files in folders with spaces; rename at import time.
- **"Untitled folder" is the macOS Finder default for new folders** — this almost certainly came from a right-click → New Folder in Finder with no subsequent rename. Sign of a hurried asset drop. Watch for other `img/untitled*` folders in future sessions; they're typically stray asset inboxes, not structural folders.

**Files touched:** `about.html` (three card bodies rewritten — GoteFigure/Rotems with `<video>` elements, Gote Golf VR with `<img>`), `styles.css` (one new `.building-card-image video` rule, 6 lines), `img/about/gote-golf-vr.png` (new, moved+renamed from `img/untitled folder/VrgolfGame.png`), `_archive/_backup-video-wiring-2026-04-24/` (backup folder with `about.html`, `styles.css`, `img/about/` tree, and `img/untitled folder/` pre-move snapshot), `.claude/learnings.md`. Folder removed: `img/untitled folder/` (empty after move). Explicitly untouched: the video files at `img/about/videos/*.mp4` and `*.jpg` (shipped in the prior session, referenced as-is), `work/` pages, `index.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`.

## 2026-04-24 — Currently Building card reorder (Rotems → GoteFigure → VR)

**What I did:** Reordered the three Currently Building cards in `about.html` from Gote Golf VR → GoteFigure → Rotems Adventures to **Rotems Adventures → GoteFigure → Gote Golf VR**. Pure DOM order change — zero content or attribute changes. Backed up to `_archive/_backup-video-wiring-2026-04-24/about-pre-reorder.html` before editing.

**What worked:**
- **Post-edit full-content diff against the pre-reorder backup** — `diff <(grep ... sort) <(grep ... sort)` on all anchor hrefs + image/video srcs returned exit 0, proving no content was added, removed, or mutated; only DOM position changed. This is the right verification pattern for reorder tasks: sort-and-diff the stable-identifier set before and after. If the multiset is identical, you have a pure reorder by definition.
- **Backup to the existing session folder** (`_archive/_backup-video-wiring-2026-04-24/about-pre-reorder.html`) rather than creating a new dated folder. Multiple related changes on the same file within the same day consolidate into one backup folder with differentiating filename suffixes (`about-pre-reorder.html`) — cleaner than a proliferation of identically-dated backup folders.
- **Verification by card count + ordered title grep** caught the bug within one check. `grep -c` on the card-root pattern confirmed exactly 3 cards; `grep -n` on `building-card-title` showed the titles in the correct order.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **First Edit attempt left a duplicate Rotems Adventures block in the file.** The old_string covered all three existing cards but the new_string I constructed included the Rotems Adventures block PLUS the original GoteFigure block PLUS the original Rotems Adventures block again (bringing the file to 4 cards with Rotems duplicated). I caught this on the first verification step (`grep -c` returned 3 but titles grep showed "Rotems Adventures" appearing twice at lines 95 and 129). Recovered with a second Edit that spliced out the duplicate block (22 lines including trailing blank line). This would not have been caught by a line-count check alone — line counts can match even when content is duplicated and something else is missing. The identifier-multiset diff would have caught it too, but the title grep found it faster.
- **Root cause of the duplicate:** I mentally modeled the swap as "move block A to after block C" but wrote the new_string by copy-pasting all three blocks in the new order on top of a mental model of an empty slot — and kept one stale block in the middle. Lesson: for a three-item reorder, **write the new_string from scratch in the new order, don't copy-paste the original and splice**. Copy-paste invites partial-splice bugs.

**What I learned:**
- **For any DOM-order-only edit, verify with an identifier-multiset diff.** Extract the stable identifiers (hrefs, srcs, titles) from the before and after states, sort each, and diff. Exit 0 = pure reorder. Non-zero = content changed, investigate. This is the canonical verification pattern for reorders — line count and total file size are too coarse to catch duplicate-with-missing bugs.
- **Pre-edit verification isn't enough for reorder tasks.** The diagnostic step I normally run (reading the current state, reporting it) caught the BEFORE state, but didn't catch the bug I made in constructing the AFTER state. Reorders need a post-edit identifier-multiset check in addition to pre-edit structure verification.
- **`grep -c` for card count + `grep -n` for ordered titles** is the two-check verification minimum. Count catches dropped cards, ordered titles catches order bugs AND duplicates (same title appearing twice). Both are fast — no excuse to skip.
- **The Currently Building card order is now: Rotems Adventures → GoteFigure → Gote Golf VR.** The video-first ordering puts the two anchor-wrapped, autoplay-video cards in the first two slots of the grid, drawing the eye to the clickable content. Gote Golf VR (static image, non-clickable) occupies the third slot. On mobile (single-column stack), this means videos play first in scroll order before the reader reaches the VR screenshot. Worth remembering if a fourth card is added later — the "non-clickable static content goes last" pattern holds.

**Files touched:** `about.html` (three card blocks reordered, zero content changes), `_archive/_backup-video-wiring-2026-04-24/about-pre-reorder.html` (pre-reorder backup), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css`, all video/image assets, all other HTML files.

## 2026-04-24 — GoteFigure case-study two-issue fix (missing trio image + stale Other Work placeholder)

**What I did:** Fixed two independent visual bugs on `work/gotefigure.html`. **Issue 1** (Storefront trio, [work/gotefigure.html:138](work/gotefigure.html#L138)): the middle `<img>` in the `.case-image-trio` section pointed at `../images/gotefigure/tees-collection.jpg` — a file that never existed on disk (verified: zero matches across all of `_archive/` too, so not a regression — the HTML ref was aspirational from a prior era). Swapped to `../images/gotefigure/product-grid.jpg`, an existing 55 KB Shopify product-listing screenshot that had been sitting orphaned in the folder since March. Updated the alt text from "GoteFigure tees collection and hoodie promo section" to "GoteFigure Shopify product listing — hoodies and crewneck sweatshirts with pricing" to match what's actually in the image (four apparel product cards: 2 hoodies, 2 crewneck sweatshirts with visible pricing). **Issue 2** (Other Work LexisNexis card, [work/gotefigure.html:154-160](work/gotefigure.html#L154-L160)): card was using the pre-thumbnail-asset-era colored-div placeholder (`<div class="project-thumbnail thumb-lexisnexis"><div class="thumb-inner">LexisNexis</div></div>`) — a faded-gray "LexisNexis" label centered on a light-blue-gray #E8EDF2 background — while every other Other Work LexisNexis ref across the site (5 locations total: in `pulse.html`, `ghost.html`, homepage bento, and two other cards) had migrated to the current `<img src="../img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png">` pattern during the 2026-04-23 thumbnail swap. Migrated the last stale card to match.

**What worked:**
- **Inspecting the candidate replacement image via `Read` on the JPG** before swapping ruled out the "guess from filename" failure mode. The file is named `product-grid.jpg` but I needed to confirm: is it actually a product grid? What product? The preview confirmed it's a 2×2 storefront grid with four apparel items (OG Rabbit Pink Hooded Sweatshirt $40, Triple Figure Hooded Sweatshirt $40, Cyclops Crewneck Sweatshirt $31.50, Triple Figure Crewneck Sweatshirt $31.50) — perfect storefront-narrative fit, flanked by PDP image left + homepage image right in the trio. Would have been wrong to assume from filename alone.
- **Identifier-multiset audit across all 4 case study Other Work sections** (done in the diagnostic step) surfaced Issue 2's asymmetry cleanly: 5 of 6 refs used the `<img>` pattern, 1 of 6 (gotefigure.html's LexisNexis card) used the old colored-div pattern. The asymmetry was the diagnostic signal — if I had only grepped `work/gotefigure.html` in isolation, the stale pattern would have looked like "that's how the site does it" rather than "this is the last holdout." **Rule: when fixing a stale-pattern bug, audit the same pattern across all equivalent call sites in the codebase first. Uniformity is the tell.**
- **Dual-fix approval gating** (user pre-approved Issue 2 in the task brief, held Issue 1 for inspection-then-approval) matched the risk profile: Issue 2 was a pure migration to an already-established pattern with a verified asset; Issue 1 required judgment about whether an orphan image was editorially suitable. Different fixes = different approval gates. Don't batch-approve both when one needs judgment.
- **Single-Edit atomic swap for Issue 1** (both `src` and `alt` changed in one Edit call on the full `<img>` tag line as the anchor) means zero risk of the alt reverting or getting out of sync with the src. Same pattern as yesterday's LexisNexis hero swap.
- **Backup as a standalone subfolder** (`_archive/_backup-gotefigure-imgfix-2026-04-24/`) rather than consolidating into the earlier GoteFigure backup folders from today — the scope is distinct enough (HTML-only fix, no asset changes) to warrant a named folder that documents intent. Makes future archaeology easier.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **`tees-collection.jpg` never existed on disk — not even in `_archive/`.** I expected at minimum one backup to contain it. Zero matches across `find _archive -iname "tees-collection*"`. This is a different failure class than "image was deleted" — the HTML reference was a placeholder from v2 or earlier that was never actually shipped as an asset. Means there's no "correct" image to restore; the fix is necessarily a replacement or a layout change, not a recovery.
- **The `product-grid.jpg` orphan predicted this exact fix 6 sessions ago.** The 2026-04-24 GoteFigure Overview V2 session's learnings entry flagged: `"img/gotefigure/product-grid.jpg exists on disk but isn't referenced anywhere (orphan, 55 KB). Not in scope."` — but actually the orphan was in `images/` (plural, the legacy folder), not `img/`. That prior learning mis-labeled the path. Still: the fact that the orphan was already flagged meant I could close it cleanly in this fix rather than leaving both a broken ref AND an orphan on the page. **Two bugs cancelled each other.**
- **The Apr 23 thumbnail swap verification** (per that session's learnings: "three-way grep verification (old filename = 0 matches, new filename = exactly 1 live match, shared `lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` = 3 matches across index + ghost/pulse Other Work cards)") was filename-stable — it only checked pages that already referenced `lexisnexis/thumbnail.png`. The gotefigure.html card didn't reference that file at all (it used a CSS-driven colored-div placeholder with no image), so it was invisible to the swap's sweep. **Filename-stable swaps only verify what's already keyed on that filename. They cannot detect pages that should reference the swapped file but don't.** Lesson below.

**What I learned:**
- **Filename-stable swaps verify forward, not backward.** An Apr 23-style swap where the filename is preserved (`thumbnail.png` stays `thumbnail.png`) naturally catches every page that already refs that filename — but is blind to pages that should ref it but don't. To catch the inverse, add a second audit step after the swap: **for each project with a thumbnail, grep for ALL alternative thumbnail representations** (colored-div class names like `.thumb-lexisnexis`, aspirational filenames like `tees-collection.jpg`, placeholder text like `"VR clip / screenshot"`) **and flag any that still render as non-image content on live pages.** This is a one-time audit per thumbnail-migration event, not per swap.
- **Stale placeholder pattern audit across all 4 case studies is now overdue.** Other Work sections confirmed clean (5/6 current + 1 fixed). But the same class-of-problem likely exists elsewhere: `.case-image-sm` sections, hero images, inline illustrations that may have been placeholders pre-asset-era. Propose a future sweep: grep every page for known placeholder class names (`--placeholder`, `thumb-*` colored-div pattern, `thumb-inner` label inside a thumbnail div) and confirm each is either intentionally still a placeholder or has a migration target. Estimated 15 minutes, high value.
- **When an image reference points to a file that never existed, the recovery tree has three branches, in order of preference:** (1) re-export or commission a new asset matching the original alt text; (2) swap to an existing asset that fits the editorial intent (verify via visual preview, correct the alt text); (3) restructure the layout to remove the slot. Branch 2 is the best of "easy wins" if an orphan fits the intent — it closes two issues at once (broken ref + orphan). Branch 1 costs asset-creation time; branch 3 costs design rhythm.
- **The `images/` vs `img/` folder split on GoteFigure** remains a slow-burn source of bugs. The Apr 24 Overview session noted: "GoteFigure case study page uses `../images/gotefigure/` (9 refs) while every other project uses the canonical `../img/<project>/`". After this session: 8 `images/` refs still live on gotefigure.html (down from 9 because the Overview swap moved one), plus 1 `img/` ref for the overview. **Recommend a future consolidation pass** that verifies each `images/gotefigure/` file has an `img/gotefigure/` twin (or gets copied over) and migrates all 8 refs in one coordinated edit. Until then, every ref into `images/gotefigure/` carries the ambient risk of silently breaking when the folder drift continues.
- **Orphan inventory for `images/gotefigure/` after this fix:** 7 of 8 files now referenced (up from 7 of 8 before — `product-grid.jpg` moved from orphan to referenced, and nothing was released to orphan state). Known remaining orphan or potentially-broken refs: none on gotefigure.html as of this session. The broader `.DS_Store` and `not.jpg`-in-`img/gotefigure` orphans from prior sessions are unchanged — still deferred.
- **Read-to-verify on image assets** (using the Read tool on a JPG/PNG and inspecting the returned rendering) is the cheapest sanity check before a visual swap. Should be default practice, not exceptional. Takes ~2 seconds of tool time; prevents the "swapped to a file whose contents don't match the narrative" failure mode.

**Files touched:** `work/gotefigure.html` (two Edit calls: Issue 2 migration on lines 154-160, Issue 1 src+alt update on line 138), `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-imgfix-2026-04-24/gotefigure.html` (pre-fix backup), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `styles.css` (the `.thumb-lexisnexis` CSS rule at line 616 is now unreferenced but left in place — single-rule orphan CSS isn't worth hunting in the same pass per prior learnings), all other HTML files, all image assets, the legacy `images/gotefigure/` vs canonical `img/gotefigure/` folder split remains as-is.

## 2026-04-24 — GoteFigure product-grid V2 swap (extension change + aspect mismatch flagged)

**What I did:** Shipped a new product-grid image on the GoteFigure case study. Source at `~/Downloads/product-gridv2.png` (620 KB, 1189×1475). Renamed the prior v1 from `images/gotefigure/product-grid.jpg` → `images/gotefigure/product-grid-v1-old.jpg` (55 KB, preserved as backup), copied the new file to `images/gotefigure/product-grid.png` (canonical name, new extension), and updated [work/gotefigure.html:138](work/gotefigure.html#L138) `src` from `product-grid.jpg` → `product-grid.png`. Alt text preserved verbatim ("GoteFigure Shopify product listing — hoodies and crewneck sweatshirts with pricing") — content of the new image is identical to v1 (same 4 products, same 2×2 layout, same prices). Backup at `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-imgfix-2026-04-24/gotefigure-pre-v2-product-grid.html` (consolidated into today's existing GoteFigure imgfix backup folder).

**What worked:**
- **Read-to-verify on the new PNG before any file operation** (per the rule logged in the previous learnings entry). Confirmed the v2 content before swapping: same four products (OG Rabbit Pink Hooded Sweatshirt $40, Triple Figure Hooded Sweatshirt $40, Cyclops Crewneck Sweatshirt $31.50, Triple Figure Crewneck Sweatshirt $31.50), same visual arrangement. This confirmed: (a) the swap doesn't require an alt-text change; (b) the swap is a quality/resolution upgrade, not a content change. Two-second tool call prevented shipping a potentially-wrong alt text.
- **Extension-change handling matched the brief's branching logic.** New file was `.png`, old was `.jpg` — per brief step 3, HTML src needed updating. Not a silent filename-stable swap. Caught and executed in one Edit.
- **`mv` for the old + `cp` for the new** (rather than `cp -f` to overwrite) kept both files on disk: the v1-old rename is unambiguous about what was replaced, and the copy from Downloads preserves the source file there in case of re-swap. Source file in `~/Downloads/` intentionally left in place — cleaning it up is a user choice, not an automatic file op.
- **Name normalization on copy** — the Downloads filename was `product-gridv2.png` (no hyphen, `v2` suffix). The canonical on-site name stayed `product-grid.png`, dropping the `v2` suffix. Keeps the filename stable for any future swap-in-place (`v3`, `v4`...) and matches the brief's "Overwrite the existing product-grid.jpg if the new file is also product-grid" wording — the implied canonical name is `product-grid`, not `product-gridv2`.
- **Single Edit call for the src change** using the full `<img>` tag as anchor — covered the extension change in one touch, alt text preserved incidentally since it was part of the anchor.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Aspect mismatch between the new PNG and the `.case-image-sm` container is severe.** New image is 1189×1475 → aspect ratio **0.806 (portrait, roughly 4:5)**. The `.case-image-sm` CSS at [styles.css:987-999](styles.css#L987-L999) enforces `aspect-ratio: 16 / 10` (1.60, landscape). With the default `.case-image-sm img { object-fit: cover; object-position: center top; }` at [styles.css:1001-1007](styles.css#L1001-L1007), `cover` will crop substantially: container wants 1.60:1 ratio, source is 0.806:1 — the visible portion is only `0.806 / 1.60 ≈ 50%` of the image height, anchored to the top. Visual result: the top row of products (two hoodies + their titles/prices) will render close to edge-to-edge; the bottom row (two crewnecks) will be **clipped below the visible frame entirely** because `object-position: center top` anchors to the top. **Only 2 of 4 products will be visible on screen.** Flagged to the user in the report.
- **Per-container CSS inheritance** bit again. The `.case-image-sm img` rule has `object-position: center top` (from line 1005) — likely the right choice for landscape content hero screenshots but wrong for a portrait product grid where the logical anchor would be `center center`. No per-image override currently exists for `.case-image-sm`, so there's no established pattern here — a third per-element override would push toward the rule-of-two extraction threshold from prior learnings (two existing overrides: LexisNexis case hero at `work/lexisnexis.html:70` and LexisNexis bento thumbnail at `index.html:163`).
- **620 KB PNG is ~11x bigger than the 55 KB JPG it replaces.** For a single `.case-image-sm` in a 3-up trio, this is acceptable but not optimal. The v1 was presumably JPG-from-Shopify-export at modest quality; the v2 is a fresh PNG with no compression optimization. A downsample + convert-to-JPG pass (target ~150 KB at Q85) would align with the size profile of the flanking images without visible quality loss.

**What I learned:**
- **Extension changes (jpg ↔ png) are a special case of "filename-stable" swap.** The path appears to be stable (`product-grid`) but the browser cache won't identify them as the same resource (different URL), and any other HTML or CSS that references `product-grid.jpg` becomes a dead link. Always grep the codebase for the OLD full filename (with extension) after an extension change, not just the bare stem — `product-grid.jpg` could be referenced elsewhere (social meta image, schema JSON-LD, other case study pages). Today's fix was single-ref (verified by grep), but the check should be mandatory on extension-change swaps.
- **Aspect-ratio check on image swaps is mandatory when the new file has a meaningfully different aspect than the old.** `.case-image-sm` is 16:10; the V1 product-grid.jpg was almost certainly landscape-ish (Shopify collection exports typically render ~1200×800 landscape). V2 PNG at 4:5 portrait is a completely different shape — and the existing CSS will crop 50% of its vertical content. Should have pre-computed the cover-crop before swapping and flagged it in a pre-execution report, not a post-execution report. **Rule: when swapping an image for another with a materially different aspect (>25% aspect ratio delta), compute the cover-crop math and flag the result before executing. If the crop loses key subject matter, pause and ask for a re-export.**
- **Name normalization convention on external imports:** `~/Downloads/file-nameVN.ext` → `<target-folder>/file-name.ext` (drop the `VN` suffix, drop any concatenation quirks like missing hyphens). Exception: if the user's brief quotes the literal destination filename with the version suffix, preserve it verbatim (the gotefigure_overviewv3.png pattern from earlier today). Today's brief didn't name the destination — convention said drop the suffix.
- **Post-swap grep should use the OLD full filename (with extension).** Today I verified by grepping `product-grid` (bare stem) which matched both the old-backup filename and the new canonical filename — didn't differentiate whether any other page/file still linked to the old `.jpg`. Better check: `grep -rn "product-grid.jpg" .` after an extension-change swap. Zero matches = clean; 1+ matches = additional call sites to update. (Checked retroactively — zero matches. But the verification step should be built into the swap workflow, not done as archaeology.)
- **File-size growth from format change warrants flagging.** Any PNG replacing a JPG at the same visual content will be 5-10x larger by default — this is a physics property of lossless vs lossy compression, not a mistake. Worth flagging in the report so the user can decide whether to downsample/re-encode. Don't silently ship a 10x size increase on a case-study page.

**Files touched:** `images/gotefigure/product-grid-v1-old.jpg` (renamed from `product-grid.jpg`, preserved as backup), `images/gotefigure/product-grid.png` (new, copied from `~/Downloads/product-gridv2.png`), `work/gotefigure.html` (one Edit: src extension change on line 138, alt preserved), `_archive/_backup-gotefigure-imgfix-2026-04-24/gotefigure-pre-v2-product-grid.html` (pre-swap backup, consolidated into the earlier same-day backup folder), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `~/Downloads/product-gridv2.png` (source file preserved in Downloads — user's to clean up if desired), `styles.css` (no CSS change made despite the aspect mismatch — flagged as a follow-up), all other HTML files.

## 2026-04-24 — Mobile intro animation redesign (vertical composition + reduced-motion retrofit)

**What I did:** Replaced the 1.6s name-only fade on viewports <1000px with a full vertical mobile intro that mirrors the desktop narrative (name → icons fade in → cursor bezier-arcs from bottom-right to Figma → click + sparks → circle wipe). Added a new Storybook icon SVG in [index.html:99-108](index.html#L99-L108). Added `iconFadeIn` / `iconFadeInCursor` keyframes at [styles.css:214-221](styles.css#L214-L221) (replace-in-place fade without the desktop orbit/float). Rewrote the `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block at [styles.css:317-331](styles.css#L317-L331) to hide 5 desktop-only icons (pen, layers, droplet, grid, type), position the 4 mobile icons at fixed-pixel offsets (Figma `0, -180`; Code `0, +120`; Storybook `0, +200`; Cursor `150, +280`), and set staggered animation-delays (Storybook 800ms → Code 900ms → Figma 1000ms → Cursor 1200ms). Added a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block at [styles.css:334-340](styles.css#L334-L340) that hides the intro layers entirely. In [main.js](main.js): hoisted `parseVwVh` to module scope (previously nested inside `playIntroAnimation`), added a new `animateMouseMobile` helper with fixed control points `(80, 150)` and `(-20, -80)` per spec, added a reduced-motion short-circuit at the top of `shouldPlayIntro`, added the `introPlayedAt` localStorage setter inside `skipIntroAnimation` so reduced-motion users don't re-trigger, and replaced `playNameOnlyIntro` with `playMobileIntro` — the new function reads Figma and cursor offsets from CSS `--ix/--iy`, runs a 700ms bezier glide at T+1300-2000ms, reuses the desktop click/spark/flash/wipe choreography, and cleans up at T+2600ms.

**What worked:**
- **CSS-driven icon positions mirroring the desktop pattern.** `playMobileIntro` reads `--ix/--iy` from `getComputedStyle(figmaEl)` and `getComputedStyle(cursorEl)` via the hoisted `parseVwVh` helper. Result: future tuning of mobile icon positions or cursor start is a CSS-only change — no JS rebuild needed. User's refinement #7 call-out was the right architectural instinct.
- **Reusing desktop keyframe/JS assets cleanly.** The existing `.intro-click-burst`, `.intro-click-flash`, and `.intro-reveal` markup required zero HTML changes — all three work on mobile with identical `clickScreenX/Y` computation (`vw/2 + figmaOffX`). Only the `display: none` gate in the old mobile media query needed removal.
- **Fixed-pixel Y offsets (per Apr 13 learning).** Honored the prior decision to use fixed pixels for icon offsets instead of vh units. Math verified across iPhone SE (375×667: Figma at y=153, Cursor-start at y=613 — 54px from bottom), iPhone 14 (390×844: Figma at y=242, Cursor-start at y=702 — 142px from bottom), and iPhone Pro Max (430×932: Figma at y=286, Cursor-start at y=746 — 186px from bottom). All comfortable margins.
- **Dedicated `animateMouseMobile` instead of parameterizing `animateMouseToFigma`.** The desktop function's computed control points (`cp1y = Math.max(sy, ey) + 30`) are tuned for a horizontal arc; hardcoding fixed CPs per spec is cleaner than adding a CP-override parameter. Two functions, each simple, each tested.
- **`animation: ... both` on the mobile icon rules.** The `both` fill-mode keeps icons invisible before `animation-delay` fires (fill-mode `backwards`) and at the opacity:0.55 end state (fill-mode `forwards`). Without `both`, the icons would flash visible at T+0 until their delay elapsed.
- **Defensive null-check with `skipIntroAnimation` fallback in `playMobileIntro`.** If any of the 5 required DOM nodes are missing, the function calls `skipIntroAnimation()` rather than silently early-returning like the desktop path does. Prevents the "stuck on intro splash" failure mode.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Timing spec vs existing CSS char-reveal stagger mismatched by ~110ms.** Spec said "500-800ms: GOTLIEB fills in," but the existing CSS has GOTLIEB's last char finishing at ~910ms (start 510ms + 400ms duration). Decided (with user approval) to accept the 110ms overlap between the end of GOTLIEB's reveal and the start of Storybook's fade-in at 800ms rather than re-tune the CSS stagger. Visually the overlap is benign — GOTLIEB is far from the icons.
- **Storybook icon inherits `.intro-icon` base `opacity: 0` on desktop (>1000px).** No desktop animation rule references `.icon-storybook`, so at desktop sizes it sits invisible at viewport center with no visual effect (pointer-events:none on parent prevents hit-test interference). Intentional — keeps the desktop 8-icon orbit untouched — but worth documenting so a future maintainer doesn't "fix" it by adding a desktop position.
- **`parseVwVh` was nested inside `playIntroAnimation` before this session.** Hoisting it to module scope and removing the inner duplicate was a small cleanup opportunity that surfaced while adding the mobile path. Didn't plan for this in the diagnostic; caught it mid-edit.
- **Breakpoint mismatch at exactly 1000px is real but out of scope.** The JS branch uses `window.innerWidth < 1000` (desktop path at 1000px), while the CSS media query is `max-width: 1000px` (mobile CSS at 1000px). At viewport width exactly 1000, desktop JS runs but mobile CSS hides the icons — the animation would play with nothing visible. Deferred to a follow-up per user direction. Fix will be one-line: change CSS to `max-width: 999px` or change JS to `<= 1000`.

**What I learned:**
- **When a mobile intro needs to mirror a desktop narrative, don't parallel-build a second animation system — reuse the desktop's click burst / flash / circle wipe markup and JS helpers, and ONLY customize: (a) the icon composition (positions + which icons appear), (b) the cursor path (control points), (c) the timing cascade.** The existing `clickScreenX/Y = vw/2 + offX` formula is the universal coordinate converter between CSS custom properties and viewport-absolute pixels — works identically on desktop and mobile.
- **`animation: ... both` fill-mode is required any time a CSS animation has a non-zero `animation-delay`.** Without it, the element flashes its un-animated state (usually `opacity: 0` here — so it flashes invisible, which is fine) until the delay elapses, then snaps to the first keyframe. With `both`, the first keyframe applies retroactively from T+0. The mobile intro's staggered icon cascade depends on this.
- **Reduced-motion handling belongs in `shouldPlayIntro`, not inside each play-* function.** Single early-return at the decision point means both desktop and mobile paths benefit with zero per-path branching. Companion: `skipIntroAnimation` must set the localStorage `introPlayedAt` flag — otherwise reduced-motion users re-enter the (skipped) intro on every visit and the flag never gets set.
- **For fixed-pixel center-relative bezier control points, keep them as literals in the animator function, not as constructor args.** The mobile cursor path uses `(80, 150)` and `(-20, -80)` — readable, tunable in one place. If a future second mobile path needs a different arc, fork the function rather than parameterize. Two simple functions > one configurable function (per Apr 13 "don't parameterize cleverly" learning).
- **Pending follow-up — 1000px breakpoint boundary bug:** JS uses `< 1000`, CSS uses `max-width: 1000px`. Exactly-1000px viewport runs desktop JS against mobile CSS. Noted here for a future surgical fix. Low-urgency (exact 1000px viewports are rare) but should be resolved before launch.
- **Project convention established: mobile intro icon Y-offsets live in CSS `@media (max-width: 1000px)` as fixed pixels.** Do NOT attempt responsive vh-based values here (Apr 13 learning stands). If short-viewport clipping surfaces during testing, the remedy is a nested `@media (max-height: 600px)` override with compressed values, not a units refactor.

**Files touched:** `index.html` (added Storybook SVG at line 99-108), `styles.css` (added iconFadeIn keyframes at 214-221, rewrote mobile media block at 317-331, added reduced-motion block at 333-340), `main.js` (hoisted parseVwVh to module scope, added animateMouseMobile helper, updated shouldPlayIntro + skipIntroAnimation, replaced playNameOnlyIntro with playMobileIntro), `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-animation-2026-04-24/` (pre-change backup of all three files), `.claude/learnings.md`. Explicitly untouched: `about.html`, `game.js`, all 4 case study pages, the desktop intro flow in `playIntroAnimation`, the 8 original desktop icon `--ix/--iy` positions, the `charReveal` keyframe and its stagger rules, all image assets.

## 2026-04-24 — Mobile intro V2 (Figma-emerges-from-name, compressed timeline)

> **Supersedes the V1 entry above ("Mobile intro animation redesign")** — the V1 three-icon vertical stack (Storybook/Code/Figma + Cursor) shipped working but Rotem's read on it after live testing was "feels empty, three tiny icons aren't earning the screen real estate." V2 collapses the composition down to a single Figma icon that springs out of the name itself, pushes the timeline from ~2600ms to ~2900ms to give the single gesture more weight, and absorbs the V1 hover-settle beat into a longer glide so the click lands decisively without a visible pause.

**What I did:** Compressed the mobile intro composition from 3 visible icons → 1 (Figma only; Code + Storybook SVGs kept in markup but hidden via CSS so the HTML stays portable to a future desktop-side V3). Added three new CSS keyframes: `figmaEmergeFromName` (spring from name origin to resting slot), `burst-expand-mobile` (3.0x terminal scale vs desktop 2.5x), `click-flash-mobile` (1.8x terminal scale vs desktop 1.5x). Rewrote the `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block to hide 7 icons total (was 5), drive Figma via the new spring keyframe at T+800ms (300ms duration), shorten the cursor fade from 350ms → 200ms, and add mobile-specific overrides for `.intro-click-burst.active` / `.intro-click-flash.active` / `.intro-reveal.active`. In `main.js`, touched only `playMobileIntro` (desktop `playIntroAnimation` intentionally untouched): outer setTimeout 1300→1400ms, `animateMouseMobile` duration 700→850ms, removed the 100ms hover-settle setTimeout that used to wrap the click block (Option C — glide absorbs settle), deepened cursor press scale 0.75→0.6, grew Figma pop scale 1.4→1.5, and tightened wipe cleanup setTimeout 400→300ms. Backup at `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-v2-2026-04-24/` (main.js + styles.css + learnings.md pre-change snapshots).

**What worked:**
- **`figmaEmergeFromName` spring pattern** — `from { translate(0,0) scale(0.5) opacity 0 }` → `to { translate(var(--ix), var(--iy)) scale(1) opacity 0.55 }` with `var(--ease-out-expo)` produces a "punches out of the name" feel without any extra wrapper element or JS orchestration. The key insight: the resting transform is already declared on `.icon-figma` via `--ix/--iy`, so the `to` state matches the declarative position — the keyframe just animates into it from (0, 0). No coordinate duplication, no JS needed. Pattern is reusable for any future icon that should "emerge from" a DOM origin rather than fade in place.
- **Mobile-only click keyframes via class-specific cascade overrides (Option A)** — rather than forking `.intro-click-burst` / `.intro-click-flash` into `.intro-click-burst-mobile` duplicate classes, the `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block overrides the `.active` rule's `animation:` shorthand to point at the mobile-suffixed keyframes. Same markup, same JS (unchanged `classList.add('active')` calls), different keyframes picked up purely via CSS cascade specificity. Pays off when the JS/markup layer stays clean — zero V2-specific branches in `playMobileIntro` for the burst/flash/wipe choreography, only the timing/scale values changed.
- **Option C for the settle beat** (extend glide 700→850ms, drop explicit settle) **over Option A (50ms explicit pause)** — the V1 hover-settle beat was visually "the cursor stops, then clicks" which read as hesitation on a 2s timeline. Stretching the glide absorbs the pause into deceleration, so the click lands on the glide's natural terminal frame. Net: one fewer setTimeout in the JS, one fewer "is this on purpose?" moment in the animation. Kept the Option C pattern over an alternative Option B (explicit 100ms pause with a "breathing" visual cue) because the 100ms breathing pause was already deliberately preserved inside the click block (the 100ms before intro-inner fade) — giving the timeline one intentional beat, not two.
- **Keyframe naming discipline: `-mobile` suffix for keyframes overridden via media query, no suffix for the canonical desktop version.** Makes the cascade-override pattern self-documenting — anyone scanning `styles.css` sees `burst-expand` and `burst-expand-mobile` side-by-side and immediately understands the mobile version is a variant, not a replacement.
- **Final V2 timeline (2900ms to homepage fully resolved):** T+0 name chars reveal → T+800 Figma emerges from name (300ms) → T+1200 cursor fade-in (200ms) → T+1400 cursor hand-off to rAF → T+1400-2250 glide (850ms) → T+2250 click fires immediately (no settle) → T+2350 intro fade → T+2400 wipe starts (300ms) → T+2700 cleanup → T+3000 reveal fade-out clears. Matches the "single decisive gesture" feel Rotem wanted.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The Storybook SVG kept-in-markup-but-hidden decision** was a judgment call that initially read as dead code. Resolved by keeping it explicitly (per Rotem's call) for two reasons: (a) re-introducing it later doesn't require a DOM edit, just a CSS change, and (b) if a future desktop V3 wants Storybook as an 8th icon on desktop, the markup is already portable. The `.icon-code, .icon-storybook { display: none !important; }` line in the mobile media block is the single point of truth for "which icons are mobile-hidden" — the 2-line comment above it is doing real load-bearing work, don't strip it.
- **The V1 entry had `.icon-code` and `.icon-storybook` positioned inside the mobile media block with their own `--ix/--iy` declarations.** Those had to be *entirely removed*, not just overridden with `display: none` — otherwise the CSS would still compute their positions before hiding them (harmless at runtime, but noisy in DevTools inspector and confusing to future readers). Removed cleanly; the mobile media block now only positions icons that are actually visible on mobile.

**What I learned:**
- **When a motion spec offers multiple tunings for the same beat (V1's "hover settle" → Options A/B/C in the V2 diagnostic), pick the one that removes a line of code over the one that adds a keyframe.** Option C (glide absorbs settle) removed the wrapping setTimeout; Option A (50ms explicit pause) would have added a keyframe or another setTimeout. Smaller diff, fewer places for timing drift to hide.
- **`animation:` shorthand in a media query override fully replaces the base `animation:` declaration — including duration, timing function, delay, and fill-mode.** So when I wrote `.icon-cursor { animation: iconFadeInCursor 200ms ease-out 1200ms both; }` inside the mobile media block, that completely overrode the desktop `iconBurstAndFloatCursor 3.6s ease-out 0.60s infinite` from the outer scope. This is the "one property at a time" failure mode of the animation shorthand — if I'd only wanted to change the duration, I would have needed `animation-duration: 200ms` specifically. For full-behavior swaps like mobile vs desktop, the shorthand is actually cleaner. Rule: **when mobile and desktop need materially different animations on the same selector, use the `animation:` shorthand in the media query override — do NOT try to override `animation-duration` / `animation-delay` piecewise**, you'll end up with one of them keeping a desktop value and silently breaking the cascade.
- **The V1 → V2 iteration pattern validates the "build-then-live-test-then-compress" cycle.** V1 shipped in a single session with three icons because that was the desktop-parallel design. Only after live testing on a real mobile viewport did the "empty feel" surface — no amount of static review would have caught it. For motion design specifically, always ship a V1 to a real device, collect a same-session reaction, then compress in V2. Don't try to hit the final composition on the first pass.
- **Keep the V1 entry in `learnings.md` alongside the V2 entry** (don't edit V1 to say "superseded") — future readers need to see both the original design choice AND the reason it was revised. The `> **Supersedes the V1 entry above**` blockquote at the top of this V2 entry threads the history without rewriting it. Pattern to repeat on any future V1→V2 motion iteration.

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (added 3 keyframes `figmaEmergeFromName` / `burst-expand-mobile` / `click-flash-mobile` after `iconFadeInCursor`, rewrote `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block — hid `.icon-code` + `.icon-storybook`, removed their position rules, swapped `.icon-figma` animation to `figmaEmergeFromName 300ms var(--ease-out-expo) 800ms both`, shortened `.icon-cursor` duration 350→200ms, added 3 mobile click/flash/wipe overrides), `main.js` (rewrote `playMobileIntro` body only — outer setTimeout 1300→1400ms, glide duration 700→850ms, removed 100ms hover-settle wrapper, cursor press scale 0.75→0.6, Figma pop scale 1.4→1.5, wipe cleanup 400→300ms, updated timeline comment block to V2 ~2900ms), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry), `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-v2-2026-04-24/` (pre-change backup of `main.js` + `styles.css` + `learnings.md`). Explicitly untouched: `index.html` (Storybook SVG stays in markup per Rotem's call), `about.html`, `game.js`, all 4 case study pages, `playIntroAnimation` (desktop path), `animateMouseMobile` / `animateMouseToFigma` / `parseVwVh` / `cubicBezier` / `easeInOutCubic` helpers, the 8 desktop icon `--ix/--iy` positions, `iconFadeIn` / `iconFadeInCursor` keyframes (still used elsewhere if referenced — though note V2 doesn't reference `iconFadeIn` on mobile anymore; left in place for now, flag as potentially-orphan for a future cleanup sweep), `charReveal` keyframe, reduced-motion media block, all image assets.

## 2026-04-24 — Mobile intro revert to pre-tonight state (V1 + V2 rolled back)

> **Supersedes BOTH the V1 entry ("Mobile intro animation redesign") and the V2 entry ("Mobile intro V2") above.** After shipping V1 and tightening it into V2 within the same night, Rotem's read was "V1 felt empty with 4 icons; V2's Figma-springs-from-name direction is strong but wasn't the right thing to execute inside tonight's focus window." Reverting both iterations cleanly to the pre-tonight state preserves shipping cadence on the more impactful non-animation tonight work (Ghost video wiring intent, About page hover/video features, GoteFigure image pipeline, Currently Building card reorder) and defers mobile animation to a future dedicated session.

**What I did:** Reverted `main.js`, `styles.css`, and `index.html` to their 02:28-captured pre-V1 snapshots at `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-animation-2026-04-24/`. Procedure: (1) safety-net backup of the post-V2 current state → `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-pre-revert-2026-04-24/`, (2) three `cp` copies from the pre-V1 backup onto live files, (3) four-point verification: `diff -q` against backup (all three byte-identical), hover rules still present at `styles.css:1017` / `1226`, `.building-card-image video` rule still present at `styles.css:1188`, and `about.html` / `game.js` / `work/*.html` mtimes confirm zero collateral damage. Net code effect: removed 5 keyframes (V1: `iconFadeIn` + `iconFadeInCursor`; V2: `figmaEmergeFromName` + `burst-expand-mobile` + `click-flash-mobile`), removed the V1 Storybook SVG block in `index.html` (11 lines), removed the V1 `animateMouseMobile` helper + `parseVwVh` hoist + reduced-motion handling in `shouldPlayIntro`/`skipIntroAnimation` + the `playMobileIntro` function entirely, and restored `playNameOnlyIntro` (the simple 1600ms name-fade). Mobile intro on <1000px viewports is now back to: name reveals over 550ms, holds, fades out at 1200ms, homepage visible by 1600ms. No icons. No cursor glide. No wipe.

**What worked:**
- **Straight three-file `cp` over surgical merge.** The conflict check confirmed zero non-animation tonight work post-02:28 on `main.js` / `styles.css` / `index.html` (all earlier tonight changes — hover features, video wiring, GoteFigure fixes — either pre-dated the backup or lived in different files: `about.html`, `work/*.html`, `img/`). So the revert was fully bounded — no manual line-level merge needed. **Rule: when reverting to a time-stamped backup, verify the backup's timestamp against the session log before choosing between straight-cp and surgical-merge. A 2-minute diff is cheaper than a 20-minute merge.**
- **Dual backup preservation pattern:** kept BOTH the pre-V1 state (`_backup-mobile-intro-animation-2026-04-24/` — the revert *source*) AND the post-V2 state (`_backup-mobile-intro-pre-revert-2026-04-24/` — the revert *target* at the moment of reversion). If future-Rotem decides V1 or V2 was actually the right direction, the post-V2 backup is a one-`cp` re-entry point; if V1 is wanted, it's recoverable from the V2 entry's diff in `learnings.md` applied back onto the current file. **Always double-backup before a revert — the cost is ~50 KB of disk, the upside is recoverability.**
- **Four-point post-revert verification** (byte-identical diffs + hover rule preservation + video rule preservation + external-file mtime check) caught that the hover-rule line number drifted from the V2 state's 1227 to the pre-V1 state's 1226. That 1-line drift is a signal of the keyframe block size change, not a bug — the same rule is there, just at a slightly different line number because V2's 5 extra lines of keyframes were removed. **Line-number assertions in a revert are noise; assertion on "is this rule present" is signal.** Adjust verification checklists accordingly in future.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The V1 + V2 learning entries are now both "documentation archaeology"** — they describe code that no longer exists in the live tree. Rather than deleting those entries (which would destroy the design rationale record), the supersede blockquote at the top of this entry threads the history: V1 → V2 → reverted. This is the third supersede-chain pattern this month (cf. Apr 13 "Revert broken intro + fixed-pixel conversion" + Apr 13 "Rebuild icon orbit positioning from scratch"). **Convention: each iteration keeps its own entry, each subsequent entry threads via blockquote-at-top. Never edit a prior entry — the timeline IS the documentation.**
- **The V2 keyframes `iconFadeIn` / `iconFadeInCursor` / `figmaEmergeFromName` / `burst-expand-mobile` / `click-flash-mobile` are all gone** — the "flag as potentially-orphan" note from the V2 entry resolved itself via the revert. If re-entering the V1 or V2 direction, those keyframes need to come back too; the backup folders hold them verbatim.

**What I learned:**
- **Shipping cadence > motion polish when they conflict in the same session.** The V2 entry documented a genuinely nice motion idea (Figma springs from name), but the right call was to revert and come back to it with a fresh focus window. Animation iteration has a cost curve that rewards dedicated sessions (build → test → live-observe → compress → ship) and punishes context-switched tweaking (build → ship other stuff → tweak → ship other stuff → tweak). **Rule: treat motion design sessions as atomic. If a motion iteration can't complete in one session, prefer revert over carry-over — stale half-built motion in the tree is worse than a clean baseline.**
- **A pre-V1 backup folder taken the day of the redesign pays off twice in one night.** First use: as a diff reference during V1 review. Second use: as the revert source after V2 was executed but deemed wrong-window. **Rule: always backup before a motion rewrite, and always keep that backup for at least 72h past the rewrite's ship — the "I thought we were done but now we're reverting" moment has a long tail.**
- **Future re-entry points for mobile animation work are now both preserved and traceable:**
  - V1 re-entry: `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-pre-revert-2026-04-24/` contains the post-V2 state. Diff it against current to reconstruct V1 (subtract V2's tweaks per V2 entry's specifics).
  - V2 re-entry: same folder. A direct `cp` restores V2 exactly.
  - Fresh direction: read V1 + V2 + this entry before starting, to inherit the "V1 felt empty, V2 was the right direction but wrong window" context.

**Files touched:** `main.js` (full revert to pre-V1 backup, byte-identical), `styles.css` (full revert to pre-V1 backup, byte-identical — all hover/video rules from earlier tonight preserved because they pre-dated 02:28 snapshot), `index.html` (full revert to pre-V1 backup, byte-identical — Storybook SVG block removed), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Backup folders: `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-pre-revert-2026-04-24/` (new — safety net for re-entry, holds post-V2 state as of 04:24), `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-animation-2026-04-24/` (unchanged — was the revert source, holds pre-V1 state as of 02:28), `_archive/_backup-mobile-intro-v2-2026-04-24/` (unchanged — holds pre-V2 / post-V1 state as of 03:25). Explicitly untouched: `about.html` (mtime 01:43 — all hover link conversions and card reorder preserved), `game.js` (mtime Mar 10 — untouched in weeks), all 4 `work/*.html` case study pages (mtimes Apr 21-24, reflecting the GoteFigure image pipeline work that's kept), all image assets in `img/` and `images/`, `CLAUDE.md`. Mobile animation work is explicitly deferred to a future focused session.

## 2026-04-24 — Ghost videos wired: homepage bento + case study first image (dark mode dual-video pass)

**What I did:** Processed two Ghost dark-mode source `.mov` files from Downloads into web-ready H.264 `.mp4` + JPG poster pairs, wired both into the portfolio. (A) `thumbnailloopvid_ghost.mov` (4.08s, 2766×1646, 1.13 MB) → `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` at 960×540, CRF 28, preset medium, stripped audio, faststart → 55 KB (target <1 MB, 94% under). Extracted 38 KB poster at 2s. (B) `ghostvidloop_img1V2.mov` (16.20s, 2866×1682, 9.05 MB) → `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` at 1280×720, CRF 24, preset medium, stripped audio, faststart → 447 KB (target <2.5 MB, 82% under). Extracted 64 KB poster at 2s. (C) Wired thumbnail into `index.html:199` Ghost bento card (`.bento-card-d .bento-card-thumb`) — swapped `<img src="img/ghost/thumbnail.png">` for canonical 7-attribute `<video>` element. (D) Wired demo video into `work/ghost.html:113` first `.case-image.reveal` block (the image immediately following hero + title + metadata + Overview + The Problem sections, previously `process-01.png`) — swapped `<img>` for `<video>` with `../img/` prefix path. (E) Added two new CSS rules mirroring existing `img` rules: `.bento-card-thumb video` at `styles.css:578` (object-position: center, matches the img rule) and `.case-image video` at `styles.css:959` (object-position: center, diverging from the sibling img rule's `center top` — rationale: video composition typically centers the subject, while static screenshots often benefit from top-anchored positioning). (F) Preserved originals as `-old.png` duplicates in live `img/ghost/` folder (`thumbnail-static-old.png`, `process-01-old.png`) alongside the existing archive snapshot at `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/`.

**What worked:**
- **Both sources were already landscape, not portrait — no rotation needed, only a height-crop to true 16:9.** Sources were 2766×1646 (aspect 1.68) and 2866×1682 (aspect 1.70), both slightly taller than 16:9 (1.778). The ffmpeg filter chain was `crop=iw:iw*9/16,scale=W:H` for both — crop height to exactly `width × 9/16` (center-cropping ~90px and ~70px off vertical respectively), then scale to target. This diverges from the April 24 (earlier tonight) About-page video pipeline where sources were 720×1280 portrait and needed a width-crop (`crop=ih*16/9:ih`) to landscape. **Rule: always ffprobe source aspect first; the crop filter direction depends on whether source is wider or taller than 16:9.**
- **Dramatically better compression than the About-page pipeline on comparable settings.** Thumbnail 4s at CRF 28 came out 55 KB vs the About videos' 920 KB at same CRF / same resolution / same duration — because the Ghost thumbnail source has low temporal entropy (static UI mockup with a small animated region), while the About videos had full-frame motion (phone footage, apparel garments). H.264 P-frame coding is inter-frame differential — if 90% of pixels don't change between frames, the stream is tiny. **Rule: don't predict output size from CRF alone; temporal entropy (how much changes per frame) dominates for short UI clips. Check the source's nature before estimating.**
- **CRF 24 for the in-case-study demo vs CRF 28 for the bento thumbnail was the right call.** The bento is a 2-inch-wide card in peripheral vision on the homepage — CRF 28 banding is imperceptible. The case study video is the reader's primary focus inside a 1040px content column at 1280×720 — CRF 24 preserves typography crispness and screen-recording detail. Even with the stricter CRF, the longer (16s vs 4s) clip came in at 447 KB because it's UI footage. **Rule: case study / primary-focus media = CRF 24. Bento / peripheral-vision media = CRF 28. Short UI loops = either works fine because entropy is low.**
- **Canonical 7-attribute `<video>` pattern unchanged from the April 24 About-page precedent** — `src poster autoplay muted loop playsinline preload="metadata" aria-label`. All four autoplay-enabling attributes (`autoplay muted loop playsinline`) are load-bearing per prior learnings; dropping any breaks iOS Safari. Third deployment of this pattern in the codebase now (About GoteFigure, About Rotems Adventures, Ghost homepage, Ghost case study = 4 live instances). This is officially the site's canonical video element.
- **Parent container inherits sizing to the new `<video>` child without any changes to the container rule.** `.bento-card-thumb` has `aspect-ratio: 16/9; overflow: hidden; width: 100%` — video fills it identically to the prior img. Same for `.case-image` which has `aspect-ratio: 16/10; overflow: hidden`. **Rule: when replacing `<img>` with `<video>` inside a sized parent, the parent's sizing contract applies unchanged. The only CSS addition needed is a child-element rule (`.parent video {...}`) mirroring the img rule.**
- **Backup-before-modify workflow via `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/`** captured `index.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `styles.css`, and a full `img/ghost/` tree snapshot before any edits. Plus `-old.png` sibling copies in the live folder for fast rollback without reaching into the archive. Two-layer preservation: archive (long-term) + live-folder-sibling (fast rollback).
- **Approval gate between STEPS 1-3 (asset processing) and STEPS 4-5 (HTML wiring) caught a CSS object-position refinement before any file was touched.** Rotem's adjustment on `.case-image video { object-position: center }` vs the proposed `center top` (which was copied from the sibling img rule) would have been a latent bug — centered video subjects would've been top-anchored and cropped wrongly. **Rule reinforced from prior session: always surface the full proposed diff for HTML/CSS changes before applying, even when the pattern seems "obviously parallel" to an existing one. Video composition differs from screenshot composition in ways that matter at the object-fit boundary.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The thumbnail compressed to 55 KB, not a typo.** First instinct was to double-check the file plays correctly because 55 KB for a 4-second H.264 video at 960×540 is unusual. Verified via ffprobe (correct dimensions, correct duration 3.69s — slight 0.4s trim from ffmpeg frame-rate normalization at the 120fps → variable-fps boundary, acceptable for a seamless loop). The low size is legitimate and reflects source entropy, not a broken encode.
- **Thumbnail duration dropped from source 4.085s to output 3.69s** — ffmpeg collapsed the variable frame rate (source `avg_frame_rate=1725/34 ≈ 50.7 fps` declared as `r_frame_rate=120/1`) down to a stable output stream, discarding the tail frames that couldn't form complete groups. Not a problem for a seamless loop but worth recording: **ffmpeg may trim up to ~0.4s off a 120fps-declared / ~50fps-actual source when transcoding to a stable output stream. If exact duration matters, add `-r` flag to force a specific output frame rate.** Not added here because loop-seamlessness outweighs exact-duration matching for a background thumbnail.
- **Existing `.case-image img` rule at [styles.css:943-949](styles.css#L943-L949) uses `object-position: center top` — but video should use `center` per Rotem's call.** The two rules are now asymmetric by design. Future readers may assume this is a mistake and "fix" it to match. Worth a comment? Decided against because (a) no comments rule in the house style, (b) the asymmetry is self-documenting once you understand the rationale (video subjects center, screenshot text anchors top). Recording here instead.
- **`.bento-card-thumb video` intentionally omits the `transition: transform 600ms var(--ease-smooth)` property that the sibling img rule at [styles.css:570-576](styles.css#L570-L576) has.** That transition only matters for the hover-zoom effect used on static img children; autoplaying videos don't benefit from a transform transition because they're constantly repainting anyway, and adding the transition would cause compositor churn on every frame. **Rule: when mirroring an img rule for a video child, drop any `transition` that targets transform — it's ornamental for static media and wasteful for moving media.**

**What I learned:**
- **Two-video batch wiring is a single coherent unit — process both, report both, wire both, verify both.** Keeping thumbnail (homepage) and demo (case study) in one pass meant one archive folder, one approval gate, one learnings entry. Splitting across sessions would've doubled the bookkeeping for no benefit. **Rule: if two or more assets land in the same visual section (or same project), batch them into one session with explicit per-asset reporting at each step.**
- **Source `.mov` aspect determines crop direction — always ffprobe first.** Today's Ghost sources: landscape-but-taller-than-16:9, so crop height. Tonight's earlier About sources: portrait 720×1280, so crop width. Yesterday's hypothetical (none yet) 16:9-exact source: no crop, just scale. Three distinct filter chains from three distinct source aspects — choosing the wrong one center-crops the subject out of frame.
- **CRF 24 vs CRF 28 choice is about viewer proximity, not about quality floor.** Bento cards are glanced at from peripheral vision in 16:9 blocks sized ~400×225. Case study content is read at arm's length in 1280×720 blocks. The eye's ability to detect banding scales with angular pixel density, so the closer / bigger the video appears, the stricter the CRF needs to be. Document this as the site's compression policy.
- **Video composition defaults to `object-position: center` for this codebase.** Rotem's CSS adjustment clarifies the convention: `.bento-card-thumb video` and `.case-image video` both use `center`. Future video CSS rules should follow this default unless there's a specific subject-anchoring need (and in that case, prefer a per-element override via inline style or a modifier class — don't change the base rule).
- **The hover-inventory count ticks up: 4 video elements live on the site now.** About Currently Building (2 — GoteFigure + Rotems Adventures) + Ghost homepage bento + Ghost case study first image. With 4 instances, the `<video>` element pattern is officially canonical for this codebase. When a fifth lands (probably Pulse or LexisNexis case study), no design discussion needed — just apply the established pattern.
- **Mobile responsive concern to verify post-deploy:** the Ghost bento card at `.bento-card-d` has a mobile aspect-ratio override at [styles.css:1325](styles.css#L1325) (`aspect-ratio: 4/3`) that's different from the desktop 16/9. The 960×540 source is 16/9, so on mobile the video will be cropped horizontally to fill a 4/3 container. Center-positioned cover-fit should handle this gracefully — the subject stays centered — but worth eyeballing at 375px to confirm nothing important gets cut off the sides. Similar check on `.case-image` (aspect-ratio 16/10 desktop, no mobile override found, 6px border-radius on mobile).

**Files touched:** `index.html` (1 line swap at line 199 — `<img>` Ghost bento thumbnail → 10-line `<video>`), `work/ghost.html` (1 line swap at line 113 — `<img>` first case-image → 10-line `<video>` with `../img/` prefix), `styles.css` (2 new rules inserted — `.bento-card-thumb video` at line 578 after the img sibling rule, `.case-image video` at line 959 after the img sibling rule, 6 lines each), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` (new, 55 KB H.264 from `~/Downloads/thumbnailloopvid_ghost.mov` via `crop=iw:iw*9/16,scale=960:540`, CRF 28, preset medium, `-an`, `+faststart`), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` (new, 447 KB H.264 from `~/Downloads/ghostvidloop_img1V2.mov` via `crop=iw:iw*9/16,scale=1280:720`, CRF 24, preset medium, `-an`, `+faststart`), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail-poster.jpg` (new, 38 KB, ffmpeg frame at 2s, -q:v 3), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo-poster.jpg` (new, 64 KB, same extraction params), `img/ghost/thumbnail-static-old.png` (new, `cp` of `thumbnail.png` for fast rollback), `img/ghost/process-01-old.png` (new, `cp` of `process-01.png` for fast rollback), `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/` (backup: `index.html`, `ghost.html`, `styles.css`, `ghost-img-snapshot/` full tree), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: Pulse / LexisNexis / GoteFigure bento cards in `index.html`, all of `about.html` (including its video elements and Currently Building cards), `work/pulse.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`, all other case-study images (`process-02.png` through `process-05.png`, `hero.png`), `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Ghost case study content below the first image (starting at "The Insight" section) is unchanged.

## 2026-04-24 — Ghost demo video aspect-ratio fix: 16:9 container override via modifier class

**What I did:** Surgical fix for the cropping issue on the Ghost case study first demo video. The video is 1280×720 (16:9) but the default `.case-image` container uses `aspect-ratio: 16/10`, so `object-fit: cover` was cropping the top and letterbox-filling the horizontal edges. Added a BEM-style modifier class `.case-image--video-wide` to the single affected div at [work/ghost.html:112](work/ghost.html#L112) and a new CSS rule `.case-image.case-image--video-wide { aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; }` at [styles.css:951-953](styles.css#L951-L953) immediately after the base `.case-image` block. The rule uses the double-class selector (`.case-image.case-image--video-wide`) rather than the single-class (`.case-image--video-wide`) to guarantee specificity beats the base rule in all cascade orderings — the double-class has specificity (0,2,0) vs the base's (0,1,0), so the override wins deterministically regardless of source order.

**What worked:**
- **BEM-style modifier class pattern** — `.case-image` (base) + `.case-image--video-wide` (modifier, double-hyphen separator). This is a surgical escape hatch: the base rule applies to every `.case-image` on the site (5 in ghost, 4 in gotefigure, 7 in lexisnexis, 5 in pulse — 21 total across the four case studies), and the modifier opts a single one of them into the 16:9 override without touching the base. No other element on the site accidentally inherits the change — verified via `grep -rn "case-image--video-wide"` which returned exactly 2 hits: the CSS rule definition and the one HTML usage. **Rule: when a single instance of a widely-used class needs a different property value, add a BEM modifier class rather than overriding via `:nth-child()`, element-of-parent selectors, or element ID. Modifier classes are self-documenting and survive DOM reordering.**
- **Pre-change grep sweep ruled out cascade collisions.** Before adding the rule, I listed all `.case-image` selectors (`grep -n "\.case-image"`): (1) base at line 940, (2) img child rule at 951, (3) video child rule at 959, (4) `.case-layout--visual .case-image { aspect-ratio: 3/2 }` at 993, (5) tablet breakpoint at 1300 (only `width` + `margin-left`), (6) mobile breakpoint at 1354 (only `width`, `margin-left`, `border-radius`). The `.case-layout--visual` override would have been a collision — it sets aspect-ratio to 3/2 — but `ghost.html` uses `.case-layout--airy` (verified via `grep -n "case-layout" work/ghost.html`), so that rule doesn't apply here. Mobile and tablet rules don't touch aspect-ratio, so the 16/9 override propagates cleanly to all viewports. **Rule: before adding a CSS override, grep every rule that matches the same base selector. Map out which ones could collide with your new property — especially media queries and layout-modifier prefixes — before writing the new rule.**
- **Double-class specificity (`.case-image.case-image--video-wide`) over single-class (`.case-image--video-wide`).** The single-class form would work here because our new rule is written after the base rule (source order tiebreaker), but the double-class form survives cascade reshuffles, CSS splits, and future media-query inserts. Specificity (0,2,0) > (0,1,0) is an invariant; source order is not. **Rule: when writing a modifier-class override, use the double-class selector form (`.base.base--modifier`) to make the override specificity-safe against future cascade changes.**
- **The fix is complementary, not competing, with the earlier-this-session `.case-image video { object-fit: cover }` rule.** Cover-fit still applies — but because the container aspect-ratio now matches the video aspect-ratio exactly (both 16:9), there's nothing to crop. Cover-fit is a no-op when source and container aspects agree. Kept the `object-fit: cover` rule for robustness against future sources that don't match perfectly.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **I should have caught the aspect mismatch at transcode time, not after wiring.** The container was 16:10 and I encoded the video to 16:9 — the mismatch was encoded into the asset pipeline. Could've been prevented by: (a) reading the existing `.case-image { aspect-ratio: 16/10 }` rule before choosing encode dimensions and matching the encode to it (1280×800 instead of 1280×720), or (b) recognizing that case-study video containers should use 16:9 by default since that's the dominant video aspect. Option (b) is the better fix long-term — the site's default for `.case-image` was chosen when only static screenshots populated it (which are often more square-ish UI crops); video is different. **Rule: when introducing a new media type (video into an image-first container), audit the container's default geometry against the media's dominant geometry. If they disagree, either retune the encode, add a modifier, or reconsider the default.**
- **`object-fit: cover` was silently filling the gap, which is why the bug manifested as "top/left/right cropping" rather than letterboxing.** A user who sees a cropped video may not immediately realize it's the container's fault — they may think the source was shot wrong. The visual signature of `aspect-ratio` mismatch + `cover` is: "the subject looks framed wrong but the edges look crisp" (vs `contain` which gives obvious letterbox bars). **Rule: if someone reports a "cropping" problem on a `<video>` or `<img>` with `object-fit: cover`, first thing to check is source aspect vs container aspect. The fix is usually container-side, not asset-side.**
- **Did not need to add a mobile breakpoint override.** My first instinct was to also add `.case-image.case-image--video-wide` inside the `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block at line 1300 and the 767px block at 1354, matching the pattern of other `.case-image` responsive rules. But since neither of those blocks touches `aspect-ratio`, the desktop `aspect-ratio: 16/9` inherits cleanly to all viewports. Added nothing to the media queries. **Rule: override only what you need to override. Don't add redundant mobile rules "for symmetry" — inheritance through unset properties is the correct CSS pattern and produces less code to maintain.**

**What I learned:**
- **Container aspect-ratio is the fix, not the source aspect-ratio.** Tempted first thought was "re-encode the video to 1280×800 to match the 16:10 container" — this would have wasted pixels and required a second encode run. The correct fix was the opposite direction: the container should match the canonical video aspect (16:9), not the other way around. Video sources are almost universally 16:9; static UI screenshots are often 16:10 or squarer. The `.case-image` container was tuned for screenshots; videos need the override.
- **Confirmed the `.case-image--video-wide` modifier is applied to exactly one element, both as a design decision and a verification step.** When the next video lands in a case study (probably Pulse or LexisNexis), it should also use this modifier. At that point the pattern becomes "any `<video>` inside `.case-image` probably wants `--video-wide`." If the modifier reaches ~3 usages across case studies, consider promoting it to the default via a more general rule: `.case-image:has(video) { aspect-ratio: 16/9 }` — CSS `:has()` is supported in all modern browsers as of 2026. But don't promote prematurely; two data points isn't a pattern.
- **Three-layer video pipeline now canonical for this site.** (1) Asset: 16:9 H.264 under `img/<section>/videos/`. (2) Markup: canonical 7-attribute `<video>` element. (3) Container: 16:9 aspect-ratio box — either the `.bento-card-thumb` default (16:9) or `.case-image.case-image--video-wide` (modifier-class override on the default-16:10 base). All three layers aligned = no cropping.
- **Modifier-class override is cheaper than any of the alternatives.** Considered: (a) `:nth-of-type()` selector on the specific child — fragile against DOM reorders, (b) element ID selector — overpowered specificity, (c) inline `style="aspect-ratio: 16/9"` on the div — breaks separation of concerns and caching, (d) per-case-study class (`.ghost .case-image:first-of-type`) — couples CSS to case-study identity, not to the semantic reason for the override. The BEM modifier documents *why* the rule exists (it's for wide video) independent of *where* it's used.

**Files touched:** `work/ghost.html` (1 line — added `case-image--video-wide` modifier class to the demo video's `.case-image` div at line 112), `styles.css` (3 lines — new `.case-image.case-image--video-wide` rule at line 951-953 immediately after the base `.case-image` block), `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/ghost-pre-aspect-fix.html` (new, pre-fix `work/ghost.html` snapshot), `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/styles-pre-aspect-fix.css` (new, pre-fix `styles.css` snapshot), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: every other `.case-image` div across all four case studies (21 total — keep the default 16:10), `.case-image img` rule (unchanged, screenshots still use the default), `.case-image video` rule (unchanged, cover-fit + center-position apply regardless of container aspect), all media-query blocks (no mobile override needed since `aspect-ratio` inherits from base), `index.html`, `about.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`. Ghost video file assets (`.mp4`, `.jpg`) are unchanged — this fix is container-side only.

## 2026-04-24 — Ghost demo v2: re-crop to remove "Scan: Payments" header strip, preserve 16:9

**What I did:** Re-encoded `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` to remove the top header strip ("Back to Overview | Scan: Payments | 2026-03-02 | 72.5%") from the frame so the video now opens directly on the purple "AI ANALYSIS" block. Before touching any asset, ran a frame-level diagnostic pass: extracted frame at t=2s, pixel-sampled the top 200 rows at x=50 and x=100 columns with Python PIL to find the Y-coordinate where the purple AI Analysis block begins, then generated a grid-annotated preview of the top 300 px to visually confirm. Result: header bar occupies Y=0-20, dark gap Y=20-40, purple block Y=40-100. User's initial estimate was 140-160 px crop, which would have destroyed the AI Analysis block entirely — real crop needed was ~32 px. Presented three mathematically clean aspect-preservation options (vs. the user's proposed options which had an arithmetic error assuming top+bottom symmetric crop preserves 16:9, which it doesn't on a 1280-wide frame). Rotem approved Option B: top crop + symmetric horizontal trim to restore 16:9 canonical aspect — `crop=1224:688:28:32` (crop window 1224 wide × 688 tall, starting at x=28 y=32). Preserved the v1 full-height file as `ghost-demo-v1-fullheight.mp4` before overwriting. Re-generated the poster frame from the new cropped video (poster dims 1224×688, 59 KB). Final asset: 1224×688 H.264, CRF 24, 430 KB (was 458 KB at 1280×720 — 6% size reduction from the 4.4% width trim). Zero CSS changes needed because the container is already 16:9 via `.case-image--video-wide`.

**What worked:**
- **Pre-execution frame diagnostic pass caught a 4× overestimate of the crop value.** The user's ask was "remove the top strip so the video starts cleanly above the purple AI ANALYSIS block" with an estimate of 140-160 px. Eyeballing the frame preview is unreliable because Claude's PNG preview renders at ~960 px wide on a 1280 px source, so proportions get visually compressed. Pixel-sampling the actual source at full resolution with PIL gave the real answer: 32 px. Executing the original estimate would have cropped out the very feature the user wanted to preserve. **Rule: when asked to crop by an estimated pixel amount, always pixel-sample the source at full resolution before accepting the estimate. Preview-image eyeballing produces 3-5× errors on typical 1280 px assets.**
- **Grid-annotated preview (`ImageDraw.line` every 20 px + `ImageDraw.text` label) was the decisive visual artifact.** Gave Rotem and me a shared reference so the conversation could point at specific Y-values rather than argue in abstractions. Generated in 5 lines of Python. **Rule: when proposing a numeric geometric parameter (crop px, border width, translation distance), generate a grid-annotated preview of the source to accompany the proposal. One image with numbered grid lines ends the conversation.**
- **Corrected the user's aspect-preservation math before executing.** Their option (c) — "crop top AND crop bottom symmetrically to preserve 16:9" — doesn't preserve 16:9 on a width-locked frame. Any pure height reduction on a 1280 px frame changes the aspect ratio from 1.778 (16:9) to something larger. To actually preserve 16:9, width has to shrink in the same proportion as height. That's why the final crop was `1224×688` not `1280×688` — the 56 px horizontal trim (28 px per side) is what restores exact 16:9. **Rule: when the user proposes a crop that claims to preserve an aspect ratio, verify the math before executing. Height-only crops never preserve aspect.**
- **1224 width is the correct rounding of `688 × 16/9 = 1223.111`, rounded UP to the nearest even integer.** Even-integer width+height is required for H.264 YUV 4:2:0 chroma subsampling — odd values either fail outright or trigger ffmpeg's auto-pad which can create a 1-pixel green/black border. Rounding up rather than down means the frame is 1 px wider than mathematical-exact 16:9 (aspect 1224/688 = 1.7791 vs ideal 1.7778, delta 0.07%) — imperceptible when rendered inside a 16:9 container that uses `object-fit: cover`, and cleaner than rounding down which would have required asymmetric x-offset (28 on one side, 29 on the other). **Rule: H.264 asset dimensions must be even. When computing from aspect math, round UP to the nearest even integer rather than to the nearest or nearest-down, and verify centering offsets remain integer-even.**
- **Zero CSS changes required because the container was already 16:9.** The previous session's `.case-image.case-image--video-wide { aspect-ratio: 16/9 }` override still applies — the container was sized for 16:9 content, and the new asset *is* 16:9 (with 0.07% rounding-epsilon).
