The container rule didn't need to know or care that the asset's absolute pixel dimensions changed from 1280×720 to 1224×688. **Rule: when the CSS sets aspect-ratio (not width/height), assets that change resolution while keeping aspect don't require any CSS updates. Aspect-ratio-sized containers are resolution-independent.**
- **v1 preservation via `cp` before re-encode, not after.** The preserve step ran before the overwrite because ffmpeg writes to the target path directly — once the re-encode runs, the original is gone. `cp img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4 img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo-v1-fullheight.mp4` then re-encode. Explicit descriptive suffix (`-v1-fullheight`) documents WHAT this version is (the full-height pre-crop), not just WHEN it was captured. If a v3 lands later with a different crop, the sibling files self-document what each represents. **Rule: version asset backups with semantic descriptors (`-v1-fullheight`, `-v2-cropped`), not just numeric versions (`-v1`, `-v2`). Future-you needs to know what the version represents, not just when it was made.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Size went DOWN 6% despite the same CRF and a seemingly "more complex" (cropped-from-higher-res) input.** New file is 430 KB vs v1's 458 KB. Expected rough parity. Explanation: cropping removed 56 px of width and 32 px of height — total pixel count dropped from 921,600 to 842,112 (8.6% fewer pixels per frame). CRF is bit-rate-per-pixel-ish, so fewer pixels = smaller file at the same visual quality. The 6% file size drop tracks the 8.6% pixel reduction closely (slightly less than linear because H.264's per-frame overhead doesn't scale with pixel count). **Rule: cropping reduces file size proportionally to pixel-count reduction, at same CRF. Don't worry that a re-encode will "regain" the size cost.**
- **Duration dropped from 16.175s to 16.158s (17ms delta).** Same phenomenon as the initial encode — ffmpeg re-normalizes frame timing on re-encode and occasionally drops a trailing partial-frame group. 17ms is less than a single frame at 60fps (16.67ms) — imperceptible. Worth noting for future audit: re-encodes can nibble a few ms off duration. If exact sync matters (e.g., syncing to audio or captions), use `-r` to force output frame rate. Not needed here.
- **Preview image eyeballing was wrong by a factor of 4-5.** The header bar looked like it occupied ~15-20% of the frame in the scaled preview, implying 140 px in a 720 px source — which is roughly what Rotem estimated. Actual measurement: 20 px, i.e. 2.8% of frame height. The preview had compressed the vertical axis proportionally with the horizontal (both at ~75% scale), so the error wasn't axis-anisotropic — it was simply that small-pixel-count regions become even harder to estimate in a downscaled preview. Absolute pixel counts for small regions need measurement, not estimation.

**What I learned:**
- **Two-stage diagnostic workflow for crop/geometry tasks:** (1) Extract a frame at full resolution. (2) Generate a grid-annotated preview for the human AND pixel-sample the actual full-res data for the proposal. The grid-preview + the numeric measurement converge on a single correct answer that both sides agree on. Skipping either stage causes misalignment — pixel-sampling without a visual gives you numbers the user can't verify; the visual without pixel-sampling lets small-region errors through.
- **"Preserve aspect ratio" + "crop from top" + "no scaling" is a three-way constraint, and the only solution is: crop horizontal trim equal to (width * N / original_height) where N is the top-crop height, centered.** Documented as the canonical fix for this class of geometry problem. For 1280×720 → crop N from top, preserve 16:9, no scaling: new_width = (720-N)*16/9, rounded up to even, center-cropped horizontally. Stored this as a reusable recipe.
- **Naming convention for versioned assets on this site:** `<asset>-v<N>-<descriptor>.<ext>`. Examples: `ghost-demo-v1-fullheight.mp4`, `hero-v2.png` (lexisnexis). Descriptor is what makes the version semantically distinct, not just a temporal marker. When a new version supersedes an older one, the older one takes the descriptor (so the current/active file stays at the base name and the preserved old file describes WHY it's still around).
- **CSS aspect-ratio containers are the right abstraction for video assets whose exact pixel dimensions may shift across edits.** Once the container is `aspect-ratio: 16/9`, asset re-encodes at 1280×720, 1920×1080, 1224×688, 960×540 all work identically — the container sizes to fit the layout and `object-fit: cover` handles any rounding-epsilon aspect drift. Locking the container to absolute pixel dimensions (`width: 1280px; height: 720px`) would have required CSS updates for every re-encode. `aspect-ratio` saves that entire class of edit.
- **Round-to-even for H.264 dimensions, always round UP when enforcing aspect math.** 1223 would produce an odd-width asset that some encoders handle poorly; 1224 is safe. The 0.07% over-width is inside the container-aspect rounding tolerance and causes no visible effect.
- **Future video encodes for this site should target exact pixel dimensions computed from aspect math up-front, not retrofitted.** If I'd known at the original encode that the asset would be placed in a 16:9 container and would need a future top-crop, I could have encoded the v1 at a pre-cropped geometry in one pass rather than encode-then-recrop-then-reencode. Doesn't matter for a once-off, but if videos become a repeat pipeline, a "crop intent" parameter at encode time saves a re-encode round later.

**Files touched:** `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` (re-encoded, now 1224×688, 430 KB, 16.158s — was 1280×720, 458 KB, 16.175s), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo-poster.jpg` (regenerated from new video, now 1224×688, 59 KB — was 1280×720, 63 KB), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo-v1-fullheight.mp4` (new, `cp` preservation of the pre-crop v1 before overwrite — 1280×720, 458 KB, identical to the file that was at the base path as of this morning), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `work/ghost.html` (`<video src>` path is unchanged — points to the re-encoded file at the same base name; `poster` attribute likewise unchanged), `styles.css` (the `.case-image.case-image--video-wide { aspect-ratio: 16/9 }` rule from the earlier session applies without modification because the new asset is still 16:9 within 0.07% tolerance), `index.html`, `about.html`, all other case study pages, `main.js`, `game.js`. Homepage bento Ghost card's `ghost-thumbnail.mp4` is unchanged — this task was for the case study demo only.

## 2026-04-24 — Ghost bento thumbnail v3 swap: filename-stable overwrite, no crop needed

**What I did:** Replaced the homepage Ghost bento thumbnail video with a new source from `~/Downloads/ghostthumbnailloop_v3.mov` (2736×1552, 1.72 MB, 6.12s, aspect 1.763). Ran the same frame-diagnostic workflow established earlier today for the demo video: extracted frames at t=1s/3s/5s, pixel-sampled top/bottom/left/right edge strips to map content-vs-padding zones, then proposed framing options. Unlike the demo video (which had a "Scan: Payments" header strip that needed a Y=32 crop), this source is clean edge-to-edge UI content with 10-16 px of dark-padding safe zone on every edge. No crop needed. Rotem approved Option A: straight `scale=960:540` with no crop filter. Final output: 960×540, 76 KB, 5.82s, CRF 28, well under the 1 MB target (92% under, same dramatic headroom as v2's 55 KB because this is also a low-temporal-entropy UI mockup clip). Regenerated poster from the new video at t=2s. Preserved v2 as `ghost-thumbnail-v2-old.mp4` via `mv` (not `cp` — the v3 encode writes to the base path, so the rename frees the name; the backup folder also holds a separate `ghost-thumbnail-pre-v3-swap.mp4` + `-poster-pre-v3-swap.jpg` copy for extra redundancy).

**What worked:**
- **Filename-stable overwrite workflow.** The `<video src>` and `<video poster>` attributes at [index.html:200-201](index.html#L200-L201) were unchanged — point to the same base filenames as before. All that changed is the bytes on disk at those paths. This is the cleanest possible HTML/CSS diff: zero. The user explicitly called this out in the task ("No HTML changes needed since we're overwriting the existing filename. Existing `<video>` tag in the bento card will pick up the new file automatically") and the pattern held. **Rule: when iterating on a media asset behind a stable reference, keep the filename stable and version the preserved older copies with `-vN-descriptor.mp4` suffixes. The HTML reference stays at `<asset-name>.mp4` permanently; version churn lives in sibling filenames only.**
- **Pre-encode edge-sampling ruled out the "do we need a crop?" question decisively.** Python PIL scan of top 20 rows, bottom 20 rows, left 20 cols, right 20 cols at 5 sample positions on the non-trimmed axis showed all four edges were solid dark RGB(12-17, 11-17, 16-18) — no content at edges, no header strip, no UI chrome to remove. This let me confidently recommend Option A (no crop) rather than defaulting to the same Y=32 crop I did for the demo video out of pattern-matching. **Rule: pattern-matching the previous session's solution to a similar-shaped problem is a failure mode. The demo video had a "Scan: Payments" header that needed cropping; this thumbnail doesn't. Each new source needs its own frame diagnostic — don't assume the previous crop value applies.**
- **0.85% aspect mismatch threshold is the "no crop needed" threshold for this pipeline.** Source was 2736×1552 (aspect 1.763); target container is 16:9 (aspect 1.778). Delta 0.85%. During `scale=960:540`, ffmpeg applies an anisotropic scale that's effectively a 0.85% vertical compression — below human detection threshold for aspect distortion (typically 2-3% for UI content, higher for abstract imagery). For comparison, the original v1 of this thumbnail (April 24 morning) had a 5.5% aspect mismatch (2766×1646, aspect 1.680) which DID need crop-then-scale. **Rule of thumb: aspect mismatch < ~1.5% → scale-only is fine. Aspect mismatch > ~3% → crop to 16:9 before scale. In between, depends on content; if edges have real content, crop; if edges are padding, scale-only works.**
- **`mv` vs `cp` for the preservation step.** Used `mv img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4 img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail-v2-old.mp4` rather than `cp` because the re-encode writes to the base path and would overwrite immediately — `mv` frees the base name in one step without leaving an intermediate state where the file exists twice. Also faster on large files (metadata-only rename vs full byte copy). `cp` + encode would have worked too but wastes disk IO. **Rule: when preserving an old version right before an overwrite-to-same-path encode, use `mv` to the preserved name, not `cp`. The file is about to be replaced anyway.**
- **`v3-old` versioned asset naming held up across three iterations now.** `ghost-thumbnail-v2-old.mp4` (preserved this session) + `ghost-demo-v1-fullheight.mp4` (preserved in the earlier session today) — same naming pattern, different semantic descriptors. The `-v2-old` is semantically distinct from the generic `-old.png` pattern used for swapped-out static images elsewhere because the version number specifies WHICH old copy (there could eventually be a v3-old when v4 lands).
- **Backup folder `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/` accumulates per-swap snapshots.** Now holds: `index.html`, `ghost.html`, `styles.css`, `ghost-img-snapshot/` tree (from morning v1→v2 video wiring), `ghost-pre-aspect-fix.html` + `styles-pre-aspect-fix.css` (from late-morning aspect fix), `ghost-thumbnail-pre-v3-swap.mp4` + `ghost-thumbnail-poster-pre-v3-swap.jpg` (from this session). One dated archive folder per day per asset-family accumulates a full day's iteration history without creating a new folder per change.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Initial instinct was to propose Option B (crop 12 px padding before scale).** My first draft recommendation included the symmetric 6-px top / 6-px bottom crop to land on a clean 2736×1540 → 960×540. On reflection, the 0.85% distortion was so far below threshold that the crop filter added zero visual benefit and some complexity. Recommended Option A instead. **Rule: when the "cleaner" option is within 1% of the "simpler" option on the metric that matters (visual fidelity), the simpler option wins. Don't over-engineer for aspect purity when the container is already absorbing the mismatch via `object-fit: cover`.**
- **Output is 76 KB, larger than v2's 55 KB despite being the "same kind of asset" and same CRF.** Expected parity or smaller because v3 is shorter (5.82s vs v2's 3.69s was same CRF, but v3 is also shorter than original 6.12s source). Actually recomputing: v3 came in at 76 KB / 5.82s = 13 KB/s; v2 was 55 KB / 3.69s = 15 KB/s. So v3 is actually encoding-rate-LOWER per second of content despite being visually richer. Total file size is just longer duration × lower per-second rate. **Rule: file size comparisons across iterations should normalize to bitrate (KB/s), not absolute size. Same-duration comparisons are fair; different-duration comparisons aren't.**
- **Duration dropped 6.12s → 5.82s (300ms, ~5%).** Much larger frame-rate-normalization trim than previous encodes (which lost ~17ms). Why: source declared `r_frame_rate=120/1` but actual avg was `6144/193 ≈ 31.8 fps`. ffmpeg's default output frame rate inherited the declared 120/1, but actual frame availability was only ~32/s — so roughly 300ms worth of trailing frames got dropped at the VFR → CFR boundary. For a seamless loop on a silent bento thumbnail, 300ms shorter is imperceptible; if exact-duration mattered, `-r 30` or `-vsync cfr -r 30` would stabilize. Not added here. **Rule: iPhone / mobile-captured `.mov` files with declared 120fps but actual ~30fps can lose several hundred ms to frame-rate normalization. If duration matters, force output frame rate explicitly; if not, accept the trim.**

**What I learned:**
- **Three Ghost videos shipped today, each with a different framing decision, each documented:** (1) Homepage thumbnail v1: full-frame crop because v1 source had 5.5% aspect mismatch with real content at edges. (2) Case-study demo v1→v2: top-crop 32 px to remove "Scan: Payments" header strip + horizontal trim to restore exact 16:9. (3) Homepage thumbnail v3 (today): straight scale with no crop because v3 source has 0.85% mismatch with padded edges. The decision tree is reusable for future video-asset swaps: (a) inventory source aspect, (b) pixel-sample edges to identify content-vs-padding zones, (c) if mismatch > 3% OR edges have content, crop; else straight scale.
- **Canonical bento thumbnail encode command for this codebase:** `ffmpeg -y -i <source> -vf "scale=960:540" -c:v libx264 -crf 28 -preset medium -pix_fmt yuv420p -movflags +faststart -an <target>.mp4`. With optional `crop=...,` prefix if edge content or aspect mismatch requires it. Bento thumbnails target <1 MB. Case study videos target <2.5 MB at CRF 24 and 1280×720 (or similar).
- **Poster frame at 2s is the right default for a 6-second loop.** Captures ~33% into the clip, past any intro fade-in, before any outro. The poster image I just extracted (960×540, 40 KB) shows the full Design System ↔ Production comparison with deviation callouts mid-animation — visually represents the video's content. If future thumbnails are shorter than 4s, adjust to 1s; longer than 10s, consider 3-4s.
- **Frame-rate declaration lies on phone-captured `.mov` files.** Both Ghost video sources today (demo + thumbnail) declared `r_frame_rate=120/1` but had `avg_frame_rate` around 30-55. This is a known iPhone ProRes / HDR recording artifact — the container is stamped at the max supported rate even when actual capture happened at lower rate. ffmpeg honors the declared rate on output by default, causing the trailing-frame trim. Benign for loops, documented for future reference.
- **"Filename-stable overwrite" as a first-class pattern.** When the site's HTML points at a stable-named asset (not a hashed or versioned URL), iteration on that asset is a pure bytes-on-disk operation. No HTML diff, no CSS diff, no cache-bust URL — just the new file at the old path. This only works because the asset is served same-origin without far-future caching. If the site ever adopts a CDN with immutable caching, the filename-stable pattern breaks and we'd need content-hashed filenames; worth knowing pre-deploy.

**Files touched:** `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` (re-encoded from v3 source, now 960×540, 76 KB, 5.82s — was 960×540, 55 KB, 3.69s from v1 source), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail-poster.jpg` (regenerated at t=2s from new thumbnail, now 960×540, 40 KB — was 960×540, 38 KB), `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail-v2-old.mp4` (new, `mv` of the pre-v3 thumbnail for fast rollback — 960×540, 55 KB, 3.69s, was the file at the base path as of this morning's encode), `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/ghost-thumbnail-pre-v3-swap.mp4` (new, extra `cp` backup of pre-v3 thumbnail — redundant with `-v2-old` but lives in archive folder), `_archive/_backup-ghost-videos-2026-04-24/ghost-thumbnail-poster-pre-v3-swap.jpg` (new, `cp` backup of pre-v3 poster), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `index.html` (the Ghost bento card `<video>` element at line 199 — src/poster paths unchanged, pointing at the re-encoded files at the same base names), `work/ghost.html` (the case study page uses `ghost-demo.mp4`, not `ghost-thumbnail.mp4` — unrelated asset), `styles.css` (no rule changes; `.bento-card-thumb video { ... }` and aspect-ratio container at `.bento-card-d .bento-card-thumb` apply unchanged because new asset is still 16:9 at 960×540 exactly), `about.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`, case study demo video (`ghost-demo.mp4` and its poster — unrelated asset), all other `img/ghost/` static screenshots.

## 2026-04-24 — Bento mobile-only reorder via display: contents + flex order

**What I did:** Changed the mobile-stacked order of the 4 homepage bento cards from DOM order (LexisNexis → Pulse → GoteFigure → Ghost) to a new editorial order (LexisNexis → Ghost → Pulse → GoteFigure) without touching any HTML. Desktop layout stays byte-identical. Implemented inside `@media (max-width: 767px)` at [styles.css:1342-1350](styles.css#L1342-L1350) with: (1) `display: contents` on both `.bento-row-top` and `.bento-row-bottom`, which dissolves the two row-grid containers on mobile and promotes all 4 cards to be direct flex children of `.projects-bento` (already `display: flex; flex-direction: column`); (2) `order` properties on `.bento-card-a/b/c/d` (values 1/3/4/2 respectively) to interleave the promoted cards across the former row boundary. Kept the preceding `grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 16px` on the rows as a safety fallback (dead code under `display: contents` but harmless, and intentional per user decision).

**What worked:**
- **`display: contents` on the row wrappers is the canonical fix for "reorder across grid-row boundaries" without HTML changes.** The DOM structure has cards split between two sibling row containers (`.bento-row-top` with a+b, `.bento-row-bottom` with c+d). CSS `order` is scoped to siblings under the same flex/grid parent — it cannot reorder across different parents. `display: contents` makes the row box disappear in layout (no grid, no border-box, no padding/margin) while keeping children in the DOM. Promoted children become flex-siblings of `.projects-bento`, and `order` works. **Rule: when CSS `order` is too scoped (can't cross a parent boundary), add `display: contents` to the intermediate parent(s) to flatten the layout tree without changing the DOM tree. Works for reorders, centering, spanning — anywhere a "structural div" is getting in the way of the layout algorithm but deleting it would require HTML edits.**
- **Flagged the breakpoint mismatch before executing.** User's spec said `@media (max-width: 1000px)`; actual bento stacking kicks in at `max-width: 767px`. The existing `@media (max-width: 1000px)` block in the file is for the intro animation cutoff, unrelated to bento. Between 768-1000px, cards are still in 2-column grids — interleaving order a/d/b/c would be visually incoherent because cards c and d stay side-by-side with their row partners. User confirmed 767px was correct. **Rule: when asked to put CSS inside a specific media query, verify the query matches the visual behavior the rule should trigger. A wrong breakpoint applied correctly is still wrong.**
- **`order` is a flex/grid-only property, and the parent was already `display: flex; flex-direction: column`.** No need to add/change display type on `.projects-bento` — it was already the right context. The only change was dissolving the intermediate row boxes so `order` could see all 4 cards as siblings. **Rule: before proposing display changes, inspect the layout context the property needs to work in. If the nearest flex/grid ancestor is already correct, the only change is at the intermediate level.**
- **Zero desktop CSS changes.** All new rules live inside `@media (max-width: 767px)`. Above that breakpoint, cards keep their DOM order inside their row grids (`grid-template-columns: 1.35fr 1fr` and `1fr 1.35fr`), the zigzag layout is untouched. Verified via grep that no bare `.bento-card-{a,b,c,d} { order: ... }` or bare `display: contents` exists outside the media query. **Rule: for mobile-only layout changes, keep all rules inside the target media query; never add unscoped properties with "and we'll override on desktop" — that leaks regression risk into the default path.**
- **Kept the existing `grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 16px` on the rows as a safety fallback.** Under `display: contents`, these properties are dead — a non-grid element doesn't use `grid-template-columns`, and `gap` on a dissolved box has no box to gap. But leaving them in means: if a future edit removes the `display: contents` rule (or scopes it further), the rows gracefully fall back to the single-column grid layout that was the pre-reorder mobile behavior. No layout collapse, no regression. The cost of keeping the dead rules is ~4 lines of CSS. **Rule: when overriding a CSS rule via a stronger layout mode (e.g., changing `display`), consider whether leaving the overridden rules in place provides a graceful fallback for future edits. If the cost is small and the safety is real, keep them.**
- **Order values 1,2,3,4 (not 1,10,20,30 or 0,1,2,3).** Simple sequential integers are more readable. Gaps (like 10,20,30) are useful when you expect insertions but the set is fixed at 4 cards for the foreseeable future. Starting at 1 (not 0) is also a minor convention — `order: 0` is the default flex value, so explicit orders starting at 1 make "this card has an explicit order" visually distinct from "this card is using the default." **Rule: use sequential integers starting at 1 unless you anticipate insertions; gaps of 10 are premature optimization for a 4-item set.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Initial reading assumed `order` alone would work.** The user's task proposal listed exactly four `order` rules and implied that would be sufficient. Only after inspecting the DOM structure (two `.bento-row-*` wrappers, not a flat flex/grid) did the parent-scoping issue become visible. Had I executed blindly, the result at mobile would have been cards a/b still above c/d (rows stay as containers), with `order` swapping within each pair — NOT the a/d/b/c interleaving the user actually wanted. **Rule: before proposing a layout solution involving `order`, `grid-area`, or `flex-basis`, always verify the current parent-child structure in the HTML. A CSS property's scope is set by the DOM, and the same property can succeed or fail based on intermediate wrappers the user may not have noticed.**
- **Two separate `.bento-row-top, .bento-row-bottom` rule blocks now exist in the same media query — one for `grid-template-columns`, one for `display: contents`.** CSS doesn't forbid this (the rules merge at compute time) and I kept them as separate blocks because they have different semantic purposes (the first is the "fallback layout" per user decision; the second is "the actual mobile layout"). A single merged block would have been 1 line shorter but obscured the fallback intent. Leaving them separate is clearer. **Rule: two CSS rule blocks for the same selector list in the same stylesheet are fine when they communicate different intent. Merging for brevity can destroy semantic grouping. Prefer clarity over compactness.**
- **Did NOT verify the reorder visually in a browser.** No headless render check was performed. Desktop layout regression is unlikely (no desktop rules changed, media query scope verified), but the specific mobile stack order hasn't been visually confirmed — a typo in `order` values (e.g., swapping two of them) would still produce a valid stack but in the wrong order. **Rule: for layout-visual changes, even with a confident code diff, flag "not visually verified" explicitly. The user should eyeball the change at the target breakpoint before shipping.**

**What I learned:**
- **`display: contents` is under-used in this codebase and probably worth promoting to a known pattern.** This is the second time it would have been the right tool — the first being unexplored in prior layout edits where the user resorted to HTML restructuring or CSS Grid area trickery. Document in the house style as "the escape hatch for DOM-vs-layout mismatch." Any time the HTML has a wrapper that's structurally correct but layout-wrong, `display: contents` can dissolve the wrapper without deleting it.
- **Mobile-only layout changes have a strong architectural idiom on this site:** scope all overrides inside `@media (max-width: 767px)`, never touch the default (desktop-first) rules. The desktop layout is the "source of truth" and mobile is a tuned derivative. Reversing this (mobile-first) would require re-architecting, which is out of scope. This project is desktop-first by convention; honor it.
- **User's breakpoint-mismatch (1000px vs 767px) pattern:** a likely failure mode for future tasks is the user specifying a breakpoint that almost-but-not-quite matches the visual behavior they want. Always verify the breakpoint against the CSS where the relevant behavior changes. In this file: `1199px` = nav/case-study, `1000px` = intro cutoff, `767px` = main mobile, `375px` = extra-small tweaks. Four breakpoints, four purposes. Don't assume they're interchangeable.
- **Editorial order vs DOM order is a legitimate design decision.** The new mobile order (LexisNexis → Ghost → Pulse → GoteFigure) moves the two biggest/flashiest projects (LexisNexis as shipped, Ghost as visually striking) to slots 1 and 2, with the two concept/personal projects (Pulse, GoteFigure) as 3 and 4. On mobile where the viewer scrolls one card at a time, the editorial hierarchy matters more than on desktop where the zigzag bento creates a Gestalt read-all-four-at-once experience. Worth noting if future projects are added: the `order` values will need to be revisited to integrate them into this editorial sequence.

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (9 new lines inserted at lines 1342-1350 inside the `@media (max-width: 767px)` block — 4 lines of `display: contents` override + 4 order rules + 1 comment; no removals; no changes outside the media query), `_archive/_backup-bento-mobile-reorder-2026-04-24/styles.css` (new pre-change backup), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `index.html` (bento DOM order unchanged — cards still in a/b/c/d source order, still wrapped in `.bento-row-top` and `.bento-row-bottom` divs; no new classes, no attribute changes), `about.html`, all `work/*.html` case study pages, `main.js`, `game.js`, all video/image assets, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Desktop zigzag bento layout (LexisNexis 1.35fr + Pulse 1fr on top row, GoteFigure 1fr + Ghost 1.35fr on bottom row) is untouched and renders identically above 767px. Existing `grid-template-columns: 1fr` fallback on the rows inside the mobile media query is intentionally preserved (dead code under `display: contents`, but safe revert path if the reorder is ever backed out).

## 2026-04-24 — PATTERN: Thumbnail Propagation Checklist

**What I did:** Fixed a cross-page thumbnail inconsistency where the Ghost homepage bento card used the new looping video thumbnail (`img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4`) but the "Other Work" sections on `work/lexisnexis.html` and `work/pulse.html` still referenced the old static PNG (`../img/ghost/thumbnail.png`). Replaced both `<img>` tags with the same `<video>` element pattern used on the homepage (autoplay, muted, loop, playsinline, preload=metadata, poster fallback, aria-label). Added a new CSS rule in `styles.css` at line 1084 — `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail img, .other-work-card .project-thumbnail video { width: 100%; height: 100%; object-fit: cover; object-position: center; display: block; }` — because the existing `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` container (aspect-ratio 16/10, overflow hidden, flex centered) had no explicit img/video sizing rule. A bare `<video>` has no default `max-width: 100%` the way the global `img` rule provides, so without this addition the video would have rendered at intrinsic size and misfit the 16:10 frame. The new rule also normalizes the existing PNG rendering (Pulse and LexisNexis Other Work thumbs) to cover-fit, which is a visual-consistency improvement — verified PNGs still display correctly after the change.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic grep before editing.** `grep -n "ghost/thumbnail\|ghost/videos" work/*.html` immediately surfaced the two stale references (lexisnexis.html:208, pulse.html:191) and confirmed ghost.html's own `ghost-demo.mp4` is a separate asset (the in-page hero demo, not a thumbnail — correctly left alone). `work/gotefigure.html` DOES have an "Other Work" section (at line 151) but it links only to lexisnexis and pulse — NOT ghost — so no Ghost thumbnail reference existed there to update. My initial diagnostic claimed gotefigure had no Other Work section at all; that was wrong and was corrected on the verification pass. The lesson: do not confuse "no reference to THIS project" with "no Other Work section at all" — those are different conditions and the second is a stronger, wrong-er claim. **Rule: for any "replace asset X across the site" task, grep the exact asset filename AND the project-slug directory (both `ghost/thumbnail` and `ghost/videos` here) across all HTML files first. The grep output IS the edit plan. Separately grep for `other-work` to enumerate which case studies have the section at all, independent of which ones happen to link to the project you're updating.**
- **CSS gap detection before editing HTML.** Before assuming the video would render correctly, I checked whether `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` had any explicit img/video sizing rule. It didn't — PNGs were working only because of the global `img { max-width: 100% }` at styles.css:61. Videos have no equivalent global default. Without the new combined rule, the `<video>` would have rendered at intrinsic dimensions and the container's `overflow: hidden` + flex centering would have produced a cropped/misfit thumbnail. **Rule: before swapping `<img>` for `<video>` in a styled container, check that the container has explicit rules for video (not just img). If not, add a combined img-and-video rule so both render identically.**
- **Combined selector for img and video.** Writing `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail img, .other-work-card .project-thumbnail video { ... }` in a single rule block (rather than two separate blocks) enforces that the two media types render identically inside the container. Future mixed content (some cards PNG, some cards video) will stay visually consistent. **Rule: when a container may hold either `<img>` or `<video>` thumbnails, write one combined selector so the rendering rules cannot drift between them.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The homepage swap alone wasn't the full fix.** The earlier Ghost video work updated only `index.html` — a natural place to stop, since the homepage bento card is the primary surface. But "Other Work" sections at the bottom of case studies re-expose the same projects as thumbnails, and those are a separate location that needed the same asset update. The inconsistency only became visible when a viewer clicked into Pulse or LexisNexis and scrolled to the bottom — a non-obvious review path. **Rule: "thumbnail swap" is never a single-file edit on this site. Every project has at least 3 thumbnail surfaces: (1) homepage bento, (2) Other Work on every OTHER case study that links to it, (3) potential meta/og:image tags. Always check all three before declaring a swap complete.**

**PATTERN: Thumbnail Propagation Checklist**

When replacing a project's thumbnail (static image OR video), the new asset must be applied in ALL of these locations:

1. Homepage bento card ([index.html](index.html)) — primary location.
2. "Other Work" section on EVERY OTHER case study page that links to this project. **As of 2026-04-24, Other Work sections exist on all three of these case study pages**:
   - [work/lexisnexis.html](work/lexisnexis.html) — links to: pulse, ghost
   - [work/pulse.html](work/pulse.html) — links to: lexisnexis, ghost
   - [work/gotefigure.html](work/gotefigure.html) — links to: lexisnexis, pulse
   - [work/ghost.html](work/ghost.html) — links to: lexisnexis, pulse
   
   (Note: `work/ghost.html` also has an Other Work section — included for completeness. The link sets above are the 2026-04-24 snapshot; verify with `grep -n "other-work-card" work/*.html` before editing, because future sessions may add/remove links.)
3. Any "Related Projects" or "You might also like" sections (none exist as of 2026-04-24, but check).
4. Favicon / social preview / og:image meta tags (if the thumbnail is used there).

**Verification command** — run this for EVERY thumbnail swap regardless of which project is being updated:

```
grep -rn "[project-slug]/thumbnail\|[project-slug]/videos" --include="*.html" . | grep -v _archive
```

Replace `[project-slug]` with the project folder name (e.g., `ghost`, `pulse`, `lexisnexis`, `gotefigure`). The output should list: homepage bento card + every Other Work card that links to the project. Cross-check against the link map above. Any single replacement that doesn't update ALL these places creates visual inconsistency between the homepage and the case study pages — a viewer sees the animated thumbnail on the homepage, then a stale static one in a case study footer.

**Companion command** — enumerate all Other Work sections regardless of which project's thumbnail is being updated, so you don't confuse "this project isn't linked from X" with "X has no Other Work section":

```
grep -n "other-work-card" work/*.html
```

**CSS pattern — video thumbnails in Other Work cards:**

If adding a video thumbnail to an Other Work card for the first time, verify `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` has a combined img+video sizing rule. As of 2026-04-24 this lives at [styles.css:1084-1090](styles.css#L1084-L1090):

```css
.other-work-card .project-thumbnail img,
.other-work-card .project-thumbnail video {
  width: 100%;
  height: 100%;
  object-fit: cover;
  object-position: center;
  display: block;
}
```

This rule must stay combined (img AND video). If it's ever split or removed, adding a new video to an Other Work card will misfit the 16:10 container.

**Ghost thumbnail video — current deployment state (as of 2026-04-24):**

`img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` is now live as the Ghost thumbnail in ALL three referenced locations:
- [index.html:199-208](index.html#L199-L208) — homepage bento card
- [work/lexisnexis.html:208-218](work/lexisnexis.html#L208-L218) — Other Work card (updated this session)
- [work/pulse.html:191-201](work/pulse.html#L191-L201) — Other Work card (updated this session)

Homepage uses `img/ghost/videos/...` (no `../`); case studies in `work/` use `../img/ghost/videos/...` because they're one directory deep. The asset file itself is stable-named — any future re-encodes overwrite at the same path and all three locations update automatically without HTML edits.

**What I learned:**
- **Thumbnail surfaces are plural, not singular.** Always treat a thumbnail update as a multi-file task. The homepage is the most visible location but not the only location.
- **The `img, video` combined selector pattern is the right default** for any container that might hold either type of media. On this site it applies specifically to `.bento-card-thumb` (already had it at styles.css:570-584) and `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` (added this session). Any new card pattern introduced in the future should follow the same shape.
- **`overflow: hidden` on a thumbnail container silently hides misfit issues** — the video renders at intrinsic size, gets cropped by the container, and visually "looks like it's working" from the top-left corner. Always verify the full rendered frame after a swap, not just "does anything appear."

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html` (img→video swap in the Ghost Other Work card, originally a single `<img>` line at 208, now a ~12-line `<video>` element at lines 208-218), `work/pulse.html` (img→video swap in the Ghost Other Work card, same shape — single line at 191 became ~12-line `<video>` at 191-201), `styles.css` (new `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail img, .other-work-card .project-thumbnail video` combined rule at lines 1084-1090, 8 new lines inserted immediately after the `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` container rule), `_archive/_backup-thumbnail-consistency-2026-04-24/` (new backup directory containing pre-change copies of the three modified files — lexisnexis.html, pulse.html, styles.css), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `index.html` (already had the video thumbnail — unchanged this session), `work/ghost.html` (uses `ghost-demo.mp4` as the in-page hero demo, not `ghost-thumbnail.mp4` — different asset, unrelated), `work/gotefigure.html` (no "Other Work" section exists — no Ghost reference to update), `main.js`, `game.js`, all other image/video assets, `about.html`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

## 2026-04-24 — Other Work thumbnail aspect ratio 16:10 → 16:9

**What I did:** Changed `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` aspect-ratio from `16 / 10` to `16 / 9` in [styles.css:1075](styles.css#L1075) (single-line change). This brings the Other Work thumbnail container into alignment with the homepage bento thumbnail container ([styles.css:645-648](styles.css#L645-L648), which defines `.bento-card-a/b/c/d .bento-card-thumb { aspect-ratio: 16 / 9 }` on desktop), eliminating a cross-surface inconsistency where the same project thumbnails rendered at different proportions on the homepage versus inside case study footers. The change is purely a container-rule edit; no asset changes, no HTML changes, no per-page changes. All four case study pages (pulse, ghost, lexisnexis, gotefigure) with "Other Work" sections inherit the new ratio automatically.

**Trigger:** The Ghost video thumbnail (16:9 native, 960×540) was rendering with ~5.6% side crop inside the 16:10 Other Work container but with zero crop on the homepage. That visible inconsistency was the entry point; investigating it surfaced the larger issue that the Other Work container's 16:10 ratio was misaligned with the homepage and with the dominant asset ratio (16:9 for the Ghost video, ~1.91:1 for Pulse, ~16:10 for LexisNexis).

**What worked:**
- **Measured before deciding.** Ran `sips -g pixelWidth -g pixelHeight` on the two PNGs and `ffprobe` on the MP4 before proposing a container ratio. Results: Pulse 800×420 (1.905), LexisNexis 2560×1593 (1.607), Ghost 960×540 (1.778). Without the measurements I'd have been guessing which ratio was "best"; with them, the trade-off became explicit — 16:9 is a net improvement (Ghost goes from 5.6% crop to 0, Pulse improves from 9.5% to 3.6% side crop, LexisNexis trades near-zero for ~5.3% top/bottom crop). **Rule: before proposing an `aspect-ratio` change, measure every asset that will flow through the container. The container ratio should be chosen to minimize total visible cropping across the assets, not to match a platonic ideal.**
- **Computed the object-fit cover math explicitly.** With `object-fit: cover`, the shorter dimension fills the container and the longer dimension overflows by (content-ratio / container-ratio − 1) × 100%, split evenly across both cropped edges. Writing this out in the proposal made the trade-off auditable for the user rather than hand-waved. **Rule: when presenting an aspect-ratio change that involves cropping, compute the per-asset crop percentage for the current AND proposed ratio. "Looks better" is not a plan; "Ghost loses 0%, Pulse loses 3.6%, LexisNexis loses 5.3%" is.**
- **Single-line change, zero HTML churn.** All four Other Work-bearing case study pages inherit the rule — no per-file edits, no risk of drift between pages. **Rule: when a container rule is defined once and applied across many pages, change the rule. Don't duplicate changes per page.**
- **Chose a ratio that matches the homepage bento on desktop (16/9).** Viewers now see thumbnails at the same proportions on the homepage and inside case study footers — the animation of the Ghost video, the horizontal reach of the Pulse dashboard, and the layout of the LexisNexis token pipeline all read consistently across surfaces. **Rule: cross-surface thumbnail aspect ratio is part of the Thumbnail Propagation Checklist — not just the asset file but the container shape should match between homepage and Other Work sections. A 16:9 asset rendered in a 16:9 container on one page and a 16:10 container on another is an inconsistency even if the asset file is identical.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **LexisNexis is now the "worst fit" where it was previously the "best fit."** Pre-change, LexisNexis at 1.607 was almost perfectly matched to the 16:10 (1.600) container — near-zero crop. Post-change, LexisNexis at 1.607 in a 1.778 container takes ~5.3% top/bottom crop. The fix for Ghost made LexisNexis slightly worse. Whether this matters depends on what content lives in the top/bottom 5% of the LexisNexis thumbnail — if it's the token grid UI header or the metric callout, the crop is bad; if it's padding/negative space, it's fine. **Follow-up item: verify LexisNexis Other Work thumbnail visually at the new 16:9 container on a rendered page. If content is clipped, re-export [img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png](img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png) at 16:9 (e.g., crop from 2560×1593 to 2560×1440, or re-export from source at the target ratio). This would be the "Approach C" targeted follow-up that was deferred from the proposal.** Not doing it preemptively because the current ~5.3% crop may well be invisible.
- **Aspect-ratio mismatch between container and asset is a silent design bug.** There's no compile error, no console warning, no broken layout — the page renders, the thumbnails appear, and the crop happens where nobody is looking. The Ghost video rendering perfectly on the homepage (16:9 asset in 16:9 container) and cropped in Other Work (16:9 asset in 16:10 container) was visible only because the asset was animated and the crop kept the motion readable but lopped the edges. A static asset with the same ratio mismatch would have been almost invisible as a bug. **Rule: when introducing a new thumbnail asset at a specific aspect ratio, audit every container the asset flows through. If any container is at a different ratio, either change the container or explicitly accept the crop — don't let the mismatch live as "we'll fix it when someone notices."**

**Connection to the Thumbnail Propagation Checklist (from the previous session):**

The earlier Thumbnail Propagation Checklist covered "apply the new asset in every location that references this project." This session added a companion concern: **the container aspect ratio at every location should match the asset's native ratio, OR all containers should at least match each other.** Update the checklist's mental model from "propagate asset filename across locations" to "propagate asset filename AND verify container ratio at every location."

Effective checklist (as of 2026-04-24):
1. Asset filename must be identical across all referencing surfaces (homepage bento, Other Work cards, any related-projects sections, meta og:image).
2. Container aspect ratio at every surface should be consistent with itself AND with the asset's native ratio where possible. If ratios differ across surfaces, the asset will render at different proportions — a visible inconsistency.
3. Combined `img, video` sizing rule must exist inside any container that may hold either media type.

Run `grep -n "aspect-ratio" styles.css | grep -i "thumb\|bento\|card"` periodically to audit container ratios in one view.

**What I learned:**
- **Homepage bento sets the aspect-ratio standard for this site.** Desktop bento is 16:9 ([styles.css:645-648](styles.css#L645-L648)). Any new thumbnail surface (Other Work, Related Projects, future sections) should default to 16:9 unless there's a deliberate reason to deviate. The site's visual language is built around 16:9 as the "correct" project thumbnail ratio.
- **Asset re-export is the last resort, not the first.** Approach C (re-export Pulse and LexisNexis PNGs at 16:9) would have produced zero crop for all three assets but required asset work and is irreversible without the source files. Approach A (container change) is one CSS line, zero asset work, fully reversible. Always prefer the CSS-only solution when the crop trade-off is acceptable; reserve re-export for cases where CSS can't hide the problem.
- **`object-fit: contain` looks good in theory and bad in practice.** The user's Approach B — use `contain` to show full thumbnails with letterbox bars — was rejected in proposal because it produces three different visual footprints (perfect fit for Ghost, horizontal bars on LexisNexis, vertical bars on Pulse) where cards that should look equal end up looking unequal. Cropping to a consistent frame is better than preserving content with inconsistent framing. **Rule: when cards are laid out as a grid expecting visual equivalence, `object-fit: cover` is almost always correct even with some crop. `contain` only works when every asset in the grid has the same native aspect ratio as every other asset.**

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (one-line change at [line 1075](styles.css#L1075): `aspect-ratio: 16 / 10` → `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9`; no other edits anywhere in the file; no additions, no removals), `_archive/_backup-other-work-aspect-ratio-2026-04-24/styles.css` (new pre-change backup — full copy of styles.css as it stood after the previous thumbnail-consistency session), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: all HTML files (Other Work markup unchanged — `<img>` and `<video>` rules inside `.project-thumbnail` still inherit the combined sizing rule added in the previous session), all image/video assets (no re-export; Pulse and LexisNexis PNGs remain at their native ratios), `main.js`, `game.js`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

**Follow-up (not done this session):** Visually verify LexisNexis Other Work thumbnail at the new 16:9 ratio. Current crop is ~5.3% top and bottom of [img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png](img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png). If the crop removes meaningful UI/content, re-export the PNG at a 16:9 ratio (source permitting) — this is the targeted Approach C fallback noted in the proposal. Test path: start `python3 -m http.server 8080`, navigate to any case study with an Other Work section (e.g., `work/pulse.html`), scroll to the Other Work grid, and compare the LexisNexis card against the same thumbnail rendered at 16:9 in the homepage bento. If the Other Work rendition visibly clips content vs. the bento rendition, escalate to re-export.



## 2026-04-26 — Ghost demo video: mobile object-fit contain to stop crop

**What I did:** Added a mobile-only override at [styles.css:1382-1387](styles.css#L1382-L1387) inside `@media (max-width: 767px)` so the Ghost case-study demo video (`.case-image.case-image--video-wide video`) renders with `object-fit: contain` and `background: var(--bg-primary)` on phones. Desktop behavior is byte-identical — the desktop rule `.case-image video { object-fit: cover }` at [styles.css:963-969](styles.css#L963-L969) still wins above 767px. No HTML changes, no asset changes. Backed up the pre-change styles.css to `_archive/_backup-ghost-video-mobile-fit-2026-04-26/` before editing.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic-first.** Before proposing the fix I traced every rule that touches `.case-image` and `.case-image video` across the cascade: base at line 940 (16/10), modifier at line 951 (16/9), video sizing at line 963 (cover/center), mobile case-image at line 1377 (only width/margin/border-radius — no aspect-ratio touch). Confirmed via `grep -n "aspect-ratio" styles.css` that nothing else flips the modifier's 16:9 on mobile. The container *is* 16:9 landscape on mobile, not portrait — the user's perception of "portrait" was a side-effect of `object-fit: cover` zooming sub-pixel rounding errors at narrow viewport widths plus the asset's 0.07% aspect epsilon (1224×688 vs. exact 16:9). The fix targets the actual root cause (the `cover` rule) rather than chasing a phantom container-shape problem.
- **Specificity math made the override durable.** The new `.case-image.case-image--video-wide video` selector scores `0,2,1` (two classes + one element) versus the existing desktop `.case-image video` at `0,1,1`. The override wins on specificity AND on source order (later rule, same media context after the cascade resolves). Either of those alone would suffice; both together means the rule survives reorders or future cascade additions. **Rule: when overriding an existing element rule conditionally (media query + modifier class), chain the modifier with the base class in the selector — `.case-image.case-image--video-wide video` not `.case-image--video-wide video` — so the new rule has higher specificity than the desktop default regardless of source order.**
- **Used the project's actual variable name, not the user's proposal.** The user's prompt suggested `var(--color-bg)` but this codebase uses `--bg-primary` (defined at [styles.css:10](styles.css#L10) light, [styles.css:29](styles.css#L29) dark). Verified with `grep -n "^  --bg\|^  --color"` before writing the rule. **Rule: always grep for the exact variable name in the target codebase before using a variable from a user's prompt or from another project's conventions. CSS-variable names don't standardize across projects; what's `--color-bg` in one codebase is `--bg-primary` here.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The "container is portrait" framing in the prompt was wrong-but-useful.** No CSS rule makes the container portrait on mobile — the modifier holds 16:9 from the desktop rule with no breakpoint override. But the user's observation that the video looked cropped was real; the root cause was just different than they framed (sub-pixel `cover` zoom on a near-perfect-aspect asset, not container/asset aspect mismatch). The fix that gets us where the user wants to be — full frame visible, no crop — is the same regardless of which root cause we attribute it to. **Lesson: when the user's diagnostic framing is partially incorrect but the symptom and the goal are clear, do the diagnostic honestly, name the discrepancy, and apply the fix that achieves the stated goal. Don't over-correct on the framing if the prescribed fix is the right one anyway.**
- **`object-fit: contain` on the video, not the container.** With `contain`, empty area inside the video element box shows the *element's* background, not the container's. So the `background: var(--bg-primary)` declaration goes on the `video` selector, not on `.case-image.case-image--video-wide`. Setting the container background would also work but is redundant — the page's case-content area is already `--bg-primary`, so transparent letterbox bars inherit that color anyway. Setting it on the video element is more defensible: it works regardless of what's behind the container.

**What I learned:**
- **Desktop-vs-mobile object-fit tradeoff for video case-images:** desktop gets `object-fit: cover` because the container width is fixed at the 1040px content column and the video's intrinsic 16:9 fills the 16:9 box exactly — zero crop, edge-to-edge fill, looks tight. Mobile gets `object-fit: contain` because narrow viewports (<400px wide) magnify any sub-pixel rounding from `aspect-ratio` resolution into visible edge-clipping of UI text inside the demo video. Letterbox bars at `--bg-primary` are visually quieter than cropped UI text. **Rule for future video case-images on this site: when a video's content has detail near the edges (UI text, labels, buttons), default to mobile `object-fit: contain` + container-bg letterbox. When the content is decorative/centered with safe edge padding, `object-fit: cover` at all breakpoints is fine.**
- **CSS variable name convention here is `--bg-primary`, not `--color-bg`.** Established at [styles.css:10](styles.css#L10) and [styles.css:29](styles.css#L29). Companion: `--bg-secondary`, `--text-primary`, `--text-body`, `--text-secondary`, `--text-muted`, `--text-faint`. No `--color-*` prefix anywhere in this codebase.
- **Backup-folder naming pattern continues:** `_archive/_backup-<descriptor>-<YYYY-MM-DD>/`. This session's folder is `_backup-ghost-video-mobile-fit-2026-04-26/`. One folder per surgical change keeps the backup history greppable by date and by intent.

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (6 new lines inserted at lines 1382-1387 inside the `@media (max-width: 767px)` block immediately after the `.case-image-sm { aspect-ratio: 16 / 10; }` rule — 1 comment line + 4 rule lines + 1 blank-line spacer; no removals; no edits outside the media query; the desktop `.case-image video` rule at lines 963-969 is unchanged), `_archive/_backup-ghost-video-mobile-fit-2026-04-26/styles.css` (new pre-change backup, full copy of styles.css before the mobile rule was added), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `work/ghost.html` (the `<video>` markup at lines 113-122 and the wrapping `.case-image.case-image--video-wide` div at line 112 are both unchanged — the fix is CSS-only), all video/image assets including `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` (still 1224×688, 16:9 within 0.07%), all other case study pages (`work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` — none use the `.case-image--video-wide` modifier, so the new rule is a no-op for them), `index.html`, `about.html`, `main.js`, `game.js`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Desktop rendering above 767px is byte-identical — the new rule is gated by the mobile media query and has no effect on tablet/desktop.

## 2026-04-26 — Regression-audit false alarm: 4 reported regressions, 0 actually regressed

**What I did:** Ran a full diagnostic pass against a brief that listed four production regressions to fix (footer quote on 4 of 6 pages, About intro "UX designer" → "product designer", LexisNexis role "UX Designer" → "Product Designer", GoteFigure dead `model-hoodie`/`tees-collection` image refs). Result: **all four are already correct in the live source**. Old footer quote appears in zero production HTML files (only in `_archive/` snapshots). About intro at [about.html:54](about.html#L54) already reads "I'm a product designer in San Jose...". LexisNexis role at [work/lexisnexis.html:79](work/lexisnexis.html#L79) already reads "Product Designer &amp; Researcher". `work/gotefigure.html` has zero references to `model-hoodie` or `tees-collection`; all 9 image src paths resolve to real files on disk including the Apr 24 v2 swap to `product-grid.png` at [work/gotefigure.html:138](work/gotefigure.html#L138). No edits made. No backup folder created (refused to create `_archive/_backup-regression-fixes-2026-04-26/` because there were no files to back up against). Reported findings, declined to execute the prescribed find/replaces (each would have been a no-op), and proposed three branches to the user: cache/deploy mismatch, wrong working tree, or memory/intent mismatch.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnose-before-edit even when the brief reads as a confident punch list.** The brief framed all four items as "regressions" with prescribed find/replace strings and a "no approval gate needed" instruction (implied by the schedule: "before tonight's case study trim work"). Running the diagnostic greps first — instead of trusting the framing and executing — surfaced that the punch list was based on stale intel, not on the current source. Had I gone straight to Edits, every Edit call would have errored on "old_string not found" and I would have either (a) escalated each error individually creating noise, or (b) tried to "find a near match" and corrupted the file. **Rule: when a brief provides specific find strings, grep for them in production files BEFORE backing up or editing. A zero-match grep is a stop signal, not a problem to engineer around.**
- **The grep output is itself the deliverable when the deliverable is "verify no regression."** The brief's STEP 1 explicitly asked for a diagnostic report; the user's phrasing ("Report findings before any edits") gave me an honest off-ramp. Producing a clean, table-formatted finding-by-finding report — including matching every src path against disk for the GoteFigure case — is more valuable than executing a fix that doesn't need executing.
- **Distinguishing production tree from archive tree in the grep output.** The first grep returned ~40 hits for the OLD quote — but every one was inside `_archive/_backup-*/`. Filtering with `--exclude-dir=_archive --exclude-dir=_v2_temp` on the cross-check grep made the production-vs-historical distinction unambiguous. This site has accumulated 25+ backup folders over 6 weeks; without the exclusion, any diagnostic grep is dominated by archive matches and reads as alarming noise. **Rule: every diagnostic grep on this codebase should default to `--exclude-dir=_archive --exclude-dir=_v2_temp`. Add `_backup` glob exclusions if archive folders ever land outside `_archive/`. Backups are evidence of past states, not current bugs.**
- **Three-branch root-cause proposal beats picking one.** Instead of guessing why the user thought there were regressions, listed three plausible explanations (cache/deploy, wrong working tree, memory/intent) and asked which applied. Each branch implies a different next action — redeploy, re-run diagnostic at a different path, or override-and-execute-anyway. The user is in the best position to identify which branch matches their context.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The brief was confident enough that I almost executed.** The phrasing "Apply all approved changes as targeted Edits" + "before tonight's case study trim work" + the granular find/replace strings read as a battle-tested punch list. The signal that something was off was subtle — the brief said "old quote on 4 files (lexisnexis, pulse, gotefigure, about), new on 2 (index, ghost)" and the actual state is "new on all 6." Specific factual claims like that are checkable in seconds with a grep, and were what triggered me to verify the premise rather than rolling forward. **Lesson: granular factual claims in a brief (specific filenames, specific counts, specific line numbers) are gifts to the diagnostic — they're cheap to falsify. Always falsify them before executing.**
- **The brief's verification loop was self-defeating.** STEP 4 said "Re-run all 4 diagnostic greps; confirm zero stale references." But STEP 1's diagnostic ALREADY shows zero stale references in production source. If STEP 1 is the same grep as STEP 4 and STEP 1 passes, then STEP 2 + STEP 3 are unnecessary. The brief's structure assumed STEP 1 would surface bugs; it didn't.
- **The brief's verification loop for GoteFigure had a false-positive bug.** The provided one-liner `for src in $(grep -oE 'src="[^"]+"' gotefigure.html | sed 's/src="//;s/"//'); do [ -f "$src" ] || echo "MISSING: $src"; done` matches ALL `src=` attributes including `<script src="../main.js?v=6">`. The `?v=6` cache-busting query string makes `[ -f ]` fail because there's no file literally named `main.js?v=6`. So the loop reports "MISSING: ../main.js?v=6" and "MISSING: ../game.js?v=3" even though both files exist. **If using this verification pattern in future, restrict to img only with `grep -E '<img[^>]+src="[^"]+"'` or strip query strings with `sed 's/?.*$//'`.**
- **Apr 21 rebrand entry already records "Swapped the footer quote on all 6 pages."** The learnings log itself shows the change landed across all 6 files — and the Apr 21 backup folder `_archive/_backup-rebrand-2026-04-21/` confirms it (every page in that backup already has the new quote, meaning even the pre-rebrand state was newer than the user's mental model). The learnings log was the authoritative source, not the brief. **Rule: when a brief lists a regression that contradicts a recent learnings entry, treat the learnings log as the prior and verify the source state directly before assuming the regression is real. Logged changes don't spontaneously revert.**

**What I learned:**
- **Why the user might have thought these were regressions.** The most likely explanation is **deploy/cache lag**. The Apr 21 rebrand committed but the live site at rotemgotlieb.com may not have rebuilt — or the user's browser is serving a cached version pre-rebrand. The fix is `git push` to trigger Netlify rebuild + hard-refresh, NOT re-editing source. Second-most-likely: the user reviewed an `_archive/_backup-*` HTML file directly instead of the production file, and the archive's snapshot of an older state read as "the live state." **Diagnostic next step if this happens again: ask which URL/path the user was viewing when they identified the bugs.** A screenshot would also disambiguate live-deploy-stale vs source-stale-on-disk.
- **The "no approval gate needed" autonomy fast-track is double-edged.** It cuts latency when the brief is correct; it amplifies cost when the brief is wrong. Diagnose-then-execute in two messages costs one round-trip; execute-on-faith and discover the brief was wrong costs the round-trip plus undo work plus learnings entry plus user trust. The asymmetry favors diagnose-first by default. **Rule: even when a brief says "execute immediately, no approval gate," run the cheapest diagnostic that would falsify the brief's premise BEFORE the first edit. If the diagnostic confirms, proceed at full speed; if it falsifies, stop and report. The diagnostic costs a few seconds and converts a "trust the brief" decision into "verify the brief."**
- **Permanent verification grep for this kind of audit on this codebase:**
  ```
  cd "/Users/rotemgotlieb/Desktop/claude portfolio march26"
  grep -rn "PHRASE_OR_FILENAME" --include="*.html" --exclude-dir=_archive --exclude-dir=_v2_temp .
  ```
  Always with the `--exclude-dir` flags; always against the canonical project root. This is the canonical "is X currently in production source" check.
- **`?v=N` cache-busting query strings break naive `[ -f ]` resolution checks.** When verifying that asset paths in HTML resolve, strip URL query strings before testing the filesystem: `path="${src%\?*}"; [ -f "$path" ]`. Or restrict the verification to `<img src>` only since image refs on this site don't carry cache-busting (only `<script>` tags do, and those are checked by browser load not by audit script).

**Files touched:** `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry — the only change made this session). Explicitly untouched: `index.html`, `about.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `styles.css`, `main.js`, `game.js`, all image/video assets, all archive folders. No backup folder created at `_archive/_backup-regression-fixes-2026-04-26/` because no files were modified — backing up unchanged files for a no-op session would pollute the archive index.

**Follow-up if the user reports the regressions are still visible on the live site after this report:** the issue is in the deployment pipeline, not the source tree. Suggested order of investigation: (1) confirm Netlify last-deploy timestamp is after 2026-04-21; (2) hard-refresh the browser (Cmd+Shift+R) on each affected page; (3) check the Netlify deploy log for the rebrand commit; (4) verify the Netlify-connected branch matches the local `main`. If all four pass and the regressions are still visible, then there's a publish-state bug somewhere in the build that warrants a separate diagnostic session.

## 2026-04-26 — LexisNexis case study trim (1199 → 800 words, 11 → 8 images)

**What I did:** Applied the user-provided verbatim rewrite of the LexisNexis case study body in [work/lexisnexis.html](work/lexisnexis.html), reducing the `<article class="case-body">` word count from 1199 to 800 (33% reduction; body-prose-only ≈724, ~40% reduction). Cut 3 images per the brief's image policy: prototype-02, prototype-03 (kept prototype-01 "wide layout view" as the strongest single Phase 2 image — it shows the full customizable workspace, which is exactly what the rewritten Phase 2 narrative sells), and tokens-03 (third Phase 3 image). Restructured the image containers to match the new image counts: Phase 2 collapsed from `.case-image-trio` (3-up grid) to a single `.case-image` div; Phase 3 collapsed from `.case-image-trio` to two stacked `.case-image` divs. Both new patterns mirror Phase 1's existing pattern of stacked single full-width `.case-image` blocks. Preserved every section ID, sidebar nav structure, h2/h3 hierarchy, class names, metadata grid, hero image, and outcome footer. Verified all 8 remaining image src paths resolve to real files on disk; sidebar nav still references all 6 section IDs (overview, the-problem, phase-1, phase-2, phase-3, outcome) plus #top; HTML tag balance is clean across section/article/div/p/h2/main/nav/aside/footer.

**What worked:**
- **Single-Edit atomic rewrite of the entire `<article>` block.** The brief noted "as a single Edit (or staged Edits if the file is too large)" — at ~125 lines of changed content the article block fit comfortably in one Edit call's old_string/new_string pair. One atomic write means zero risk of intermediate broken states or half-applied content. The Edit succeeded first try because I anchored on the full `<article class="case-body">` opening and closed at the matching `</section>` for the outcome block (just before Other Work, which was preserved verbatim and stayed outside the diff).
- **Diagnose-then-execute even under auto mode.** Auto mode said "skip approval gate, execute immediately," but the brief's STEP 1 still asked for a diagnostic report. Producing the word count + image inventory + image-existence check + image-keep recommendation BEFORE editing meant: (1) I caught that the brief's word-count estimate (~1500) was high — actual was 1199, so the trim ratio was less aggressive than implied; (2) I had a defensible "keep prototype-01" recommendation rooted in alt text + narrative fit before touching anything; (3) verification at STEP 4 had a clean baseline to compare against. **Rule: auto mode collapses the approval gate, not the diagnostic. Run the cheap pre-execution checks; just don't ask "should I proceed."**
- **Image structural decisions tied to the rewrite shape, not preserved blindly.** The brief said "Match the existing HTML structure (section tags, h2/h3 hierarchy, class names) — only the prose inside changes" but the image policy explicitly cuts images. Cutting images necessarily shifts container counts. The right read: preserve the *naming and class taxonomy* (`.case-image`, `.case-image-sm`, `.case-image-trio`), but pick whichever variant fits the post-cut child count. With 1 image in Phase 2, `.case-image-trio` (`grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr`) would render as one image in the left third with two empty columns — visually broken. With 2 images in Phase 3, same problem (one empty column). Switching to single `.case-image` divs that match Phase 1's existing pattern was the structurally honest answer. **Rule: when an image-policy change conflicts with the "preserve structure" instruction, the policy wins on element count and the structure-preservation applies to taxonomy/classes/IDs/section boundaries.**
- **Other Work block lives inside `<article class="case-body">` and was preserved verbatim.** Caught this because the diagnostic word-count regex (`<article>...</article>`) returned 800, but the body-prose-only count is ~724 — the difference is exactly the Other Work labels and titles. Knowing this distinction meant I could report both numbers and explain the gap, rather than letting the user wonder why "800" doesn't match their mental model of "case study body." **Rule: `<article class="case-body">` on this site is not just the case-study prose — it includes Other Work. Word-count diagnostics should report both "article-tag total" and "body-prose-only" to disambiguate.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **The user's verbatim copy itself came in at ~790 prose words, over the user's stated 550-700 target range.** Applied verbatim per brief instruction; the result inherits the source's word count. I flagged this in the verification report rather than (a) silently trimming further to hit target, or (b) silently shipping the overage. The decision tree for resolving this is the user's, not mine: accept the voice/length trade-off, prune within the rewrite, or drop the long Phase 1 "I was bad at this at first" paragraph specifically (it's ~109 words and the largest single chunk). **Rule: when a brief provides verbatim text + a target word count, count the verbatim before applying. If the verbatim already overshoots, surface the discrepancy in the proposal step rather than silently overshooting at execution time.** This session ran in auto mode so no proposal step existed — the discrepancy got surfaced at verification instead, which is acceptable but later than ideal.
- **The brief's image policy decision for Phase 2 ("recommend which based on alt text and prior learnings") had no prior-learnings signal.** Searching learnings for prototype-01/02/03 references returned nothing — these images haven't been re-exported, re-cropped, or repositioned in any prior session. So the call was made on alt text alone: prototype-01 ("wide layout view") wins because it most directly shows the customizable dashboard the new Phase 2 prose describes. prototype-02 ("component detail view") is a zoom-in that doesn't sell the customization story. prototype-03 ("scenario layout") shows one configuration, which is narrower than the full-workspace view of -01. **Rule: when "use prior learnings" comes up empty, fall back to "what does the current narrative most need to show?" and explain the rationale in the proposal/report so the user can override if their mental model differs.**
- **Phase 1's "I was bad at this at first" paragraph is 109 words — 13.7% of the entire body trim target.** The user marked this paragraph as part of the verbatim rewrite, so it's intentional voice (not bloat). But it's also the single biggest source of the word-count overshoot. If trimming further is wanted in a follow-up, this paragraph is the natural target. The voice asset it provides — self-effacing, specific, action-oriented learning narrative — is high-value for the senior-role hiring audience CLAUDE.md describes. **Trade-off note: cutting it would hit 550-700 target cleanly but at the cost of the case study's most distinctive personality moment.** Recommended preserving it and accepting the overage; user can decide.
- **Removed copy that wasn't in the rewrite:** the original "design infrastructure is leverage" thesis paragraph in Overview (line 102, `<strong>` emphasized), the "I saw three problems woven into one..." structural framing paragraph in The Problem (line 115), and the "What I'd do differently: ask for help sooner..." closing paragraph in Outcome (line 193). Each of these was load-bearing in the original — but the rewrite intentionally removed all three. Two reasons it works: (1) the leverage thesis is already the site-wide footer quote, so repeating it in the body was duplicative; (2) the help-asking thesis is preserved inside the new Phase 1 paragraph about the senior researcher, so the closing paragraph was redundant; (3) the "three problems woven" framing was meta-structural commentary that the new copy replaces with the action-oriented "I ran the user research that shaped the product roadmap, built the prototypes that validated it..." sentence in Overview. The rewrite knew what it was doing. **Rule: when applying a verbatim rewrite that drops a previously-load-bearing line, audit whether the same thesis appears elsewhere in the new copy or site shell before assuming it's a regression.**
- **The brief's verification one-liner false-positives on `<script src>` again.** Same `?v=N` cache-busting bug as last session: `[ -f "main.js?v=6" ]` fails because there's no file with that literal name, even though `main.js` exists. Carried forward as a known false-positive pattern. Not worth fixing the brief's verification snippet inline this session, but the next time I write a "verify all src paths" verification, default to `<img src>`-only or strip `?.*$` first.

**What I learned:**
- **33% word reduction with 27% image reduction is a healthy ratio for a "trim, don't restructure" pass.** Trimming words faster than images (or vice versa) tends to leave the page feeling unbalanced — too text-heavy or too image-heavy relative to its prior rhythm. This pass cut roughly proportionally: 11→8 images and 1199→800 words. Visual rhythm before/after: original had 11 images across 6 sections (heaviest in Phase 2 at 3 and Phase 3 at 3); new layout has 8 images across the same 6 sections (Phase 1 still 2, Phase 2 now 1, Phase 3 now 2, Outcome still 1, Overview still 1, plus hero). The biggest rhythm shift is Phase 2 dropping from a horizontal 3-up trio to a single full-width image — this changes the page's reading cadence at that point from "scan three thumbnails" to "land on one wide screenshot." Compatible with the trimmed text density there (Phase 2 went from 3 paragraphs to 2).
- **`.case-image-trio` is a 3-children-only component on this site.** Two children leaves an empty column; one child leaves two empty columns. Both look broken. **Rule: if cutting from a trio leaves <3 children, switch to single `.case-image` divs (potentially stacked). Don't try to make `.case-image-trio` work with non-3 counts.** Could be worth adding `.case-image-pair` (2-up grid) as a future taxonomy expansion if 2-image clusters become common — currently they're not common enough to justify the new class.
- **"Trim X% of words" is a more honest framing than "reach exactly N words" when applying a verbatim rewrite.** The user's brief framed the goal as "~600 words, range 550-700" but the source-of-truth verbatim copy itself was ~790 prose words. Either the user wanted me to further trim within the verbatim (which contradicts "apply verbatim"), or the target was aspirational/approximate (more likely). Reporting both numbers and naming the discrepancy lets the user clarify intent without me silently picking one interpretation.
- **Backup folder convention held:** `_archive/_backup-lexisnexis-trim-2026-04-26/lexisnexis.html` is the pre-trim snapshot. One file in the folder because only one file was modified — no need for a tree of unrelated files. Easy revert path: `cp _archive/_backup-lexisnexis-trim-2026-04-26/lexisnexis.html work/lexisnexis.html`.
- **Verification grep pattern for any future "structural trim" pass on a case study:**
  ```
  python3 -c "
  import re
  with open('work/<page>.html') as f: c = f.read()
  body = re.search(r'<article class=\"case-body\">(.*?)</article>', c, re.DOTALL).group(1)
  prose = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', body)
  print(len(re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', prose).strip().split()), 'words in <article>')
  "
  ```
  Run before and after the edit; report both numbers in the verification step. Keep this snippet handy.

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html` (single Edit replacing the entire `<article class="case-body">` content from the opening tag through the closing of the `outcome` section's last `<p>` — about 95 lines of HTML rewritten to ~73 lines; the Other Work `<section>` immediately after was preserved verbatim outside the diff; nav/sidebar/metadata/hero/footer all unchanged), `_archive/_backup-lexisnexis-trim-2026-04-26/lexisnexis.html` (pre-trim backup, full file copy), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `index.html`, `about.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `styles.css`, `main.js`, `game.js`, all image/video assets including the cut prototype-02.png, prototype-03.png, tokens-03.png (files remain on disk under `img/lexisnexis/` as orphans — not deleting because they may be referenced in `_archive/` snapshots or future re-introduction; orphan inventory is a separate cleanup pass), all other archive folders, `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Sidebar nav, scroll-progress bar color, theme toggle, all metadata grid values (Role, Timeline, Team, Tools), and the page's H1 (`Bridging Design and Engineering`) — unchanged.

**Follow-up if user wants to hit the 550-700 word target:** the natural cut is the Phase 1 "I was bad at this at first..." paragraph (109 words). Removing it brings body prose to ~615, comfortably mid-range. Trade-off: loses the case study's most distinctive personality moment. Alternatively, trim 50-100 words evenly across all 6 sections (less surgical, more diluting). Recommend: ask the user which they want, don't preemptively cut.

## 2026-04-26 — LexisNexis image layout fix + Image Layout Protocol established

**What I did:** Restored a 3-up small-grid row on [work/lexisnexis.html](work/lexisnexis.html) that had been silently dissolved during the earlier same-day trim session. Replaced three separate `<div class="case-image reveal">` full-width blocks (prototype-01.png, figma-variables.png, color-palette.png) with a single `<div class="case-image-trio reveal">` containing three `.case-image-sm` children. The trio sits as a sibling between phase-2's `</section>` and phase-3's `<section>` — the structurally correct location given that this entire page uses the convention "image divs sit between sections as siblings, never inside `<section>` tags." All three alt strings preserved verbatim from the original `<img>` tags. Phase 3 prose preserved verbatim (was part of the Edit's anchor span, not modified). Net structural delta: -3 single `.case-image` divs, +1 `.case-image-trio` div with 3 `.case-image-sm` children.
