## 2026-04-26 — Resume modal fixes: PDF fit, modal sizing, custom-cursor z-index

**What I did:** Three targeted fixes on the resume modal shipped earlier today. (1) Layered the iframe hash params from `#view=FitH` to `#view=Fit&zoom=page-fit&toolbar=1` on all 3 modal-equipped pages (index/about/process) so the PDF fits to the modal box vertically AND horizontally instead of just fitting to width and overflowing the viewport. (2) Enlarged `.resume-modal-content` from 92vw / 900px / 92vh to 95vw / 1100px / 95vh so the PDF has more room at its natural fit-to-page zoom (browser PDF viewers manage their own zoom internally — there is no iframe API to control it, so the only lever we have is making the modal bigger). (3) Bumped `.custom-cursor` z-index from 9999 to 10005 ([styles.css:330](styles.css#L330)) so the custom cursor renders above the resume modal (z-index 10000) and any other current overlay (intro overlay 10000, two existing 10001 layers). Backup at `_archive/_backup-resume-modal-fixes-2026-04-26/` containing 4 pre-edit files (styles.css, index.html, about.html, process.html — main.js untouched). Cache-bust bumped on the 3 changed pages from `?v=11` to `?v=12` for both styles.css and main.js (main.js bumped for symmetry with styles.css even though main.js is unchanged this session — keeps both refs synced per page); 404 + 4 case studies stay at `?v=11` since they reference no changed CSS selectors. This re-introduces minor cache-bust drift (12 vs 11 across 8 pages) one day after the prior session's unification pass — see "What I learned" point 3 below.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic-first STEP 1 collapsed the cursor bug in seconds.** Two greps in parallel revealed `z-index: 9999` on `.custom-cursor` and `z-index: 10000` on `.resume-modal` — the bug WAS the z-index ordering, not anything subtle about the cursor system. No need to read the cursor's JS init code at all (though I did, briefly, to confirm the cursor is purely visual with `pointer-events: none`). **Rule confirmed: when a UI element "disappears" inside another UI element, audit z-index FIRST. The bug is z-index 80% of the time. Don't read JS until CSS layering has been ruled out.**
- **Option (a) "bump cursor z-index" beat options (b)/(c) on every dimension.** (b) "disable cursor while modal open" requires modal-aware logic in the cursor system (coupling) plus an open/close toggle hook (more state). (c) "restore native cursor on modal interactive elements" loses the site's signature interaction inside the modal AND requires per-element `cursor:` declarations. (a) is one CSS line, zero coupling, preserves cursor everywhere. **Rule: when a global UI overlay (like a custom cursor or overlay layer) needs to render above a specific other layer, bumping z-index is almost always the cleanest fix vs. coupling the two systems with state-aware enable/disable.**
- **Headroom (10005 not 10001) is intentional and now codified.** The bump from 9999 to 10005 leaves room for 4 future overlay layers (10001, 10002, 10003, 10004) to land between the resume modal (10000) and the cursor without forcing a cursor re-bump. Existing 10001 layers (intro overlay variant + a skip-link) already exist; the cursor at 10005 sits cleanly above them all. **Rule: when bumping z-index of a "should always be on top" element above a discovered competitor, leave 4-5 levels of headroom for future overlays. Tight bumps (10001 above 10000) mean the next overlay forces a tandem re-bump.**
- **Layered hash params is a strict superset, not a replacement.** `#view=Fit&zoom=page-fit&toolbar=1` — Chrome/Edge honor `#view=Fit` (whole page visible). Firefox honors `&zoom=page-fit`. Safari is the most stubborn: it ignores most PDF hash parameters and uses its own toolbar. `&toolbar=1` keeps the PDF toolbar visible in browsers that respect the hide/show param (Chrome) so the user can manually zoom. Worst-case (Safari): user gets the same default behavior they'd have gotten with `#view=FitH`. Best-case (Chrome/Firefox): page fits to box on initial load, no scrolling needed. **No regression possible — the layered params are additive.**
- **Backup-before-modify worked exactly as the rule prescribes.** 4 files copied to `_archive/_backup-resume-modal-fixes-2026-04-26/` BEFORE any Edit. If any edit had been wrong, I had a one-command rollback available. **Rule confirmed: always create the backup folder + copy ALL files-to-be-modified before the first Edit, even for "small" 3-issue sessions. The cost is one mkdir + one cp; the value is zero-anxiety experimentation.**
- **HTTP-served verification proved CSS reaches the browser, not just the file.** `curl -s "http://localhost:8080/styles.css?v=12" | grep "z-index: 10005"` returned the new line — proof that the dev server is serving the updated file at the new cache-bust URL. **Rule: for any cache-bust bump, always verify the served file (HTTP) contains the new content, not just the file-on-disk. Cache-bust bugs are sneaky — the file can be correct but the HTML can reference an old `?v=` value.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Edit calls on 3 HTML files needed Read first; they failed without it.** I dispatched 5 Edit calls in parallel (2 for styles.css + 3 for the HTML files). The 2 styles.css Edits succeeded because styles.css had been Read in STEP 1 diagnostic. The 3 HTML Edits failed with "File has not been read yet" because I'd only `grep`'d those files, not Read them. **Lesson:** grep doesn't satisfy the Read-before-Edit requirement. Even brief Reads (10 lines around the target) are required before Edit. **Rule: before any Edit batch, ensure every target file has been Read in the same session — use parallel Read calls if needed. A grep-only diagnostic in STEP 1 is fine for understanding, but does NOT unlock Edit on those files.**
- **`replace_all` on `?v=11` was bounded only because I'd manually checked the audit beforehand.** I used `replace_all: true` to bump `?v=11 → ?v=12` on the 3 HTML files, but `?v=11` could plausibly have appeared in inline data attributes, comments, or other unrelated places. It didn't (post-edit audit confirmed only styles.css and main.js refs were touched, and game.js?v=3 was correctly untouched). **Rule: `replace_all` on a `?v=` cache-bust value is safe IF you've grep'd for `?v=` first and confirmed the matches are all the expected `<link>` and `<script>` tags. Always do the audit grep AFTER the replace_all to confirm no collateral damage.**

**What I learned:**
- **Custom-cursor z-index standing rule (codified):** the custom cursor is currently at `z-index: 10005` ([styles.css:330](styles.css#L330)). When introducing ANY new overlay or modal to this site (toast, tooltip, full-screen image viewer, etc.), the new element's z-index MUST stay below 10005, OR the cursor MUST be bumped in tandem. The rule is: **the custom cursor must always render above any z-indexed UI on this site that could host interactive content.** The cursor disappearing over interactive UI is exactly the bug fixed today; preventing recurrence requires this invariant. Practical workflow: when adding a new overlay, grep `styles.css` for `z-index: 1000` to see the current top of the stack; pick a value at or below 10004; if you must go higher, bump the cursor too. Currently known z-indexes ≥1000 on this site: `.intro-overlay` 10000, two layers at 10001, `.resume-modal` 10000, `.custom-cursor` 10005.
- **PDF iframe zoom hash parameters — canonical pattern (codified):** any future PDF embed on this site MUST use `#view=Fit&zoom=page-fit&toolbar=1` as the canonical hash. Browser support reasoning:
  - **Chrome/Edge** (Chromium-based): honor `#view=Fit` (entire page visible at once). Also honor `&toolbar=0` to hide their toolbar, so explicit `&toolbar=1` is the safe default.
  - **Firefox** (PDF.js): does NOT honor `#view=Fit` reliably. DOES honor `&zoom=page-fit` (PDF.js-specific param). Layering both means Firefox respects the second param.
  - **Safari**: largely ignores PDF hash parameters and uses its own viewer behavior. Falls back to default rendering — which is roughly equivalent to `#view=FitH` (fit width, scroll vertically) anyway. No regression.
  - **Mobile**: irrelevant — the site has a `@media (max-width: 767px)` mobile fallback that hides the iframe entirely and shows Download + Open-in-new-tab buttons.
  Layering covers the most cases. The pattern is additive (no parameter overrides another), so adding more browsers to the support matrix later is a no-risk extension.
- **"Modal size matters because we can't control PDF viewer zoom" insight (codified):** browser PDF viewers (Chrome's pdfium, Firefox's PDF.js, Safari's PDFKit) all manage their own zoom level internally, and there is NO iframe-side JavaScript API to read or set that zoom from the parent page. The `#view=` and `&zoom=` hash params are the ONLY external lever, and they only fire on initial load (changing them later doesn't re-zoom). This means the only way to make a PDF look bigger inside an embedded iframe is to make the iframe (and its container) bigger. **Rule for any future PDF-in-modal feature on this site: the modal's max-width and max-height should be as large as the design tolerates. Don't spend time trying to JavaScript-control the PDF zoom — that lever doesn't exist.**
- **Cache-bust drift is a feature, not a bug (codified).** Yesterday's session unified all 8 HTML files to `?v=11`. Today's session bumped 3 files to `?v=12` (because only those 3 reference changed CSS selectors / changed iframe HTML). The drift comes back the moment we touch fewer than all pages — and that's correct, because the per-page cache-bust value should reflect the page's actual content state. Bumping unchanged pages just to maintain visual uniformity is busywork that invalidates browser caches for no functional reason. **Rule: cache-bust unification is for periodic cleanup ("when drift exceeds 3+ values, do a unify pass"), NOT a permanent invariant. Per-page drift after a single-session multi-page edit is expected and correct. The unify pass is for when accumulated drift across many sessions becomes hard to audit, not for after every session.**
- **Diagnostic-first STEP 1 saved a wrong fix.** If I'd jumped straight to "fix cursor disappearing in modal" without diagnosing, I'd likely have proposed option (b) "disable cursor when modal open" — which sounds reasonable but couples two unrelated systems. The 30-second diagnostic (two greps, look at z-indexes) made option (a) obvious and trivial. **Rule confirmed for the Nth time: STEP 1 diagnostic is non-skippable. The value of diagnosing-first is that the right fix often becomes self-evident from the data, AND surfaces options the brief didn't enumerate.**

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (2 Edits: cursor z-index 9999→10005 at line 330 + modal-content sizing 92vw/900px/92vh → 95vw/1100px/95vh at lines 1612-1614; total file unchanged at 1718 lines), `index.html` (2 Edits: iframe hash + cache-bust styles+main `?v=11→?v=12` via replace_all), `about.html` (2 Edits: same), `process.html` (2 Edits: same), `_archive/_backup-resume-modal-fixes-2026-04-26/` (NEW backup folder containing 4 pre-edit files: styles.css, index.html, about.html, process.html), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `main.js` (cursor system is purely CSS-controlled for the z-index fix; modal handler is unchanged), `game.js`, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `404.html`, all 4 case study HTML files in `work/`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all image/video assets, all other `_archive/_backup-*` folders, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Image Layout Protocol respected — no images touched).

**Cache-bust state after this session (drift re-introduced, deliberately):** index.html, about.html, process.html → `styles.css?v=12` and `main.js?v=12`. 404.html, work/lexisnexis.html, work/pulse.html, work/ghost.html, work/gotefigure.html → still at `styles.css?v=11` and `main.js?v=11`. game.js stays at `?v=3` everywhere. Drift is 2 values across 8 pages — within the acceptable threshold (under 3 distinct values). Next unify pass not needed yet.

**Verified mechanically (this session):**
- All 3 iframe `data-resume-iframe-src` attributes now read `/Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf#view=Fit&zoom=page-fit&toolbar=1` (grep confirmed).
- `.resume-modal-content` sizing reads 95vw / 1100px / 95vh (sed confirmed).
- `.custom-cursor` z-index reads 10005 (sed confirmed).
- Currently top z-indexes on the site: 10000 (intro overlay + resume modal), 10001 (two existing layers). Cursor at 10005 sits above all.
- All 6 HTTP endpoints return 200 (`/`, `/about.html`, `/process.html`, `/styles.css?v=12`, `/main.js?v=12`, `/Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`).
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=12 contains the new cursor z-index AND new modal sizing (curl + grep confirmed — the new CSS reaches the browser, not just the file).
- Cache-bust on the 3 changed pages reads `?v=12`; game.js correctly stays at `?v=3` on those pages.
- Backup folder contains all 4 pre-edit files (`ls` confirmed).

**Verified at deploy / requires browser (handed to user):**
- PDF fills the modal frame at default zoom (no large empty vertical space inside the modal).
- Modal feels appropriately large (closer to filling the screen, not tiny inside a large viewport).
- Custom cursor is visible over the X close button and the Download PDF button on desktop.
- All other modal behavior unchanged: open on trigger click, close on ESC, close on backdrop click, close on X button click, body scroll lock while open, focus management, mobile fallback at ≤767px.
- Dark mode: no regression on cursor or modal appearance.
- Cross-browser PDF rendering: Chrome/Edge fits to page; Firefox fits to page (via `&zoom=page-fit`); Safari falls back to default behavior.

**Follow-up if user wants to fully close the loop:**
1. Browser-test all 3 modal-equipped pages at desktop viewport across Chrome, Firefox, Safari to confirm the layered hash params actually fit-to-page in each browser.
2. Lazy-iframe pattern remains available for any future heavy-content modal on this site (image lightbox, video preview, embed). Reuse the `data-*-src` data-attribute carrier pattern for consistency.
3. The 3 heavier LexisNexis PNGs (overview-v2 1964 KB, research-insight 1281 KB, tag-taxonomy 932 KB) remain candidates for `oxipng` install or JPG q92 escalation.
4. Standing custom-cursor z-index invariant now codified: when introducing any new overlay z-index, verify it stays below 10005, OR bump the cursor in tandem.

## 2026-04-26 — Resume modal: PDF.js viewer (replaces iframe)

**What I did:** Swapped the `<iframe class="resume-modal-iframe">` PDF embed inside the resume modal for a [PDF.js](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/) v3.11.174 canvas-based viewer (loaded lazily from cdnjs on first modal open). Added a custom toolbar with prev/next page (auto-hidden for single-page PDFs) and zoom in/out buttons. Replaced `.resume-modal-iframe` CSS with `.resume-modal-pdfjs-container` + `.resume-modal-pdfjs-canvas` + `.resume-modal-pdfjs-toolbar` + `.resume-modal-pdfjs-btn` + helpers (~70 lines appended/replaced in [styles.css](styles.css)). Replaced the entire 52-line modal handler block in [main.js:449-500](main.js#L449-L500) with a ~140-line combined block that wires up the same modal lifecycle (open/close/ESC/backdrop) PLUS PDF.js init: dynamic script injection, `getDocument` → `getPage(1)` → `calcFitToWidthScale` → render at min scale; zoom controls clamped to `[fitToWidth, 2*fitToWidth]` with `0.25*fitToWidth` step; window-resize re-render at min scale (RAF-debounced); graceful degradation to the existing mobile-fallback panel on PDF.js load/init failure. Updated mobile media query selectors from `.resume-modal-iframe { display: none }` to `.resume-modal-pdfjs-container, .resume-modal-pdfjs-toolbar { display: none }`. Replaced the `<iframe>` line on all 3 modal-equipped pages ([index.html:261](index.html#L261), [about.html:241](about.html#L241), [process.html:172](process.html#L172)) with the new container + canvas + toolbar markup. Cache-bust on the 3 pages bumped `?v=12 → ?v=13` for both styles.css and main.js. Backup at `_archive/_backup-resume-modal-pdfjs-2026-04-26/` containing 5 pre-edit files.

**What worked:**
- **Replacing the entire 52-line modal block as one unit was cleaner than bolting PDF.js onto a sibling block.** Both blocks would have shared `resumeModal`, `resumeLastFocused`, and the open/close lifecycle — splitting them would have meant duplicate `if (resumeModal)` guards and racy initialization. The new combined block keeps all modal-related state in one closure, with PDF.js init triggered by `openResumeModal` after a `requestAnimationFrame` defer (so the container has its final layout width before fit-to-width is calculated). **Rule: when extending an existing feature with a new responsibility that needs the same lifecycle hooks, replace the existing block as one unit. Don't append a "PDF.js wiring" sibling block that duplicates the open/close handler — duplication is the bug magnet.**
- **Lazy-load via dynamic `<script>` injection + Promise singleton.** Pattern: `loadPdfJs()` returns a memoized Promise. First call appends the `<script>` to head, sets `script.async = true`, resolves on `onload` (after also setting `pdfjsLib.GlobalWorkerOptions.workerSrc`), rejects on `onerror`. Subsequent calls return the cached Promise (or `Promise.resolve()` if already loaded). This means: (a) library is fetched once even if user opens/closes the modal repeatedly; (b) library is fetched ONLY on first modal open (zero pageload cost); (c) failure mode is captured cleanly via `.catch()` chain. **Promote as a permanent pattern: any heavy third-party JS on this site MUST use the lazy-script-inject + Promise-singleton pattern. Never inline a third-party `<script>` tag that fires on pageload unless it's font-shop-required (Fontshare).**
- **`requestAnimationFrame` defer before `initPdfViewer()` was load-bearing.** First implementation called `initPdfViewer()` synchronously inside `openResumeModal`. This raced the modal's `aria-hidden` toggle: the modal was still `display: none` when `pdfContainer.clientWidth` was read, returning 0, which gave `pdfFitToWidthScale = NaN`. Wrapping the init call in `requestAnimationFrame` lets the browser apply the `aria-hidden="false"` style and lay out the modal before the width is read. **Rule: any code that reads layout dimensions of an element that just changed visibility (display none→flex, opacity 0→1 with reflow) MUST defer to the next animation frame. The order is: toggle visibility → next frame → read dimensions.**
- **Devicepixelratio-aware canvas rendering for crisp Retina output.** Standard PDF.js pattern: `canvas.width = viewport.width * dpr` (backing buffer), `canvas.style.width = viewport.width + 'px'` (CSS dimension), `ctx.setTransform(dpr, 0, 0, dpr, 0, 0)` before render. Without DPR scaling, the resume looks blurry on Retina displays at default zoom. With DPR scaling, the resume is pixel-crisp regardless of display density. **Rule for any future canvas-based render on this site: ALWAYS multiply backing buffer by `window.devicePixelRatio || 1` and apply `setTransform(dpr,...)` before render. Set CSS dimensions to the unscaled values. The 3-line cost buys Retina sharpness for everyone on macOS / iPad / modern Windows.**
- **Toolbar button enable/disable at zoom bounds is the clearest UX signal for "this is the minimum zoom."** Initial render = min zoom = zoom-out button is disabled (visibly grayed out via `opacity: 0.4` and `cursor: not-allowed`). User can't accidentally try to zoom out and wonder why nothing happens. Same pattern at max zoom (zoom-in disabled). The disabled state itself communicates the constraint better than any tooltip or message would. **Rule: when a UI control has a hard limit (zoom bound, list start/end, page first/last), the disabled state at that limit is the correct affordance. Don't hide the button (loses position consistency) and don't let it stay enabled but no-op (frustrating). Disable + visibly gray out.**
- **Floating-point comparison guards (`+0.001` / `-0.001`) for zoom bound disabling.** Without the epsilon: floating-point drift after multiple `+= ZOOM_STEP * fitToWidthScale` operations means `currentScale` can land at e.g. `1.0000000001 * fitToWidthScale` when it should be exactly `fitToWidthScale`. The `<= minScale + 0.001` comparison catches this drift cleanly. **Rule: for any float-bound comparison in JS (zoom limits, scroll positions, animation completion, scale matrices), always use an epsilon guard. Strict `==` or `<=` against accumulated float math is a bug magnet.**
- **Verifying the cdnjs URLs return 200 BEFORE finishing the session caught a potential silent failure.** `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -L https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/pdf.js/3.11.174/pdf.min.js` returned 200 for both lib and worker — confirming the CDN URL pattern is correct and the version exists. If a future session bumps the version, this same curl should be the first verification step. **Rule: when adding a new external CDN dependency, the smoke test MUST include a `curl` against both the lib AND worker (or any sub-resources) URLs. Don't trust the version string from documentation — verify it's actually present at the CDN.**
- **Re-using the existing mobile-fallback panel as the failure-mode UI was a reach-for-existing-DOM win.** The PDF.js init failure path calls `showPdfFallback()` which sets `display: none` on the canvas/toolbar and `display: flex` on `.resume-modal-mobile-fallback`. Same DOM element that the mobile media query uses, just toggled via inline style on desktop instead of media query. Zero new HTML or CSS needed for the failure UI. **Rule: when designing a graceful-degradation path for a desktop feature, audit whether existing mobile-fallback DOM can be reused. The fallback content is usually identical (download the resource, open in new tab) — duplicating the markup is wasteful.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **`grep -c` returns nonzero exit when match count is 0, which broke a chained `&&` audit.** I'd written `grep -c "iframe-stale-class" file && echo "..." && grep -c "new-class" file && ...` expecting all three greps to print their counts. The first grep returned 0 matches → exit code 1 → `&&` short-circuited the entire chain. Fix: separate the audits with `;` not `&&`, OR append `|| true` after each grep that's expected to potentially return 0. **Rule: in shell audits where "match count = 0" is a valid PASS condition (e.g., "no leftover stale references"), use `;` separators or `|| true` guards. `grep -c ... && next-command` only runs the next command if matches were found — exactly the inverse of the intent for a "should be zero" assertion.**
- **PDF.js v4.x requires native ES module loading; v3.x doesn't.** I almost defaulted to "latest stable" (v4.x) before remembering that the lazy-script-inject pattern only works with UMD/IIFE builds. v4.x is shipped as ES modules first; using it would have required either `<script type="module">` (which has different caching/loading semantics and doesn't expose the global `pdfjsLib`) or a UMD shim. v3.11.174 is the last v3.x release and exposes `window.pdfjsLib` cleanly. **Rule: when picking a third-party library version, explicitly check whether the version's distribution model is UMD/IIFE (works with `<script src=...>`) or ESM (requires `<script type="module">` + import). On this vanilla-JS site, ALWAYS pick UMD versions; ESM-only libraries require non-trivial integration that defeats the lazy-script-inject pattern.**
- **The `<iframe>` selector cleanup needed careful attention to the mobile media query.** First instinct was "just delete `.resume-modal-iframe` rule." But the mobile media query also referenced `.resume-modal-iframe { display: none }` — leaving that line orphaned would create CSS that targets a non-existent class (harmless but stale). Caught it during the second Edit pass and replaced it with `.resume-modal-pdfjs-container, .resume-modal-pdfjs-toolbar { display: none }`. **Rule: when removing a CSS class entirely, grep for ALL references first (selector AND any parent media-query references), not just the primary rule definition. A media-query reference to a now-deleted selector is a stale-CSS smell that compounds.**

**What I learned:**
- **PDF.js as the canonical PDF viewer pattern for this site (codified).** Any future PDF embed on this site MUST use the PDF.js viewer pattern, not `<iframe src="*.pdf">`. The iframe approach has unsolvable problems: (a) Chrome ignores hash params for iframe-embedded PDFs (cannot fit-to-page), (b) browser PDF viewers manage their own zoom internally with no parent-side API to read or constrain, (c) Mobile browsers render PDFs in iframes inconsistently or not at all, (d) viewer chrome (toolbar, sidebar) varies wildly by browser and can't be styled. PDF.js solves all four: canvas-based render gives full control over fit, zoom is JS-controlled with hard min/max bounds, mobile path falls back to native download (which is the right UX anyway), and the toolbar is custom-styled to match the site. **Trade-off: ~300KB of JS on first modal open (lazy-loaded, cached after) and an external CDN dependency (cdnjs). Both are acceptable for the win in viewer control.**
- **External CDN dependencies are no longer zero-count on this site (codified tradeoff).** Pre-this-session: only Fontshare (fonts). Post-this-session: Fontshare + cdnjs (PDF.js). The "vanilla, zero external runtime deps" framing is broken at this point. The graceful-fallback pattern (`.catch()` → show download buttons) makes the SPOF concern acceptable for the resume modal, BUT every additional external dep compounds the surface area for "the user has no internet / the CDN is down" failure modes. **Standing rule: if a future feature needs another external runtime JS dependency (3rd, 4th, etc.), that's a decision moment to consider self-hosting (vendoring the lib into `/vendor/` or similar) instead. The threshold for "self-host is worth the maintenance burden" gets lower as the dep count rises. Currently at 2 (Fontshare + cdnjs); flag if a 3rd is proposed.**
- **Lazy-load pattern for heavy third-party JS, codified.** Pattern: `loadLibrary()` returns a memoized Promise. First call appends `<script>` (with `async = true`) to head, resolves on `onload` (after any post-load setup like `workerSrc`), rejects on `onerror`. Subsequent calls return the cached Promise. The trigger is the user action that needs the library (modal open, button click, scroll-into-view) — never pageload. This pattern is now used twice on this site: (a) the lazy-iframe-src for the original resume modal (since retired), (b) the lazy PDF.js script load (this session). The pattern generalizes to any heavy resource: video player libraries, charting libraries, animation libraries, etc. **Promote as a permanent pattern: every third-party library on this site (current + future) MUST be lazy-loaded on the user action that needs it, never on pageload. Fontshare is the only exception (fonts are needed before any rendering).**
- **"Default state IS the minimum" zoom UX, codified.** Most PDF viewers initialize at "fit to width" or "fit to page" then ALLOW the user to zoom out further (showing whitespace around the PDF), which is rarely useful and adds a "weird empty state" failure mode. The choice here: initial render fits to width, AND the zoom-out button is disabled at that level (because there's no useful zoom-out below "fit"). This makes the affordance immediately clear: "you can zoom IN from here, but you can't zoom OUT." The disabled zoom-out button at initial state is the visual proof of this constraint. **Rule for any future viewer with bounded zoom: pick the natural fit level as both the initial render AND the minimum zoom. Disable the zoom-out button at initial load. Don't silently allow over-zoom-out — that's an affordance that confuses more than it helps.**
- **CDN choice: cdnjs.cloudflare.com matches CLAUDE.md library convention.** Per the .claude/CLAUDE.md tech stack notes, this site loads external resources from CDN providers — Fontshare for fonts, now cdnjs for PDF.js. cdnjs is preferred over jsdelivr / unpkg / others because: (a) Cloudflare's edge network has the best global latency, (b) cdnjs URL format is stable and version-pinned (no fuzzy "latest" resolution), (c) cdnjs has no rate-limiting at typical site traffic levels. **Rule: any future external JS library on this site MUST be loaded from cdnjs.cloudflare.com unless there's a documented reason to prefer another provider (e.g., the lib is only published on jsdelivr). Pin to an exact version (e.g., 3.11.174), never use `/latest/`.**

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (2 Edits: replace `.resume-modal-body` + `.resume-modal-iframe` with `.resume-modal-body` updated + 7 new pdfjs selectors at lines 1667-1737; update mobile media query at line 1775 from `.resume-modal-iframe` to `.resume-modal-pdfjs-container, .resume-modal-pdfjs-toolbar`; net file size ~1718 → ~1782 lines), `main.js` (1 Edit: replace lines 449-500 with new ~140-line combined modal+PDF.js block; net ~503 → ~593 lines), `index.html` (2 Edits: replace iframe line with 14-line container+canvas+toolbar markup + cache-bust `?v=12→?v=13`), `about.html` (2 Edits: same), `process.html` (2 Edits: same), `_archive/_backup-resume-modal-pdfjs-2026-04-26/` (NEW backup folder with 5 pre-edit files: styles.css, main.js, index.html, about.html, process.html), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `game.js`, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf` (file content unchanged), `404.html`, all 4 case study HTML files in `work/` (no modal markup, no need for cache-bust bump), `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all image/video assets, all other `_archive/_backup-*` folders, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Image Layout Protocol respected — no images touched).

**Cache-bust state after this session (drift continues, deliberately):** index.html, about.html, process.html → `styles.css?v=13` and `main.js?v=13`. 404.html, work/lexisnexis.html, work/pulse.html, work/ghost.html, work/gotefigure.html → `styles.css?v=11` and `main.js?v=11`. game.js stays at `?v=3` everywhere. Drift is 2 distinct values across 8 pages — within the 3-distinct-values threshold; no unify needed yet.

**Verified mechanically (this session):**
- All 5 references to `resume-modal-iframe` and `data-resume-iframe-src` have been removed across all source files (grep returns 0 in HTML, CSS, JS).
- New `.resume-modal-pdfjs-canvas` markup present exactly once on each of index.html / about.html / process.html.
- Cache-bust on the 3 changed pages reads `?v=13` for styles.css and main.js; `game.js?v=3` correctly untouched.
- Other 5 pages (404 + 4 case studies) still on `?v=11` — expected, no leak.
- All 6 HTTP endpoints return 200 (`/`, `/about.html`, `/process.html`, `/styles.css?v=13`, `/main.js?v=13`, `/Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`).
- cdnjs URLs return 200 for both `pdf.min.js` and `pdf.worker.min.js` at version 3.11.174 (verified with `curl -L`).
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=13 contains 8 PDF.js-related selectors (curl + grep confirmed).
- HTTP-served main.js?v=13 contains 12 PDF.js-related references (curl + grep confirmed).
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=13 contains ZERO `.resume-modal-iframe` references (clean removal confirmed).
- main.js syntax valid (`node --check` passes).
- Backup folder contains all 5 pre-edit files (`ls` confirmed).

**Verified at deploy / requires browser (handed to user):**
- Modal opens; PDF.js loads from cdnjs (~300KB); resume renders at fit-to-width within ~300-500ms.
- PDF visibly fills the modal frame at default zoom (no large empty vertical or horizontal space).
- Zoom-out button is visibly grayed out (disabled) at initial render.
- Zoom-in works: each click increases scale by 25% of fit-to-width. Reaches 2x of fit-to-width at the 4th click; zoom-in button then disables.
- After zooming in, zoom-out works back down to fit-to-width and stops there (zoom-out re-disables at minimum).
- All 3 modal-equipped pages (index, about, process) work identically.
- Mobile fallback (≤767px viewport): PDF.js viewer hidden via CSS, download/open buttons shown. PDF.js library is NOT fetched on mobile (saves ~300KB).
- PDF.js failure path: if cdnjs is blocked or down, `.catch()` shows the same fallback panel.
- No console errors.
- Resume content fully readable at default zoom.
- Window resize while modal open: re-renders at new fit-to-width (RAF-debounced).
- Custom cursor (z-index 10005 from prior session) remains visible above the modal and toolbar buttons.

**Follow-up if user wants to fully close the loop:**
1. Browser-test the modal at desktop and mobile viewports across Chrome, Firefox, Safari to confirm PDF.js renders consistently and zoom controls behave identically.
2. Consider self-hosting PDF.js (`/vendor/pdf.js/3.11.174/`) if a 3rd external runtime dependency gets added in a future session — see "External CDN dependencies are no longer zero-count" learning above.
3. Lazy-script-inject pattern is now codified for any future heavy third-party JS (chart library, video player, animation library, etc.). Reuse the `loadLibrary()` Promise-singleton pattern from this session's main.js.
4. The 3 heavier LexisNexis PNGs (overview-v2 1964 KB, research-insight 1281 KB, tag-taxonomy 932 KB) remain candidates for `oxipng` install or JPG q92 escalation if further perf is desired.
5. Standing custom-cursor z-index invariant (≥10005) and the iframe-zoom-control limitation (no parent-side API, swap to PDF.js) are now codified rules — future sessions should respect both.

## 2026-04-27 — Mobile fixes batch (6 issues, 1 deferred)

**What I did:** Batched mobile-quality session covering 6 issues across all 8 HTML pages + styles.css + main.js. (1) Resume modal goes full-screen on mobile with PDF.js viewer enabled (was: hidden + fallback panel); deleted `isMobileViewport()` function and its guard; added a universal "Loading…" indicator that injects into `.resume-modal-pdfjs-container` on init start and removes on first render complete or failure. Sticky header (top) and toolbar (bottom) on mobile with 44px close-button touch target. (2) Standardized all 4 bento cards to `aspect-ratio: 16/9` on mobile (was: a=16/10, b=3/2, c=16/10, d=4/3 — all different, all causing thumbnail crops on Pulse and Ghost specifically). (3) Reusable `.image-modal` component shipped on all 4 case study pages; `.expandable` class applied to all 4 hero `<img>` elements; mobile-only click handler (gated by `matchMedia('(max-width: 767px)')` at handler-bind time); native pinch-zoom via `touch-action: pinch-zoom` on the modal image. Verified all 4 case studies' viewport meta tags allow zoom (no `user-scalable=no` or `maximum-scale=1`). (4) `.case-image-trio` mobile rule changed from `grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 10px;` to `1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 6px;` so the 3-up trio persists at mobile (was: collapsed to single column, breaking the original 3-up design intent on lex+gote case studies). (5) Issue 7 (standalone `.case-image-sm` mobile cap) deferred — no standalone usage exists yet, all `.case-image-sm` are trio children whose width is set by the grid. Issue 6's fix fully satisfies the small-image-stays-small intent for current usage. Cache-bust bumped to `?v=14` on the 7 changed pages (3 modal-equipped + 4 case studies); 404 stays at `?v=11`. Backup at `_archive/_backup-mobile-fixes-2026-04-27/` containing 9 pre-edit files (5 root + 4 case studies). Session note from brief: nav order kept Work → Process → About → Contact (Issue 1 explicitly skipped per user).

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic-then-batch-execute saved a lot of context.** Instead of opening files repeatedly across 6 issues, did one parallel diagnostic pass at session start (3 grep calls + 4 strategic Reads), then presented one consolidated proposal, got one approval, then executed every Edit. The proposal phase surfaced the Issue 7 deferral BEFORE writing any unused CSS — caught that the proposed `max-width: 60%` rule on `.case-image-sm` would conflict with trio grid children (33vw < 60vw cap, so no effect; OR worse, would override trio grid width if min-width interaction kicked in). **Rule confirmed: for batched multi-issue sessions, always run all diagnostics first, then present consolidated proposal. Catches cross-issue conflicts (Issue 6 fix + Issue 7 proposal contradicted each other) before any Edit lands.**
- **Standardizing all 4 bento mobile aspect-ratios with a grouped selector beats 4 separate rules.** Instead of `.bento-card-a .bento-card-thumb { aspect-ratio: 16/9 }` × 4, used a comma-grouped selector `.bento-card-a .bento-card-thumb, .bento-card-b ..., .bento-card-c ..., .bento-card-d .bento-card-thumb { aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; }`. Also signals intent: "these 4 cards share a single aspect-ratio invariant." If a future card divergence is needed, it requires adding a NEW rule — making the divergence visible. **Rule: when N selectors share an identical property value AND that value is conceptually a shared invariant (not coincidence), use a grouped selector. The grouping IS the documentation.**
- **Universal loading indicator (not mobile-only) was the right call.** Brief proposed mobile-only loading indicator since "mobile users feel the latency more." But desktop users on slow connections experience the same ~300-500ms PDF.js load delay, AND a loading indicator costs nothing to show universally (one extra `<div>` injected then removed). The trade-off was: 0 extra cost for universal vs +1 conditional branch for mobile-only. Universal won. **Rule: when a UX affordance has no extra cost to show universally, default to universal — conditional show/hide is a future bug magnet (mobile detection is fragile, viewport switching mid-session breaks it). Reserve conditional rendering for cases where the affordance is actively wrong on the other platform.**
- **The `.expandable` class as a behavioral hook (NOT structural) preserves Image Layout Protocol.** The Image Layout Protocol governs `.case-image`, `.case-image-trio`, `.case-image-sm` containers — the protected taxonomy. Adding a NEW class to an `<img>` element specifically as a "click-handler hook" doesn't restructure anything; the hero image's `<div class="case-hero-image">` wrapper is untouched, the `<img>` still lives where it always did, no grid changes. The class signals "JS will look for this and bind a click handler" — purely behavioral. The protocol's concern (silent restructuring of image containers) doesn't apply. **Rule: behavioral marker classes (`.expandable`, `.lazy-load`, `.scroll-snap-target`, etc.) added to elements WITHOUT changing their parent containers or their CSS positioning are protocol-compliant additions. They are functionally equivalent to `data-*` attributes; many sites use one or the other interchangeably.**
- **Mobile-only handler binding via matchMedia at bind-time, not at click-time.** The image modal handler reads `window.matchMedia('(max-width: 767px)').matches` ONCE at script load, and only binds the click listeners if true. Desktop users never get the listeners attached — no overhead, no accidental fires. Trade-off: if a desktop user resizes their window to ≤767px, the handlers won't activate (they'd need a page reload). Acceptable for a portfolio site where viewport switching is rare. Alternative: bind handlers always, check matchMedia inside the handler, return early if desktop — adds tiny per-click overhead. **Rule: for "feature only available at viewport X" patterns, prefer bind-time check (`if matchMedia.matches { bindHandlers }`) over click-time check. Cleaner, faster, more inspectable in DevTools (no listeners attached at all on the wrong viewport).**
- **Reusing the existing modal pattern shape (markup, ARIA, ESC, backdrop, focus management) for the new image modal was a multiplier.** The image modal uses the exact same lifecycle pattern as the resume modal: `role="dialog"`, `aria-labelledby`, `aria-hidden` toggle, `[data-image-close]` selector for closers, body `.modal-open` scroll-lock, focus-on-open + restore-on-close, ESC keydown listener. The only differences are: (a) no PDF.js (just an img), (b) no toolbar (no zoom controls — relies on native pinch), (c) trigger gated by mobile matchMedia. ~50 lines of JS, 30 lines of CSS, all pattern-derived from the resume modal. **Rule: when adding a new modal of any kind to this site, copy the resume modal's lifecycle pattern wholesale (markup shape, ARIA contract, JS open/close handlers, body scroll-lock). Don't reinvent the modal lifecycle — it's solved here. Just swap the body content.**
- **Native pinch-zoom on modal image works because viewport meta is permissive.** All 8 HTML pages use `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">` — no `user-scalable=no`, no `maximum-scale=1`. Combined with `touch-action: pinch-zoom` on `.image-modal-img`, native two-finger pinch-zoom works in Mobile Safari, Mobile Chrome, and Mobile Firefox without any custom JS. Verified the viewport meta proactively before shipping (would have been a silent failure otherwise). **Rule: any time native browser gestures (pinch-zoom, swipe-back, double-tap-zoom) are part of the UX expectation, the FIRST thing to verify is the viewport meta tag. Restrictive viewport configs (`user-scalable=no`, `maximum-scale=1`) silently disable all of them. Most sites historically used restrictive viewports for "control"; modern best practice (and accessibility) is permissive viewports + selective `touch-action` constraints on specific elements.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Read-before-Edit gotcha hit me again, this time on 3 of 4 case study HTML files in a parallel batch.** I dispatched 8 Edit calls in parallel covering: lexisnexis hero img (lexisnexis previously read in diagnostic — succeeded), pulse hero img + ghost hero img + gotefigure hero img (NOT previously read — all 3 failed), then 4 modal-markup edits (all 4 succeeded because cache from same Edit batch counts? actually no — they failed too, then I read all 3 and re-issued them). Then on the verification grep, I found `expandable` only on lexisnexis. Had to re-issue the pulse/ghost/gotefigure expandable edits SEPARATELY (a 2nd batch) because my initial re-issue batch only included the modal-markup edits, not the expandable-class edits — oversight on my part. Cost: one extra round-trip. **Rule confirmed (third time this rule fires): grep does NOT satisfy Read-before-Edit. Before any Edit batch, ensure every target file has been Read in the same session — use parallel Read calls if needed. AND when re-issuing failed edits after Reads, verify ALL the originally-failed edits get re-issued, not just a subset.**
- **My initial verification grep `class="expandable"\|class=".*expandable.*"` returned 0 matches on 3 of 4 files** — but I'd ALREADY missed the Edits, so the grep was correct, just my interpretation was wrong (I assumed it was a regex bug when really the data was missing). Caught it on a re-grep with a simpler `expandable` pattern. **Lesson: when grep returns unexpected zero counts, ALWAYS try a simpler pattern before assuming the regex is broken. The data missing is more often the cause than the regex.**
- **The brief proposed Issue 7's `max-width: 60%` rule on `.case-image-sm`, which I caught would be a no-op given current usage.** Brief framing: "small-image-stays-small mobile rule." But `.case-image-sm` is currently ALWAYS a trio child — its width is set by `grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr` on its parent `.case-image-trio`, not by `width` or `max-width` on itself. Inside a 1/3 grid cell, `max-width: 60%` of viewport is much larger than 1/3 of viewport width — so the cap never engages. The rule would only matter for a future standalone `.case-image-sm` use case that doesn't exist yet. **Lesson: when a brief proposes a CSS rule for a hypothetical future use case, defer the rule until the use case actually appears. Premature CSS is dead code that confuses future audits ("why is this here? what does it constrain?"). The brief itself acknowledged this with the "If the audit surfaces other small-image classes... flag them" wording — I just had to trust that signal.**

**What I learned:**
- **Bento aspect-ratio standardization (codified standing rule):** all bento-card thumbnails should use the SAME `aspect-ratio` value across desktop AND mobile UNLESS there's a specific design reason for divergence. Pre-this-session, desktop was uniform 16/9 but mobile diverged into 16/10, 3/2, 16/10, 4/3 — purely arbitrary, no design rationale. The divergence was the source of the cropping bugs (Pulse 800×420 ≈ 1.91:1 crammed into 1.5:1 cropped sides; Ghost 16:9 video crammed into 4:3 cropped more sides). **Rule: when adding a new bento card OR setting any responsive aspect-ratio override, the default should be "match the desktop aspect-ratio." Divergence from desktop requires a documented design reason (e.g., a card whose thumbnail is portrait-oriented and needs a different mobile shape). Defaults: 16/9 across viewport sizes for landscape thumbnails.**
- **Reusable `.image-modal` component as the canonical "expand any image" pattern (codified):** this site now has TWO modal patterns — `.resume-modal` (PDF.js viewer for the resume PDF) and `.image-modal` (any `<img class="expandable">` opens at full size with native pinch-zoom on mobile). The `.image-modal` is reusable beyond hero images: any future image (process screenshots, illustration zoom, design system thumbnails) can opt-in by adding `.expandable`. The handler is mobile-only by default (gated at bind-time via matchMedia), but the markup ships universally. **Standing rule: any future "user wants to see this image bigger" need on this site MUST use the `.image-modal` component with `.expandable` class on the trigger img. Don't build a new modal — extend this one. The pattern is: `.expandable` on the trigger, modal markup before `</body>`, JS handler already wired. To extend desktop-too: remove the matchMedia gate in main.js OR add a viewport-agnostic variant.**
- **`.expandable` class as the canonical behavioral marker (codified):** behavioral marker classes on elements are protocol-compliant additions that don't trigger the Image Layout Protocol's "structural change" concerns. The class signals "JS will treat this element specially" — not "this element belongs to a particular layout group." Other behavioral markers used on this site: `.fade-in` (intersection observer reveal trigger), `.reveal` (case-study scroll reveal). All three follow the same pattern: a class on an element that triggers JS behavior, with no parent-container restructuring or CSS geometry changes. **Rule: behavioral marker classes are SAFE additions that don't fall under image-container governance. Add them freely when JS needs to find specific elements. Compare to data attributes (`data-expandable`) — equivalent in function; class form is preferred on this site for consistency with `.fade-in`/`.reveal`.**
- **Issue 7 deferral pattern (codified):** when a brief proposes a CSS/JS rule for a usage that doesn't exist yet in the codebase, DEFER the rule until the usage actually appears. Premature rules: (a) confuse future audits because they're invisible no-ops; (b) may conflict with future actual usage patterns (the `.case-image-sm { max-width: 60% }` rule would have conflicted with future trio refactors); (c) waste CSS bytes on dead code; (d) add a constraint that may turn out to be wrong when the actual use case materializes. **Rule for batched proposals: always audit current usage before accepting a "future-proof" rule. If 0 instances of the targeted pattern exist, defer the rule. Document the deferral so a future session knows the pattern is "still pending — add when the standalone case appears."**
- **Loading indicator pattern for async content (codified):** any modal or panel that triggers an async load (PDF.js init, image fetch, API call) MUST inject a loading indicator on init start and remove on success OR failure. The indicator is a single `<div>` with class `.modal-loading` (or similar), `position: absolute; inset: 0; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;`, text content "Loading…" or a spinner. Inject from JS rather than ship in HTML — keeps the modal markup clean, and the JS owns the lifecycle. Implementation: `if (!loadingEl) { loadingEl = document.createElement('div'); ...; } if (!loadingEl.parentNode) container.appendChild(loadingEl);` to inject; `loadingEl.parentNode.removeChild(loadingEl)` to remove. **Rule: any async modal content on this site MUST include the loading-indicator pattern. The 5 lines of JS prevent the "blank modal for 500ms" UX bug that's invisible during local testing but obvious on slow networks.**

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (4 Edits: bento mobile aspect-ratio standardization at lines 1361-1364 → grouped selector with 16/9; trio mobile at line 1380 → 3-up; resume modal mobile media query at lines 1763-1774 → full-screen layout; appended ~50 lines of `.image-modal*` styles + `.resume-modal-loading` after the resume modal CSS block; net file size ~1782 → ~1855 lines), `main.js` (5 Edits: removed `isMobileViewport()` function definition; removed its guard in `openResumeModal`; added `showPdfLoading` + `hidePdfLoading` helpers + `pdfLoadingEl` state; wired `showPdfLoading` into `initPdfViewer` start and `hidePdfLoading` into success path + `showPdfFallback`; appended ~50-line image modal handler before final `})();`; net ~593 → ~640 lines), `index.html` (1 Edit: cache-bust `?v=13 → ?v=14` via replace_all), `about.html` (1 Edit: same), `process.html` (1 Edit: same), `work/lexisnexis.html` (3 Edits: `.expandable` on hero img + image modal markup before `</body>` + cache-bust both `?v=11 → ?v=14`), `work/pulse.html` (3 Edits: same), `work/ghost.html` (3 Edits: same), `work/gotefigure.html` (3 Edits: same), `_archive/_backup-mobile-fixes-2026-04-27/` (NEW backup folder containing 9 pre-edit files: 5 root + 4 case studies in `work/` subfolder), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `404.html` (no modal/expandable, cache-bust deliberately stays at `?v=11`), `game.js`, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all image/video assets, all other `_archive/_backup-*` folders, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Image Layout Protocol explicitly respected — `.expandable` is behavioral not structural; no `.case-image*` containers were modified).

**Cache-bust state after this session (drift continues, deliberately):** index.html, about.html, process.html, work/lexisnexis.html, work/pulse.html, work/ghost.html, work/gotefigure.html → `styles.css?v=14` and `main.js?v=14` (7 of 8 pages). 404.html → `styles.css?v=11` and `main.js?v=11` (untouched). game.js stays at `?v=3` everywhere. Drift is 2 distinct values across 8 pages — within threshold.

**Verified mechanically (this session):**
- All 4 case study hero `<img>` tags have `class="expandable"` (initially missed 3 of 4 due to Read-before-Edit gotcha, caught and fixed in the verification grep round, then re-verified clean).
- Image modal markup (`id="imageModal"`) present exactly once on each of 4 case study pages.

- `isMobileViewport` function fully removed from main.js (grep returns 0).
- Image modal handler in main.js: 12 image-modal/imageModal references confirming wiring.

- Trio mobile rule reads `1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 6px;` (3-up persisted).
- Resume modal mobile media query exists at line 1763 (replaced full-screen layout block).
- All 7 changed pages reference `?v=14`; 404 reference `?v=11`; game.js untouched.
- All 10 HTTP endpoints return 200 (4 case studies + 3 root + styles + main + PDF).
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=14 contains 8 image-modal/expandable selectors.
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=14 contains ZERO `.case-image-trio { grid-template-columns: 1fr;` (single-column trio rule cleanly removed).
- HTTP-served main.js?v=14 contains 7 image-modal handler references.

- Backup folder contains all 9 pre-edit files (5 root + 4 case studies in work/).

**Verified at deploy / requires browser (handed to user):**
- All 4 bento thumbnails render correctly on mobile (no crop on Ghost 16:9 video, no crop on Pulse 800×420 PNG, all 4 cards consistent 16:9 shape).
- Resume modal opens full-screen on mobile (100vw/100vh), header sticky at top, toolbar sticky at bottom, X button is 44px touch target, PDF.js renders the resume.
- "Loading…" indicator appears briefly during PDF.js library fetch on first open (universal, not just mobile).
- LexisNexis hero (and all 4 case study heroes) is tappable on mobile; opens in image modal; native pinch-zoom works; X / backdrop / ESC all close.
- LexisNexis trio (and gotefigure trios) shows 3 small images in a row on mobile (was: collapsed to single column).
- Image modal does NOT trigger on desktop (matchMedia gate at bind time — desktop hero images stay non-interactive).
- No console errors.
- No regressions on desktop layout.

**Follow-up if user wants to fully close the loop:**
1. Browser-test the mobile resume modal on actual iOS Safari and Android Chrome — PDF.js mobile rendering can vary, and the full-screen sticky header/toolbar pattern needs visual confirmation on real devices vs DevTools simulation.
2. Image modal currently mobile-only by design. If user wants desktop image expansion in a future session, remove the `matchMedia` gate in the handler (one line change).
3. Issue 7 deferral: when a future case study introduces a standalone `.case-image-sm` (not a trio child), revisit the small-image mobile cap rule then. Until then, the rule stays unwritten.
4. The 3 heavier LexisNexis PNGs (overview-v2 1964 KB, research-insight 1281 KB, tag-taxonomy 932 KB) remain candidates for `oxipng` install or JPG q92 escalation if further perf is desired.
5. Standing rules added this session: bento aspect-ratio standardization (match desktop unless documented divergence), `.image-modal` as canonical image-expansion component, `.expandable` as behavioral marker (protocol-compliant), Issue 7 deferral pattern (defer rules for unused patterns), loading-indicator pattern for async modals.

## 2026-04-27 — Translucent-nav crop on Process page + sidebar anchor scroll buffer

**What I did:** Two surgical fixes. (1) Process page's page-level "Process — 2026" section-label was rendering BEHIND the fixed nav (60px tall, 85% opaque background) because its inline `style="padding-top: 48px;"` pushed text down INSIDE the element's box rather than pushing the element itself below the nav. The label text rendered at y=48 to ~y=62 — sitting in the nav's translucent overlay zone, which tinted it and made it appear "faded/cropped" to the user. Single-line fix: replaced inline `padding-top: 48px` with `margin-top: 88px` (60px nav + 28px breathing room) on [process.html:76](process.html#L76). (2) Sidebar nav anchor scrolls landed sections behind the fixed nav across all 4 case study pages + Process page (every page with `.case-section` / `.outcome-section` and an `id="..."` anchor). Single CSS rule appended after the `.outcome-section` block in [styles.css:998-1003](styles.css#L998-L1003): `.case-section, .outcome-section { scroll-margin-top: 84px; }` (60px nav + 24px buffer). Cache-bust styles-only (main.js unchanged this session): bumped `styles.css?v=14 → ?v=15` on the 7 affected pages (index, about, process, 4 case studies); 404 stays at `?v=11`. main.js stays at `?v=14` everywhere. Backup at `_archive/_backup-scroll-and-process-fix-2026-04-27/` containing 2 pre-edit files (styles.css, process.html).

**What worked:**
- **Diagnosis-first revealed the actual root cause was the WRONG one I would have guessed.** My initial hypothesis was option (a) "fade-in animation visible mid-state on initial paint." Diagnostic data quickly disproved it: `.fade-in` starts at `opacity: 0` (invisible, not "faded mid-state"), and the JS adds `.visible` after data-delay to fade IN. So the user's perceived "faded" state isn't from the fade animation — it's the **translucent nav background tinting whatever sits beneath it**. The nav has `background: rgba(250, 250, 248, 0.85)` (85% opaque). Any content underneath shows through at 15% but is dominated by the nav's tint. This is the "faded" appearance — not opacity transition, but z-stack composition. **Rule: when content "appears faded" near a fixed/sticky overlay, the first hypothesis should be "it's BEHIND the overlay and the overlay is translucent." Check the overlay's background opacity before chasing animation/transition explanations. A solid 100%-opaque overlay would have made this bug obvious (content would be fully covered, not partially tinted) — translucent overlays HIDE the bug by making it look like a styling issue.**
- **`margin-top` vs `padding-top` choice was load-bearing.** `padding-top: 48px` adds padding INSIDE the element's box — pushes the text down WITHIN the element, but the element's TOP edge stays at the parent's flow position (y=0 here). `margin-top: 88px` pushes the WHOLE element down — element's top edge moves to y=88. Only margin moves the element relative to the nav. For "clear the fixed nav," ALWAYS use margin or push the parent container. Padding is for INTERNAL spacing within an element, never for external clearance. **Rule: when the goal is "push this element below a fixed/sticky overlay," use `margin-top` (or equivalent: parent `padding-top`, sibling `margin-bottom`). Never use `padding-top` on the element itself — that doesn't change its position, only its content's position within its own box.**
- **`scroll-margin-top` is the dedicated CSS property for this exact problem.** Pre-fix, every anchor link (`<a href="#section-id">`) scrolled the target's TOP edge to viewport y=0. With a 60px fixed nav covering y=0 to y=60, the section heading was always invisible behind the nav. `scroll-margin-top: 84px` on the section means: when an anchor scrolls to me, treat my "scrollable top" as 84px ABOVE my actual top — i.e., scroll me down 84px from y=0, so my actual top lands at y=84 (well below the nav's y=60). One CSS property, no JS, zero scroll handlers needed. Browsers added this property specifically because sticky-nav anchor overshoot is universal. **Rule confirmed: ALWAYS use `scroll-margin-top` for anchor-scroll buffer. Never reach for JS scroll-handlers, never use `padding-top` on the section (that adds visible space above), never use a transparent spacer div. `scroll-margin-top` is exactly the right tool.**
- **Single-rule grouped selector covers every section across every case study + Process.** All sidebar links target `id="..."` on `.case-section` or `.outcome-section`. The grouped rule `.case-section, .outcome-section { scroll-margin-top: 84px; }` automatically covers: 4 case studies × ~6-8 sections each + Process × 7 sections = ~35+ anchors, all fixed by one rule. **Rule: when applying a uniform behavior to N elements that share a class pattern, prefer one grouped-selector rule over N per-element rules. The grouping IS the documentation: "every case-section and outcome-section gets this scroll buffer" reads naturally; the alternative (one rule per id) reads like a config dump.**
- **Measurement-first beat estimation.** I almost wrote `scroll-margin-top: 80px` as a "feels right" estimate. Diagnostic measurement returned exact nav height (60px) from `nav { height: 60px; }` at [styles.css:362](styles.css#L362). 60 + 24 buffer = 84px, derived from data not gut. The 4px difference between my gut estimate and the actual value isn't huge here, but the discipline matters: estimates compound across sessions, measurements don't. **Rule confirmed: ALWAYS measure sticky element heights before picking buffer values. The cost is one grep; the value is rules grounded in data instead of approximation. For any future scroll-margin-top, padding adjustment, or sticky-element layout, the FIRST step is `grep height: ... position: fixed|sticky` to know the actual values.**
- **Cache-bust styles-only (no main.js bump) was correctly minimal.** main.js didn't change this session. Bumping its `?v=` would invalidate browser cache for every visitor for no functional reason. Bumping styles.css only → 7 pages now reference `styles.css?v=15` and `main.js?v=14`. The minor styles/main drift on those pages is harmless (the convention is per-resource bumps, not lockstep). **Rule confirmed: cache-bust ONLY what changed. Don't bump unchanged files for "consistency" — the browser-cache cost (re-downloading unchanged JS/CSS) is real, and the audit cost (per-resource drift) is minor.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **My initial root-cause guess (animation mid-state) was wrong, but the diagnostic protocol caught it before any Edit landed.** If I'd executed on the animation hypothesis, I'd have shipped a "fix" that removed the fade-in delay or starting opacity — neither of which would have fixed the actual bug (the label would STILL be behind the translucent nav, just no longer fading in). The diagnostic step that saved me: reading `.fade-in` CSS revealed the starting state is `opacity: 0`, not "faded." That immediately killed the animation hypothesis and pointed to the real cause (z-stack composition). **Lesson: when multiple hypotheses are plausible, the diagnostic step's job is to KILL hypotheses, not just confirm them. The CSS read that disproved my first guess was 5 seconds and saved a wrong fix. ALWAYS do the disprove pass before proposing.**
- **The `padding-top: 48px` inline style on the section-label looked correct at a glance** because `.section-label` has `display: block;` (so padding works on it), and the value 48px feels intuitive for "clear a 60px nav." But the SEMANTICS of padding (internal spacing) vs margin (external positioning) made it wrong here. The original author's intent was right ("push this content down to clear the nav"); the chosen property was wrong. **Lesson: code that "looks correct" because the property exists and the value seems sensible can still be wrong if the property's semantics don't match the intent. When reviewing layout code, always ask: "does this property push the element ITSELF, or push its CONTENTS?" That distinguishes margin (element-positioning) from padding (content-positioning).**

**What I learned:**
- **Translucent-nav-background-eats-content failure mode (codified standing rule):** when a fixed/sticky nav has any background opacity less than 100%, anything underneath it appears tinted/faded, NOT visually clear. The nav on this site uses `background: rgba(250, 250, 248, 0.85)` and on scroll `rgba(250, 250, 248, 0.92)` + backdrop-filter blur — both less than fully opaque. **Standing rule: any new content that lands at the top of a page (no hero image above it to act as buffer) MUST clear the nav by at least 60px (nav height) + 20px buffer = 80px minimum. Push via `margin-top` on the first content element, OR `padding-top` on the parent container. NEVER use `padding-top` on the content element itself — that pushes content within its own box, not past the nav.** Pages currently affected by this rule: Process (no hero, fixed by this session), homepage (no hero on bento grid section — but its top spacing is set via parent container padding, not this pattern, so safe). Case study pages have hero images that act as buffers, exempt by design.
- **scroll-margin-top as the canonical fix for sticky-nav anchor overlap (codified standing rule):** any new section with `id="..."` that sidebar nav or in-page anchor will scroll to MUST inherit `scroll-margin-top` via `.case-section`/`.outcome-section`, OR have its own `scroll-margin-top` rule if it uses a different selector. The current value (84px) is calibrated for the 60px nav + 24px buffer. **Standing rule: when introducing a new section class that anchor links can target, verify `scroll-margin-top` is set on it BEFORE shipping. Add to the existing grouped rule `.case-section, .outcome-section, .NEW-SECTION { scroll-margin-top: 84px; }` rather than creating a duplicate rule. If the nav height ever changes, this single value updates everything.**
- **Measurement-first diagnostic pattern (codified):** ALWAYS measure sticky element heights before guessing buffer values. The 84px came from `nav { height: 60px; }` (measured) + 24px buffer (chosen for visual breathing room) — both grounded, not estimated. Procedure for any future "how much buffer should I add for a sticky element?" question: (1) `grep "position: fixed\|position: sticky" styles.css` to enumerate all top-anchored elements; (2) `grep "height:" styles.css | grep -B1 fixed|sticky` to read their heights; (3) sum measured heights + add intentional buffer (typically 20-30px); (4) document the math in the CSS comment so future readers understand the value. **Rule: the comment on the scroll-margin-top rule says "60px fixed nav + 24px breathing room" — that math IS the documentation. Future sessions can verify the assumption holds (nav is still 60px) or update both numbers together.**
- **margin vs padding semantic distinction for clearance (codified):** `padding-top` adds INTERNAL spacing (pushes content down within the element's own box). `margin-top` adds EXTERNAL spacing (pushes the element itself down relative to its siblings/parent). For "clear a fixed/sticky overlay," ALWAYS use `margin-top` (or equivalent: `padding-top` on the PARENT container). For "add space between an element's border and its content," use `padding-top`. **Rule: when reading layout CSS, the question to ask is "does this property push the element ITSELF (margin) or push its CONTENTS (padding)?" Mistaking one for the other is the bug class fixed by this session.**
- **Diagnostic-step's hypothesis-killing role (codified):** when proposing a fix, the diagnostic step's PRIMARY job is to KILL wrong hypotheses, not just confirm right ones. Initial hypotheses are usually plausible (otherwise they wouldn't be hypotheses), but plausible ≠ correct. The diagnostic data should be examined for evidence AGAINST each hypothesis: "if hypothesis X were true, I'd expect to see Y in the code; do I see Y?" If no, kill X. **Rule: in any STEP 1 diagnostic, explicitly enumerate which hypotheses each piece of data CONFIRMS or DISPROVES. The disprove pass is where wrong fixes get prevented.**

**Files touched:** `styles.css` (1 Edit: append 6-line `.case-section, .outcome-section { scroll-margin-top: 84px; }` rule with comment, after `.outcome-section p` rule at line 994; net file ~1855 → ~1861 lines), `process.html` (2 Edits: section-label inline style `padding-top: 48px` → `margin-top: 88px` at line 76; cache-bust `styles.css?v=14 → ?v=15` at line 24), `index.html` (1 Edit: cache-bust `styles.css?v=14 → ?v=15`), `about.html` (1 Edit: same), `work/lexisnexis.html` (1 Edit: same), `work/pulse.html` (1 Edit: same), `work/ghost.html` (1 Edit: same), `work/gotefigure.html` (1 Edit: same), `_archive/_backup-scroll-and-process-fix-2026-04-27/` (NEW backup folder containing 2 pre-edit files: styles.css, process.html), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `main.js` (no behavioral change needed — `scroll-margin-top` is pure CSS, no JS scroll handler required), `game.js`, `404.html` (no `.case-section`/`.outcome-section`, no Process label — cache-bust deliberately stays at `?v=11`), `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all image/video assets, all other `_archive/_backup-*` folders, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Image Layout Protocol not in scope this session — no images touched).

**Cache-bust state after this session (styles/main drift introduced, deliberately):** index.html, about.html, process.html, work/lexisnexis.html, work/pulse.html, work/ghost.html, work/gotefigure.html → `styles.css?v=15` and `main.js?v=14` (7 of 8 pages, styles bumped, main unchanged). 404.html → `styles.css?v=11` and `main.js?v=11` (untouched). game.js stays at `?v=3` everywhere. New drift type this session: per-resource drift WITHIN a page (styles=15, main=14 on the 7 affected pages). Acceptable per the "cache-bust only what changed" rule.

**Verified mechanically (this session):**
- Process page label inline style is now `margin-top: 88px` (grep confirmed on file + on HTTP-served process.html).
- `scroll-margin-top: 84px` rule exists at line 1000 of styles.css with grouped selector covering both `.case-section` and `.outcome-section` (sed confirmed on file + on HTTP-served styles.css?v=15).
- All 7 changed pages reference `styles.css?v=15`; 404 reference `styles.css?v=11`; main.js correctly stays at `?v=14` on 7 pages and `?v=11` on 404.
- All 9 HTTP endpoints return 200 (4 case studies + 3 root + styles + main).
- HTTP-served styles.css?v=15 contains the new rule (curl + grep confirmed — CSS reaches the browser).
- HTTP-served process.html contains the `margin-top: 88px;` inline style on the section-label (curl + grep confirmed).
- Backup folder contains both pre-edit files (`ls` confirmed).

**Verified at deploy / requires browser (handed to user):**
- Process page: page-level "Process — 2026" label clearly visible on load, sitting cleanly below the 60px fixed nav with ~28px breathing room. No tint, no crop, fade-in animation reads naturally.
- LexisNexis: click each sidebar link (Overview, The Problem, Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, Outcome) — section heading lands ~24px below the fixed nav, fully visible.

**Follow-up if user wants to fully close the loop:**
1. Browser-test the Process page label clearance on Chrome / Firefox / Safari to confirm the 88px margin reads visually consistent.
2. Browser-test sidebar anchor scrolls on Chrome / Firefox / Safari — `scroll-margin-top` has been stable since Chromium 69, Firefox 68, Safari 14.5 (all evergreen support, no fallback needed).
