3. **Backup-before-modify discipline at session start.** User explicitly asked for `_archive/_backup-homepage-subtitle-broaden-2026-04-30/index.html`. Created the directory, copied the pre-edit file, listed contents to confirm 15573-byte original preserved. Standard pattern, repeated cleanly.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Nothing surprising this session — the change was as simple as briefed.** No CSS implications (the `.hero-subtitle` rule wraps text agnostic of length), no responsive concerns (paragraph reflows naturally at any width), no animation timing (the `data-delay="0.15"` on the `fade-in` is independent of paragraph length). One-sentence trim, period-already-existed, done.

**What I learned:**

1. **Hero copy is positioning copy — every word filters the audience either in or out.** The removed sentence "Looking for founding designer roles at seed and Series A startups" was a hard filter: it told visitors NOT in that bucket (recruiters at larger companies, hiring managers at growth-stage or public companies, IC roles outside founding-designer scope) that the candidate wasn't open to them. The user is broadening the target. Removing the line opens the funnel without making any positive claim that doesn't fit the broader audience. **Rule: when copy explicitly names a narrow target, it pre-filters everyone else. If the user's strategy is broadening, the right move is removal, not rewording — because any "and X and Y and Z" rewording either (a) makes the line lose punch by listing too many things, or (b) still implies a list and excludes whatever isn't on it. Silence on the role-spec is more inviting than an enumerated list.**

2. **Sentence-removal trim discipline: when a sentence ends in a period that's already terminating the prior sentence, the trim is clean by default.** The previous sentence ended "...end-to-end." with its own period. Removing " Looking for founding designer roles at seed and Series A startups." (with the leading space) leaves the prior period intact. No orphan punctuation cleanup needed. **Rule: when trimming a final sentence from a multi-sentence paragraph, include the leading space in the deletion. The prior sentence's terminating period stays. Verify with grep that the removed phrase is gone AND the prior sentence's tail is intact.**

3. **HTML text edits don't propagate the cache-bust dance.** Only versioned (cached) assets need `?v=N` bumps. The site's `.html` files are fetched fresh every navigation; `.css` and `.js` are referenced with versioned query strings because they ARE long-cached. Knowing which assets are cached vs. fresh per request determines whether cache-bust is required. **Rule already promoted: HTML text-only changes need no cache-bust. CSS/JS changes always need cache-bust. This session: HTML-only → no bump.**

**Files touched:**
- `index.html` (line 157: trimmed final sentence " Looking for founding designer roles at seed and Series A startups." from hero subtitle paragraph; first 3 sentences preserved verbatim)
- `_archive/_backup-homepage-subtitle-broaden-2026-04-30/index.html` (NEW backup, pre-edit copy, 15573 bytes)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)

Explicitly untouched: hero `<h1>` text ([index.html:156](index.html#L156)), hero resume link ([index.html:158](index.html#L158)), bento grid ([index.html:161](index.html#L161)+), all other homepage copy, `styles.css` (no CSS change — paragraph still uses `.hero-subtitle` rule unchanged, no length-dependent styling), `main.js` (no JS change — `data-delay` for fade-in unchanged), `game.js`, all case-study HTML files, `about.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all other `_archive/_backup-*` directories, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (no rule additions/edits — this session is pure content trim, no architectural learnings to promote). Image Layout Protocol respected (no image change). Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1-6 still hold (no video change). JS-State Classes Standing Rule still holds (no class change).

## 2026-04-30 — Cache-bust ?v=19 → ?v=20 (force CDN/browser refresh of layer-5 fix)

**What I did:** Pure cache-bust pass on all 8 active HTML files. No CSS, no JS, no markup changes. The `.reveal:has(video) { opacity: 1; transform: none; }` rule from the earlier 2026-04-30 session was already live in `styles.css` at line 476, and all 8 HTML files were already at `?v=19`. User reported the iOS autoplay symptom was still appearing after the prior deploy. To force a fresh fetch through any intermediate CDN edge cache, browser HTTP cache, or service-worker cache that might still be serving a pre-fix `styles.css`, bumped `?v=19` → `?v=20` on all 8 files: index, 404, about, process, work/{lexisnexis,pulse,ghost,gotefigure}. Backed up to `_archive/_backup-cache-bust-2026-04-30/` (preserves directory structure including `work/` subfolder). Verified each file diff against backup contains only the cache-bust line — zero collateral.

**What worked:**
1. **Diagnostic-first protocol caught a duplicate-fix request before doing harm.** The prompt as written instructed "add `.reveal:has(video) { opacity: 1; transform: none; }` rule" — but STEP 1 (read `styles.css` and confirm current state of `.reveal` rules) revealed the rule was already at line 476 from the prior session. Halting at STEP 2 with an "already done — three reasons it might still be broken, four options for what to do next" report let the user pick the actual right action (cache-bust to invalidate the deployed asset) instead of executing a no-op CSS change. **Rule re-confirmed: STEP 1 is the gate. Read the actual file state before assuming the prompt's premise is current. Diagnose against ground truth, not against the prompt's framing.**
2. **Backup-then-edit-then-diff-against-backup pattern as a self-verifying workflow.** Copying all 8 files into `_archive/_backup-cache-bust-2026-04-30/` (preserving the `work/` subdir) before editing, then running `diff <(grep -v 'styles.css?v=' BACKUP) <(grep -v 'styles.css?v=' CURRENT)` per file — this *proves* via tooling that nothing else changed. The grep-out of the cache-bust line lets the diff focus only on collateral changes; clean diff = no surprise edits. Cheaper than re-reading every file end-to-end. **Rule: when running pure mechanical edits across multiple files, the backup-diff loop is the verification mechanism. Diff the whole file against backup, with the intentional change filtered out, and confirm zero output.**
3. **`grep -c 'pattern'` per file as a uniqueness pre-flight.** Before swapping `?v=19` → `?v=20`, confirmed each file had exactly 1 occurrence of `?v=19`. If any file had 2+, a naive edit would break — the second occurrence wouldn't be the styles.css link. (None did, but the check was free.) **Rule: before any "swap pattern X → Y" mechanical edit, grep-count occurrences per file. If any file shows >1, switch to a more specific match (include surrounding context) or fall through to the Edit tool's exact-match guarantee.**
4. **Read tool's "must read before edit" constraint reinforced single-file-edit discipline.** Initial attempt was 8 parallel Edits without prior Reads — all 8 failed. Realizing the constraint, I ran 8 parallel Reads (with limit:30 to keep token cost minimal), then 8 parallel Edits — all 8 succeeded. The constraint exists to prevent edits-without-context; the pattern of "small Read + targeted Edit" satisfies it cheaply. **Rule: when batching edits across N files, always pair each with a small Read first. The Read can be limited (e.g., `limit:30`) — Edit only requires the file has been read in the conversation, not that the entire file was read.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **First-instinct batch-edit failed because Edit requires a prior Read in the conversation.** Tried to fire 8 Edits in parallel without prior Reads, expecting it to work as a generic file-mutation tool. It correctly refused with "File has not been read yet." This is a session-scoped state — Edit checks whether THIS conversation has Read the file, not whether the file is readable. Confused for a moment whether sed via Bash would be cheaper than 8 Reads + 8 Edits; settled on Reads-then-Edits because (a) the tool's contract favors that pattern, (b) sed introduces a regex correctness concern that Edit's exact-string match avoids, (c) parallel Reads + parallel Edits is 16 calls but cheap each. **Rule: Edit's "must Read first" gate is per-conversation, not per-file-on-disk. For batched edits, always front-run with a parallel batch of small Reads.**
- **The user prompt's "fix the issue" framing was load-bearingly wrong.** The prompt described the iOS autoplay symptom and prescribed the `:has(video)` CSS rule as the fix. That rule was the right diagnosis on the prior session — but in this session, that rule already existed. Following the prompt literally would have meant re-adding an already-existing rule and re-running an already-done deploy. The actual problem was at a different layer (deployed asset cache), and the right action was a different mechanism (cache-bust). **Rule: when a prompt prescribes a fix in detail, treat the prescription as a hypothesis to verify, not an instruction to execute. The user knows what they observed; they may have an outdated mental model of where the work currently sits. STEP 1 reconciles those.**

**What I learned:**
1. **Cache-bust as a deploy-side "force re-fetch" mechanism — independent layer from the underlying fix.** When a CSS/JS fix is correct AND deployed AND the user's device still shows the bug, the next layer to suspect is asset cache: CDN edge, browser HTTP cache, service worker. Bumping the `?v=` query param invalidates all three because (a) the URL is different, so HTTP cache miss; (b) the URL is different, so CDN cache miss until populated; (c) most service workers cache by URL, so URL change = miss. The `?v=N` convention on this site is the canonical mechanism. **Rule: any deployed-but-not-rendering fix on this site gets a cache-bust as the first remediation step before assuming the fix itself is wrong. Bump on all 8 active HTML files in lockstep — never partial — to keep the version consistent across all pages.**

2. **Cache-bust must hit ALL pages in lockstep — partial bumps create inconsistency.** If only `index.html` bumped to `?v=20` but project pages stayed at `?v=19`, a user navigating from home → project would (a) get fresh CSS on home, (b) get cached old CSS on project. Worse: the `?v=19` cached CSS would persist for the project pages until they got bumped too. **Rule: cache-bust is an atomic operation across all 8 active HTML files. Always bump all eight (root: index, 404, about, process; work/: lexisnexis, pulse, ghost, gotefigure) to the same number in the same commit. Never bump a subset.**

3. **Backup directory structure must mirror source structure for safe rollback.** When the source has a `work/` subdir, the backup must too — otherwise rollback is ambiguous (do I copy `work/lexisnexis.html` or `lexisnexis.html` from the backup?). The `mkdir -p _archive/_backup-cache-bust-2026-04-30/work` step preserved the structure; the per-file `cp` calls used the matching destination paths. **Rule: backups for multi-directory edit passes use `mkdir -p` for each subdir, then `cp` per file with the matching dest path. Do not flatten directory structure in backups.**

4. **The prompt's prescribed action vs the actual root cause can diverge — and STEP 1 catches it.** This session's prompt prescribed the CSS fix for the autoplay bug; the actual root cause this time was cached deployed asset, which the CSS fix wouldn't address. STEP 1 reading the current file state surfaced the divergence. The user's option-pick (Option C: cache-bust + redeploy) confirmed the diagnosis. **Rule: when STEP 1 reveals the prescribed fix is already done, present the user with a menu of likely-next-layer remediations (CDN cache, deploy lag, deeper cascade issue) rather than executing the no-op. Three-to-four options keeps the user in driver's seat.**

5. **`?v=` query-string cache-bust convention is the canonical mechanism on this site — preserve it across all future asset changes.** Per the prior 2026-04-30 entries, the version number is monotonically increasing (... `?v=18` → `?v=19` → `?v=20` ...). Never reuse a number. Never roll back the number. The number is purely an opaque token to defeat caches — its semantic content is "this is a different URL than the prior one." **Rule: `?v=N` numbers monotonically increase forever. Find the highest number across the codebase and increment.** Audit before bumping: `grep -rh 'styles.css?v=' --include='*.html' . | sort -u` to confirm the current state is uniform across active pages.

**Files touched:**
- `index.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `404.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `about.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `process.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `work/lexisnexis.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `work/pulse.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `work/ghost.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust `?v=19` → `?v=20`)
- `_archive/_backup-cache-bust-2026-04-30/` (NEW — pre-edit copies of all 8 files, preserving `work/` subdir for rollback)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)

Explicitly untouched: `styles.css` (the `:has(video)` rule from the earlier 2026-04-30 session is already correct at line 476), `main.js`, `game.js`, all video files, all `<video>` element attributes, all image files, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (the standing rules from this morning's two entries already cover the layered iOS autoplay model — no rule-promotion needed for a pure cache-bust), `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all other `_archive/_backup-*` directories. Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1-6 unchanged. JS-State Classes Standing Rule unchanged. Image Layout Protocol respected (no image container changes).

## 2026-04-30 — Mobile video autoplay layer 6: parse-time `.play()` invocation + diagnostic instrumentation closes a 7-day loop

**What I did:** Added a parse-time `attemptVideoAutoplay()` pass that queries every `<video>` and calls `.play().catch(() => {})` at `DOMContentLoaded` (or immediately if the DOM is already parsed). Inserted at [main.js:284-304](main.js#L284-L304) between the STAGGERED LOAD ANIMATION block and the SCROLL REVEAL IO block. This is belt-and-suspenders alongside the morning IO retry — it catches videos that are already above-the-fold at parse, which never trigger the IO callback, so the IO retry alone misses them. Removed the entire `?debug=videos` instrumentation block ([main.js:729-883](main.js#L729-L883) in the prior version) — 155 lines covering the `v.play` override, the floating `__videoDebugPanel`, `Force play()` and `Snapshot` buttons, `readState`/`render` helpers, and the clipboard fallback. The diagnostic was complete (real-iPhone telemetry from this session showed parse-time `paused:true / lastPlayState:never-called` on every video, then `paused:false / lastPlayState:resolved` after manual `Force play()` tap — proving the autoplay machinery itself works once invoked, but iOS denies the implicit `autoplay` attribute pre-gesture). Cache-bust: `main.js?v=16` → `?v=17` across all 8 active HTML files in lockstep. Backup: `_archive/_backup-autoplay-final-2026-04-30/` (preserves `work/` subdir; 8 HTML + main.js pre-edit).

**What worked:**
1. **The diagnostic-first protocol finally broke a 7-day fix loop.** Apr 24 added 7-attribute pattern. Apr 29 fixed codec. Apr 30 morning removed `.page-transition.active`. Apr 30 midday added `.reveal:has(video)` override. Apr 30 afternoon cache-busted `?v=19` → `?v=20`. Each fix was technically correct AND addressed a real layer of the iOS autoplay gate stack — but the user kept coming back with the same symptom. The breakthrough this session was *not* another fix attempt. It was 30 minutes of pure instrumentation: a floating debug panel that polled `userActivation`, computed-style opacity/visibility/display, paused/readyState/networkState, and (critically) a `v.play` monkey-patch that recorded the resolution state of every `.play()` promise. The data discriminated between hypotheses: visibility:1 at parse + lastPlayState:never-called → the `autoplay` attribute is being denied AND no `.play()` API call is reaching iOS at parse. The IO retry only fires for offscreen videos that scroll into view; above-the-fold videos never trigger it. **Rule promoted to CLAUDE.md (new): when a bug returns after fix attempt N, the next session must be diagnostic-only — instrument, measure, prove which gate is failing — NOT another fix attempt. Going in circles without measurement is a code smell that means the diagnostic model is wrong.**
2. **The IO-retry-only mental model was wrong for above-the-fold videos.** The morning's IO `video.play().catch()` pattern was added under the assumption "any video the user sees got revealed by IO." But IO only fires for `.reveal` elements that *enter* the viewport during scroll. Videos that are already in the viewport at parse (the homepage bento grid, the Pulse hero, the Ghost slider) are above-the-fold, IO sees them as already-intersecting at observation time — but the prior pattern unobserves them after one fire. Without a parse-time pass, the IO callback may run *once* on initialization with `entry.isIntersecting:true` (depends on rootMargin/threshold timing), or may not run at all if the threshold's already-met state is treated as no-op. **Rule: any IO-based "rescue" pattern needs an explicit parse-time bootstrap for elements that are already in the target state at parse. Don't assume the IO callback fires for already-met conditions.**
3. **Layer-6 fix is additive, not corrective.** The two-line `attemptVideoAutoplay` function does not undo or override any prior session's fix. The codec from Apr 29 still matters (Mobile Video Standing Rule §2). The `.page-transition.active` removal from Apr 30 morning still matters (JS-State Classes §2). The `.reveal:has(video)` override from Apr 30 midday still matters (Mobile Video Standing Rule §6). The `?v=17` bump still matters (cache invalidation). All five prior fixes are necessary preconditions for the parse-time `.play()` to succeed. **Rule re-confirmed: when a bug has multiple contributing causes, removing the symptom requires fixing every layer. No single fix is "the" fix — the stack must hold all the way through.**
4. **Two-changes-only scope held.** No CSS changes, no HTML structural changes, no `<video>` attribute changes, no styles.css cache-bust, no game.js touched. Only main.js (insert + delete) and the 8-file cache-bust. Diff against backup confirms zero collateral. **Rule re-confirmed: scope discipline is verified by `diff <BACKUP> <CURRENT>` filtered for the intentional change. Clean diff = scope held.**

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Five fix sessions in seven days produced an identical user-facing symptom: "video shows poster + center play icon."** The symptom did not discriminate between layers. Each fix was correct for the layer it addressed, but with no instrumentation, the next session's framing was always "the previous fix didn't work — try harder at the same layer or the next adjacent one." The fix loop was: attempt → user reports same symptom → assume one layer deeper → attempt → repeat. Five rounds. The breakthrough was inverting the protocol: **measure first, fix second**. Once the instrumentation panel reported `lastPlayState:never-called`, the layer-6 hypothesis (no parse-time play() invocation) became testable in 5 minutes. **Rule: identical symptoms across multiple fix sessions is THE diagnostic signal that the model is wrong. Stop fixing. Instrument.**
- **The `.catch(() => {})` pattern was *correct* but obscured layer-6's invisibility.** The morning IO retry uses `v.play().catch(function () {})` — silently swallows rejections. In a normal session this is the right pattern (defensive, no-noise). In a debugging session it's a black box. The instrumentation overrode `v.play` to record both resolution paths to a visible field. After this session: the production code keeps the silent catch (correct for prod), but the standing rule for *future debugging* is to add explicit logging to BOTH paths during diagnostic phases. **Rule already in 2026-04-30 midday entry, re-confirmed: defensive `.catch()` patterns hide failures during diagnostic. Always log both `.then()` and `.catch()` during any video-autoplay debug phase. Remove the logs only after the pattern is confirmed working.**
- **The IIFE-at-end-of-body pattern made `DOMContentLoaded` listener registration potentially too late.** main.js loads at the bottom of `<body>` without `defer`. By the time the IIFE executes, `document.readyState` is typically `"interactive"` — DOMContentLoaded has already fired, so `document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', ...)` would silently never run. The `if (document.readyState === 'loading')` guard branches on whether DOMContentLoaded has fired yet: if loading, register the listener; otherwise, call immediately. Without the guard, the listener-only pattern would silently no-op on every page where main.js loads after parse completes (i.e., every page on this site). **Rule: when registering DOMContentLoaded listeners from a `<script>` placed at end-of-body, ALWAYS gate on `document.readyState === 'loading'` and call the function immediately if not. Bare `addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', ...)` from end-of-body is a silent no-op.**

**What I learned:**

1. **iOS Safari autoplay has SIX layers, not five.** Promoted to CLAUDE.md. The full stack, in evaluation order at parse:
   1. `<video>` attributes (Mobile Video Standing Rule §1: 7-attribute pattern)
   2. Codec compliance (Mobile Video Standing Rule §2: H.264 Main / L4.0 / yuv420p / 30fps CFR)
   3. Container + MIME (Mobile Video Standing Rule §2: `.mp4`, not `.mov`)
   4. Parse-time occlusion (JS-State Classes Standing Rule: no opaque overlays at parse)
   5. Cascaded ancestor visibility (Mobile Video Standing Rule §6: no `opacity:0` / `display:none` / `visibility:hidden` / off-screen `transform` on any ancestor)
   6. **Parse-time `.play()` API invocation (NEW, this session): the `autoplay` attribute alone is not sufficient — for above-the-fold videos, an explicit `.play().catch(() => {})` call must run at `DOMContentLoaded` (or immediately if the DOM is already parsed). The IO retry only fires for videos that *scroll into view*; videos already in view at parse need a parse-time bootstrap.**

   AND a meta-layer: **(M) Asset cache invalidation (`?v=N` query string monotonic bump on all 8 HTML in lockstep)** — without this, none of the above six fixes propagate to a real iPhone past the deploy.

   All six gates + the cache layer must pass independently. Failing any one denies autoplay silently with no UI signal beyond "poster + play icon."

2. **Why this loop took five sessions to close.** The bug had multiple layered contributing causes. Each session's fix was real and necessary. But the surface symptom was identical across all six layers ("video shows play icon"), so each session's framing was indistinguishable from the previous. Without instrumentation, the only signal was "user reports it's still broken" — a binary, not a vector. Five rounds of attempt-and-report ate seven calendar days. The final session's instrumentation panel produced a vector: `userActivation:false`, `paused:true`, `lastPlayState:never-called`, `opacity:1`, `visibility:visible`, `readyState:4`. That vector pointed at exactly one remaining hypothesis (layer 6: no parse-time `.play()` call), and the fix took five minutes to write. **Rule promoted: identical symptoms across N fix sessions is a code smell. Switch protocols: stop fixing, build instrumentation, measure, then fix once with confidence.**

3. **The Recurring Bug Rule (NEW STANDING RULE in CLAUDE.md):** when a bug returns after fix attempt N (typically N≥2 for a single bug), the next session is **diagnostic-only**. No code changes intended to fix the bug. Instead: instrument the affected subsystem with explicit logging or a visible debug panel; reproduce on the real failing platform (iOS Safari, in this case); collect a vector of state — not just "broken vs not broken" but "what specifically is the value of every variable that could plausibly be wrong." Only after the diagnostic produces an unambiguous answer does the next fix session run. The diagnostic itself is throwaway code, removed in the same session as the fix. The cost is one session of "no progress on the bug." The benefit is closing the loop. Going in circles without measurement is a code smell that means the diagnostic model is wrong.

4. **Above-the-fold vs below-the-fold videos need different autoplay rescue strategies.** Below-the-fold: IO retry on `entry.isIntersecting`. Above-the-fold: parse-time `.play()` pass at `DOMContentLoaded`. Both are belt-and-suspenders; neither alone covers the full surface. Standing rule: any future video added to a page needs both rescue mechanisms in place. The current main.js has both — preserve them on any future refactor.

5. **DOMContentLoaded listener registration must be guarded for end-of-body scripts.** The pattern `if (document.readyState === 'loading') { addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', fn) } else { fn(); }` is the canonical wrapper. Without the else-branch, end-of-body scripts silently never register the listener. **Rule: every `addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', ...)` in main.js must be wrapped in this readyState guard.**

6. **Diagnostic instrumentation should be feature-flagged (`?debug=videos`), not always-on.** The 155-line debug block was gated on a URL query parameter — invisible to normal users, accessible to the developer with one URL change. After the diagnostic phase closes, the entire block is removed (this session). The pattern of "URL-flag-gated debug panels" is reusable across future debugging sessions on this site. **Rule: future diagnostic instrumentation goes behind a `?debug=<feature>` URL flag, lives until the bug is closed, then gets fully removed in the same session as the fix. Do NOT leave debug panels in production code "in case we need them later" — they rot, accumulate, and pollute the codebase.**

**Files touched:**
- `main.js` (CHANGE A: inserted 20-line `attemptVideoAutoplay` parse-time play() pass between STAGGERED LOAD ANIMATION and SCROLL REVEAL blocks at lines 284-304; CHANGE B: removed 155-line `?debug=videos` instrumentation block previously at lines 729-883 — net change −135 lines, 885 → 750)
- `index.html` (cache-bust `main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `404.html` (cache-bust `main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `about.html` (cache-bust `main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `process.html` (cache-bust `main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `work/lexisnexis.html` (cache-bust `../main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `work/pulse.html` (cache-bust `../main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `work/ghost.html` (cache-bust `../main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust `../main.js?v=16` → `?v=17`)
- `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (UPDATE 1: extended Mobile Video Standing Rule with rule #7 — Parse-time `.play()` invocation — covering DOMContentLoaded `.play()` pass for above-the-fold videos. UPDATE 2: NEW top-level "Recurring Bug Rule" standing rule covering the diagnostic-only protocol when a bug returns after fix attempt N)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)
- `_archive/_backup-autoplay-final-2026-04-30/` (NEW backup folder, 9 files: 8 HTML originals + main.js original, preserving `work/` subdir)

Explicitly untouched: `styles.css` (no CSS change — `.reveal:has(video)` rule from prior session at line 476 still correct; `styles.css?v=20` cache-bust unchanged), `game.js`, all video files (codec already compliant from Apr 29), all `<video>` element attributes (canonical 7-attribute pattern preserved), all image files, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, all other `_archive/_backup-*` directories, root `CLAUDE.md`. Image Layout Protocol respected (no image container changes). Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1-6 still hold (this session adds §7, doesn't modify the existing rules). JS-State Classes Standing Rule still holds (this session does not affect it). The morning's IO retry at [main.js:315](main.js#L315) preserved as defense-in-depth alongside the new parse-time pass at [main.js:297](main.js#L297) — two retry sites, complementary surfaces.

## 2026-04-30 — ATS Skills section + resume HTML source

**What I did:** Replaced the sparse two-paragraph Skills & Tools block on About with a 6-category ATS-optimized definition list (Design, Research, Process, Tools, Code, AI Workflow), and created a brand-new `resume/index.html` source file (one didn't exist — the PDF was generated from an external source that had been lost). Regenerated `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf` at 1 page using Chrome headless print-to-PDF.

**What worked:**
- **`<dl>/<dt>/<dd>` for ATS:** semantic definition list is the right primitive for category→skills mapping. ATS scrapers pick up `<dt>` as label and `<dd>` as values without needing class hooks. Reused `.experience-heading` typography (700/uppercase/letter-spacing 0.08em) for `<dt>` so the new section reads as a sister of the existing Experience section without inventing new tokens.
- **Reading the existing PDF directly via `Read` tool** to extract content + visual identity instead of asking the user. The PDF text layer was clean enough to reconstruct the section structure 1:1.
- **Chrome headless print-to-PDF invocation:**
  ```bash
  /Applications/Google\ Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome \
    --headless --disable-gpu --no-pdf-header-footer \
    --print-to-pdf="Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf" \
    "file:///absolute/path/to/resume/index.html"
  ```
  `--no-margins` is NOT a real Chrome flag — silently ignored. The valid flag is `--no-pdf-header-footer` (kills page-numbering chrome). CSS `@page { margin }` controls actual edge spacing.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Page-fit iteration took 5 passes.** Initial body line-height 1.42 + generous job/h2 margins put content ~9 lines over a single page. Each compression pass recovered ~2-3 lines: page margin 0.45in→0.25in, body line-height 1.42→1.18, ul li line-height to 1.2, h2 margin-top 12px→3px, additional section to 9pt. The skills-tight class (originally meant for skills-only fallback) became insufficient on its own — the whole document needed compression, not just the section that grew.
- **`mdls -name kMDItemNumberOfPages` returns CACHED Spotlight metadata.** After regenerating the PDF, mdls reported `2` even when the actual PDF was 1 page. `mdimport -r` (force re-import) plus a 1s sleep is required to refresh. The authoritative quick-check is `grep -aoE "/Count [0-9]+" PDF` against the raw PDF bytes — that reads the PDF root /Pages /Count entry directly.
- **Chrome bytes-written varied wildly across runs (48k → 277k → 290k).** The first PDF was small because the Satoshi font hadn't fully loaded over the network before print. Subsequent runs embedded the font, hence the ~270kb floor. Don't trust file-size as a proxy for content amount; verify visually OR via /Count.

**What I learned:**

1. **ATS keyword strategy hierarchy: visible HTML > meta tags > resume PDF text layer.** The About page Skills section in real DOM gets crawled by Google + every ATS bot that follows links. Resume PDF text layer is parsed by ATS systems that ingest uploaded PDFs. Meta tags only help search-engine snippet generation, not ATS. Put the keyword set in BOTH the HTML page and the PDF, with identical category structure, so a recruiter searching either entry point lands on the same canonical taxonomy.

2. **Six-category Skills structure for this site is canonical:** Design, Research, Process, Tools, Code, AI Workflow. Use it everywhere keywords are surfaced (about.html, resume/index.html, future LinkedIn export). Do NOT collapse "Process" back into "Design" or split "AI Workflow" into "Engineering" — that taxonomy was deliberately chosen to map to how 2026-era recruiter searches actually segment candidates.

3. **Resume HTML source belongs IN the repo at `resume/index.html`.** Treat it as a first-class artifact, not a one-off. Single self-contained HTML file with embedded `<style>` and `@page` for print. Output PDF goes to `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf` at root for the existing download link. Never delete the source again — it took an extra session to reconstruct it.

4. **For 1-page resume page-fit, compress globally, not just the new section.** The `skills-tight` class fallback strategy was wrong — when a new section pushes a 1-page resume to 2 pages, the document's line-height and margin reservoir need to absorb the new content. Default reservoir on this resume: `@page margin 0.25in 0.55in 0.22in`, body line-height 1.18, ul li line-height 1.2, h2 margin-top 3px / margin-bottom 1px / padding-bottom 1px. Those numbers fit the current 4-job + 2-project + 6-skills-category content envelope on letter paper. Adding another job or 2+ skills categories will require trimming bullets, not further CSS compression — the typography is already at the legibility floor.

5. **`--no-margins` is not a valid Chrome flag.** `--no-pdf-header-footer` is the real one. Confirmed by docs + behavior. CSS `@page { margin }` is what controls margins; the Chrome flag only affects browser-injected page chrome. Whenever a "headless print to PDF" recipe shows `--no-margins`, substitute mentally.

6. **Use raw-PDF `/Count` regex to verify page count, not `mdls`.** `mdls` is convenient but cache-stale; `grep -aoE "/Count [0-9]+" file.pdf | head -1` reads the PDF root catalog and is authoritative. The first /Count entry in any PDF is the document's page count.

**Files touched:**
- `about.html` (Skills section replaced; cache-bust v=20→v=21)
- `styles.css` (added `.about-skills .skills-grid` rules + mobile breakpoint variant)
- `index.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust only, v=20→v=21)
- `resume/index.html` (NEW — single self-contained source for the resume PDF)
- `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf` (regenerated)
- `_archive/_backup-ats-skills-2026-04-30/` (backup of about.html, styles.css, original PDF)

## 2026-04-30 — Cursor lag fix: viewport-scope the parse-time .play() pass + GPU-promote .custom-cursor

**What I did:** Constrained `attemptVideoAutoplay()` at [main.js:294-306](main.js#L294-L306) to only fire `.play()` on videos whose `getBoundingClientRect()` intersects the viewport (`rect.top < window.innerHeight && rect.bottom > 0`). Added `will-change: transform;` to `.custom-cursor` at [styles.css:334](styles.css#L334) to guarantee its own compositor layer. Cache-bust `main.js?v=17 → ?v=18` AND `styles.css?v=21 → ?v=22` across all 8 active HTML files in lockstep. Backup at `_archive/_backup-cursor-lag-fix-2026-04-30/`.

**What worked:**
1. **Diagnostic-first protocol applied correctly the first time.** User reported "site feels laggy after today's deploys, custom cursor lags." Per the Recurring Bug Rule, the first session output was a pure diagnostic — no code changes. The diagnostic produced a state vector across 7 dimensions: today's file mtimes, the parse-time `.play()` pass structure, custom-cursor JS+CSS, compositor-heavy CSS additions, skills-grid CSS, video count per page (Pulse=4, About=2, others=0–1 with weights), and reveal-IO observation count. The vector pointed at one most-likely cause (rank #1: parse-time `.play()` over all 4 Pulse videos saturating decoders → main-thread starvation → cursor RAF stutter), one contributing factor (`.custom-cursor` lacking `will-change`), and ruled out 4 lower-probability causes with reasoning. User approved fix on first proposal.
2. **The viewport filter is 4 lines of pseudo-trivial code** (`var rect = v.getBoundingClientRect(); if (rect.top < window.innerHeight && rect.bottom > 0) { ... }`), but its semantic effect is significant: on Pulse, decoder fan-out at parse drops from 4 simultaneous videos (~2.3 MB cumulative) to 1 (the above-fold `ai-fly.mp4` at 359 KB). On About, drops from 2 to 0 (Currently Building section is below fold; videos defer to IO retry on scroll). Layer 6 of the iOS autoplay stack is preserved for above-fold videos; below-fold videos fall through to the IO retry that was always handling them before today's parse-time pass landed.
3. **`will-change: transform` on `.custom-cursor` is defense-in-depth, not the primary fix.** Without it, the cursor was always vulnerable to main-thread starvation. With it, even if a future change re-introduces parse-time pressure, the cursor stays smooth because it's on its own compositor layer (transform-only updates skip paint). Pre-existing analogous use at [styles.css:167](styles.css#L167) for intro icons confirms the project's pattern.
4. **Cache-bust both files in lockstep.** Both `main.js` and `styles.css` changed; both query strings bumped (`?v=18` and `?v=22`) on all 8 HTML files. Without the lockstep, Vercel CDN + browser cache would serve mixed-version assets to returning users (e.g., new HTML pointing at old CSS) — a known foot-gun documented in the Mobile Video Standing Rule meta-layer (M).

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Six of the 16 cache-bust Edits failed on the first parallel batch** because the Edit tool requires a prior Read of each target file in the same conversation. Only `about.html` and `work/pulse.html` had been read during diagnostic; the other 6 needed a Read pass first. Resolved by reading the 6 files in parallel (single-line reads at the relevant offset), then re-running the failed Edits in the second batch. **Rule: when the planned set of Edits spans files not yet read in the conversation, the read pass and the edit pass cannot be batched into a single parallel call. Either read all targets up front, or split into read-batch → edit-batch.**
- **`diff` returns exit code 1 when files differ — that's expected, not an error.** My verification script chained `&&` so the bash command "failed" (exit 1) even though the diff output was correct. Cosmetic; results were still readable. **Rule: when verifying with `diff`, expect non-zero exit; use `; ` chaining or accept the non-zero exit code.**
- **The fix is structurally additive to Mobile Video Standing Rule §7, not a contradiction.** §7 said "the autoplay attribute alone is insufficient — fire `.play()` at parse." It did NOT specify scope. The unscoped implementation pattern shipped on Apr 30 evening was a bug in §7's canonical example, not a flaw in §7's principle. The principle still holds: above-fold videos need parse-time `.play()`. The scope correction here updates the canonical pattern to match the actual constraint. **Rule: standing-rule code examples are part of the rule's surface — when the example has a load-bearing scope decision, surface that decision explicitly in prose, not just in code.**

**What I learned:**

1. **Any `document.querySelectorAll('video').forEach(...)` operation should default to filtering by viewport visibility before firing.** The unscoped pattern is almost always wrong: it treats below-the-fold videos identically to above-the-fold ones, which is correct for nothing — autoplay rescue is viewport-bound (videos scroll into view), decoder budgets are viewport-bound (only visible videos need to be decoding), and main-thread budgets are viewport-bound. **Rule promoted to CLAUDE.md (extends Mobile Video Standing Rule §7): video operations that fire across the page MUST scope by viewport intersection. The canonical filter is `getBoundingClientRect().top < window.innerHeight && rect.bottom > 0`.**

2. **The cost of saturating media decoders shows up where you least expect it: in the custom cursor, not the videos themselves.** The videos play fine — they're handled by GPU/media threads. What suffers is the main thread, which gets clogged with media-element callbacks (`loadedmetadata`, `canplay`, `playing`) and Promise resolution storms. The most-RAF-dependent visible UI is the first to degrade. On this site, that's the lerp-driven custom cursor. **Rule: when reasoning about main-thread starvation, think about which UI is most-RAF-dependent — it's the canary that surfaces the problem before the user reports the actual cause.**

3. **`will-change: transform` is cheap defense-in-depth for any element animated via JS-set transforms in a RAF loop.** Compositor-layer promotion isolates the element from main-thread paint. Tiny memory cost (one extra layer); large smoothness payoff under main-thread pressure. **Rule: any DOM element that updates `transform` from a continuous RAF loop should declare `will-change: transform` in CSS. Currently applied: `.custom-cursor` (this session), `.intro-icon` (pre-existing).**

4. **Diagnostic-first paid off again.** The Recurring Bug Rule was promoted yesterday from a 7-day iOS autoplay loop. The first time it was applied to a different bug — and it worked: instead of jumping at "the cursor lag must be a JS bug, let me rewrite the lerp," the diagnostic identified that the cursor was the *symptom* and the parse-time `.play()` pass was the *cause*. **Rule re-confirmed: when the user reports a perf regression, the diagnostic-only first session is the right protocol. The temptation is to fix the most visible symptom; the discipline is to find the cause.**

5. **Two-changes-only scope held, with the cache-bust meta-layer included.** Source changes: 1 main.js function body (+5 lines, -1 line), 1 styles.css declaration (+1 line). Cache-bust: 16 strings across 8 HTML files. No HTML structure changes, no game.js, no video files, no `<video>` attribute changes. Diff against backup confirms zero collateral. **Rule re-confirmed: scope discipline is verified by `diff <BACKUP> <CURRENT>` filtered for the intentional change. Clean diff = scope held.**

**Files touched:**
- `main.js` (viewport filter added inside `attemptVideoAutoplay`; comment updated to reflect the SCOPE constraint)
- `styles.css` (added `will-change: transform` to `.custom-cursor`)
- `index.html`, `about.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust both `main.js?v=17→18` and `styles.css?v=21→22`)
- `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Mobile Video Standing Rule §7 updated to scoped canonical pattern + new sub-rule §7a on viewport-scoping any cross-page video operation)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)
- `_archive/_backup-cursor-lag-fix-2026-04-30/` (NEW backup folder, 10 files: 8 HTML + main.js + styles.css originals, preserves `work/` subdir)

Explicitly untouched: `game.js`, all video files, all `<video>` element attributes, all image files, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `resume/index.html`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, root `CLAUDE.md`, all other `_archive/*` directories. Image Layout Protocol respected (no image container changes). Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1–6 unchanged. JS-State Classes Standing Rule unchanged. Recurring Bug Rule applied (first non-autoplay use; protocol held).

## 2026-05-06 — LexisNexis bento thumbnail swap (audit priority #1) + dark-mode flashlight hedge

**What I did:** Replaced the LexisNexis homepage bento thumbnail — formerly a typographic statement (`--color-brand-primary : #de4721` / `FULL DAY → 30 SECONDS / 3 PEOPLE → 1 DESIGNER` text-on-card) — with a real product asset: the 4-column color-token reference (Red 700 / Blue 600 / Gray 500 / Green 600) cropped from `img/lexisnexis/new/color-palette.png` (1600×1338 source) to a clean 1600×900 16:9 frame via Pillow top-crop, saved as `img/lexisnexis/bento-thumbnail-v2.png`. Updated [index.html:169](index.html#L169) — src + alt rewrite + removed stale inline `style="object-position: center bottom;"`. Added one-line dark-mode hedge in styles.css to tame the "flashlight effect" the audit flagged on this card specifically: `[data-theme="dark"] .thumb-lexisnexis img { filter: brightness(0.88); }` at [styles.css:46](styles.css#L46). Cache-bust `styles.css?v=22 → ?v=23` across all 8 active HTML files in lockstep. Backup at `_archive/_backup-bento-lexis-thumb-2026-05-06/`.

**What worked:**
1. **Diagnostic-first paid off again — the audit's recall was slightly off, viewing the actual files corrected it.** The audit recommended "the dark-themed Figma token-library panel with Red / Blue / Gray / Green columns." Viewing the case study's image inventory directly revealed two real assets each holding part of that description: `new/figma-variables.png` is dark-themed (matches "dark-themed Figma panel") but portrait orientation (786×1470) — incompatible with 16:9 bento. `new/color-palette.png` is light-themed but holds the structurally-distinctive "Red/Blue/Gray/Green columns" signal that the audit explicitly flagged. Picked the structural signal over the theme/format match. Lesson: **audit recommendations describing internal assets must be verified against the actual asset inventory; recall conflations are common, structural signal beats decorative match.**
2. **Pillow top-crop preserved exactly the audit's signal.** Source `color-palette.png` is 1600×1338 (~1.20:1). Cropped top 0–900 keeps: column header swatches (Red 700, Blue 600, Gray 500, Green 600 — the four named columns the audit cited), hex values for each, and weight-steps 50–700. Lost only the bottom 700/800/900/950 dark rows. The cropped band reads as a richer, more saturated grid than a centered crop would have produced. **Rule: when cropping a token-palette / type-spec / system-reference image to a constrained ratio, top-align the crop — labels and column headers carry the read; the lightest/darkest scale endpoints are tribute, not signal.**
3. **One-line CSS dark-mode hedge.** `[data-theme="dark"] .thumb-lexisnexis img { filter: brightness(0.88); }` reduces compositor-output luminance of the white-surrounding image when sitting on the `#2A3440` thumb background. 12% brightness reduction is large enough to take the flashlight edge off, small enough to keep the column colors readable. Single-rule scope means trivially reversible; pattern transfers to any other "light image on dark theme card" situation on the site if it recurs. Did NOT need filter-precomposed-darken (that fights the cascade); did NOT need a dark-themed alternate asset (would have doubled file count for marginal gain).
4. **Asset-creation discipline held.** New file is `bento-thumbnail-v2.png`, not overwriting the existing `thumbnail.png` (which stays in place untouched — no longer referenced from any HTML, but preserved in case of revert). Filename change forces fresh fetch on every browser without a `?v=N` image cache-bust. The original thumbnail.png is also captured in the timestamped backup for true rollback safety.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Stale `alt` text on the existing image in [index.html:169](index.html#L169) didn't match the actual rendered image.** Pre-edit alt: "Pipeline diagram: Figma variables exporting as JSON into Storybook tokens." Actual image content: a typographic statement showing CSS variable syntax + the metric. The mismatch was probably a leftover from an earlier image swap where the alt wasn't updated. The fix here happens for free since the swap rewrites alt anyway, but **rule re-confirmed: every `<img>` swap must rewrite the alt to match the new asset; never inherit alt across image changes. Stale alt is an accessibility regression that hides under a passing visual review.**
- **The audit's "dark-themed" descriptor for the recommended image was wrong.** The strongest match for the audit's intended signal (`new/color-palette.png`) is light-themed. The audit's recall conflated `figma-variables.png` (dark, portrait, fewer-columns) and `color-palette.png` (light, near-square, 4-columns). **Rule: when an audit references a specific internal asset, verify the asset before quoting the audit's framing back into the work. The recommendation is the *signal*; the descriptor is the messenger and may be lossy.**
- **Aspect-ratio mismatch (1.20:1 vs 16:9) needed pre-cropping rather than relying on `object-fit: cover`.** With cover, the browser would have center-cropped vertically and clipped both the column headers AND the dark base-700/800/900/950 rows — losing the strongest top-of-image signal. Pre-cropping with explicit framing intent is one extra step at edit time that avoids fragile runtime layout decisions. **Rule: when source aspect ≠ container aspect AND the source has explicit semantic structure (headers, captions, axes, edges that carry meaning), pre-crop at build time. Don't push the framing decision to `object-fit`/`object-position` at runtime — those parameters are blunt tools that don't know which 33% of the image is the signal.**

**What I learned:**

1. **Bento thumbnails are load-bearing for visual coherence at the homepage tier.** The audit's gut-check score of 4/5 (vs 5/5) traced to one card breaking the "real product UI" rhythm of the other three. Replacing one image — 30 minutes of work — should lift that score because the lead card now matches the visual archetype of the other three (real-asset / real-photo / real-video). The lesson is structural: **on a 4-card bento grid, all four must be the same archetype; deviation reads as inconsistency to a high-speed scan, not as variety. Save the typographic-statement archetype for in-case-study heroes where the reader has already paid the attention cost to reach it.**

2. **Pre-cropping > `object-fit: cover` whenever the source has semantic structure.** `object-fit: cover` is correct for photographic content (lifestyle photos, abstract textures, environmental shots) where any sub-region is roughly equivalent. It is wrong for design specs, UI screenshots, charts, code panels, or any image where edges/headers/labels carry meaning. The 30-second `sips` or Pillow crop at edit time produces a sharper read than three layers of CSS positioning hacks. **Rule: ask "does the source have semantic structure?" before deferring framing to CSS. If yes → pre-crop with intent. If no → `object-fit: cover` is fine.**

3. **The `[data-theme="dark"] .thumb-X img { filter: brightness(N); }` pattern is a generalizable hedge for the dark-mode-flashlight problem.** When a portfolio site uses dark-themed shells with light-themed thumbnails (a deliberate choice to keep project color visible across modes), bright-image-on-dark-card creates the flashlight artifact. A 0.85–0.92 brightness filter on the image (NOT the whole card — the card's own colored bg should stay) softens the contrast without changing the color identity of the image. **Rule promotion candidate: if a future thumbnail swap on Pulse/Ghost/GoteFigure surfaces the same flashlight, apply the same one-line filter pattern keyed on the thumb-X selector.**

4. **Audit-driven workflow shape.** This was the first session where work was driven by a written audit document rather than a user-initiated symptom. Worked well: STEP 1 was "verify the audit's claims against the actual codebase" (cheap, surfaces audit's recall errors), STEP 2 was "translate the audit's recommendation into a concrete asset/edit pair" (slower, requires viewing actual files), STEP 3 was "execute the audit's priority #1 only" (scope discipline — did not jump to priority #2 or stretch fixes). **Rule: when working from a written audit, audit the audit before acting on it. Authors describe assets from memory, sometimes inaccurately. The codebase is authoritative.**

**Files touched:**
- `img/lexisnexis/bento-thumbnail-v2.png` (NEW, 1600×900, 223 KB; Pillow top-crop of `img/lexisnexis/new/color-palette.png`)
- `index.html` (line 169: src + alt rewrite + inline `style=` removed; line 24: cache-bust v=22→v=23)
- `styles.css` (line 46 NEW: `[data-theme="dark"] .thumb-lexisnexis img { filter: brightness(0.88); }`)
- `about.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust only, v=22→v=23)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)
- `_archive/_backup-bento-lexis-thumb-2026-05-06/` (NEW backup folder, 3 files: index.html + styles.css pre-edit, thumbnail-original.png preserving the original typographic asset for rollback)

Explicitly untouched: `main.js` (no JS change → no `?v=` bump on main.js — still at v=18), `game.js`, all video files, all `<video>` element attributes, `img/lexisnexis/thumbnail.png` (the original typographic asset, preserved on disk; no longer referenced from any HTML; could be deleted in a future cleanup pass), all other image files, `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `resume/index.html`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, root `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (no new standing rule promoted; this session's learnings are content/process patterns, not yet promotion-worthy after one occurrence). Image Layout Protocol respected (no image container class changes — `.bento-card-thumb` and `.thumb-lexisnexis` unchanged in structure, only added a dark-mode `filter` declaration). Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1–7b unchanged. JS-State Classes Standing Rule unchanged. Recurring Bug Rule not invoked (no recurring bug; this was a clean audit-driven swap).

## 2026-05-06 — Pulse Other Work img → video swap (cross-surface consistency)

**What I did:** Converted the Pulse Other Work cards on `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/ghost.html`, and `work/gotefigure.html` from `<img src="../img/pulse/v2/compare.png">` to a 7-attribute `<video>` element pointing at `../img/pulse/v2/videos/bento-thumbnail.mp4` (and matching poster). Same asset that the homepage bento Pulse card already uses ([index.html:178-196](index.html#L178-L196)) — 55 KB MP4, iOS-compliant from the 2026-04-29 codec re-encode session. Markup pattern matches the existing Ghost Other Work card verbatim. No CSS, JS, or cache-bust changes — HTML-only on three files.

**Trigger:** User reported Pulse Other Work cards showing static thumbnail while Ghost Other Work cards on the same pages autoplayed correctly. Diagnostic showed it was not an iOS autoplay-gate failure — the Pulse card was a literal `<img>`, not a `<video>`. Root cause traced to an explicit decision in the 2026-04-27 Pulse V2 integration session: "keep videos on the homepage bento only, use a static still for Other Work cards to avoid loading 3 video elements per case-study page." User explicitly overruled that perf trade-off this session: the additional 110 KB per page (55 KB × up to 2 Other Work videos) is negligible against the visual inconsistency cost.

**What worked:**
- **Diagnostic-only first session.** Followed the Recurring Bug Rule from [.claude/CLAUDE.md](.claude/CLAUDE.md): the first prompt explicitly forbade fix attempts and demanded a state vector. Outcome: in 4 grep/Read calls I had a complete picture (which 4 files, which 3 are affected, which markup pattern Ghost uses, which asset is the candidate). The fix took 3 Edit calls and was approved on the first proposal because the diff was unambiguous. **Rule re-confirmed: when symptoms look like an autoplay-gate bug but might be something simpler, the first session should verify the markup is even a `<video>` element before assuming an autoplay layer is failing.**
- **Reused the homepage bento asset (`bento-thumbnail.mp4`) verbatim.** Same MP4, same poster, same aria-label string. Cross-surface consistency is now perfect — homepage and case-study Other Work cards play the identical loop. **Rule: when a homepage thumbnail asset is iOS-compliant and sized correctly, it's also the right asset for Other Work cards. Don't generate a second video.**
- **Backups before edits, atomic 3-line `<img>` → 12-line `<video>` swap on each file.** Pre-edit copies of all three HTML files in `_archive/_backup-pulse-otherwork-video-2026-05-06/`. Each Edit was a tight diff (4-line context match, no risk of collision with other content).
- **Cascaded ancestor visibility (Mobile Video §6) verified before swap.** Walked DOM from new `<video>` to `<body>`: parent `.other-work.reveal` defaults to `opacity: 0`, but the `.reveal:has(video)` exemption rule from 2026-04-30 already covers this case (Ghost Other Work video on the same surface proves it works in production). No new CSS needed.

**What failed or surprised me:** Nothing surprising in this session. The 2026-04-27 perf decision ("keep Other Work as static") was the prior surprise that this session corrected — but it was a deliberate choice at the time, not a bug. The lesson is that perf-vs-consistency trade-offs made early in a refactor can become invisible inconsistencies later. The cost of the static decision was 1.5 weeks of "Pulse Other Work doesn't autoplay" being a latent inconsistency that nobody noticed until the user looked at it side-by-side with Ghost.

**What I learned:** Promote a permanent rule: **any project whose homepage bento card uses `<video>` MUST also use `<video>` for its Other Work cards on every case study that links to that project. No img/video asymmetry across surfaces.** Codified in [.claude/CLAUDE.md](.claude/CLAUDE.md) under a new "Cross-Surface Thumbnail Consistency Rule" section (added this session). The rule is testable with a single grep: every project that has a `<video>` in the homepage bento should produce zero `<img>` matches when grepped for its slug across all `work/*.html` Other Work blocks.

**Files touched:** `work/lexisnexis.html` (single Edit, line 186 `<img>` line replaced with 11-line `<video>` block at lines 186-196 — Pulse Other Work card; section block now spans 184-208 → 184-218; no other content changed), `work/ghost.html` (single Edit, line 198 `<img>` line replaced with 11-line `<video>` block at lines 198-208 — Pulse Other Work card; section block now spans 196-204 → 196-214; no other content changed), `work/gotefigure.html` (single Edit, line 168 `<img>` line replaced with 11-line `<video>` block at lines 168-178 — Pulse Other Work card; section block now spans 156-174 → 156-184; no other content changed), `_archive/_backup-pulse-otherwork-video-2026-05-06/` (NEW backup folder containing pre-edit copies of all three modified files: lexisnexis.html 17067 bytes, ghost.html 16289 bytes, gotefigure.html 12645 bytes), `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (NEW section "Cross-Surface Thumbnail Consistency Rule" added after JS-State Classes Standing Rule), `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry). Explicitly untouched: `index.html` (homepage Pulse bento was already a video — unchanged), `work/pulse.html` (no Pulse Other Work card on its own page — has LexisNexis + Ghost), `about.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `styles.css` (combined `img + video` sizing rule on `.other-work-card .project-thumbnail` from 2026-04-24 already supports video — no CSS edit needed), `main.js` (parse-time `.play()` pass and IO retry from 2026-04-30 evening already handle the new videos — no JS edit needed), `game.js`, all image/video assets (the `compare.png` formerly referenced is now an orphan on disk at `img/pulse/v2/compare.png` — preserved per zero-cost rollback convention), root `CLAUDE.md`, all other archive folders. **No cache-bust on `?v=` params** — HTML changes don't require it; only CSS/JS edits do.

**Verification:**
- All three case study pages: Pulse Other Work card now contains `<video>` with all 7 mobile autoplay attributes — confirmed via grep.
- Asset paths resolve: `img/pulse/v2/videos/bento-thumbnail.mp4` (55,099 bytes) and `bento-thumbnail-poster.jpg` (46,178 bytes) both exist.
- No `compare.png` references remain in any Other Work block (orphan PNG preserved on disk).
- Cascaded ancestor visibility on the new video: `.reveal:has(video)` exemption already in place; Ghost Other Work video on same surface proves the autoplay path works.


## 2026-05-06 — Ghost video frame shave + Pulse case-study lead-with-artifact restructure

**What I did:** Two audit-driven fixes before May 8th recruiter call. (1) Shaved hairline black outline rectangles off `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` (1224×688 → 1192×656, crop 16px each side) and `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` (960×540 → 948×534, crop 6px sides + 3px top/bottom — 16:9-preserving variant), regenerated both posters at t=0.5s. (2) Restructured `work/pulse.html` to lead with the Living Observatory artifact: swapped static `compare.png` hero for video `ai-fly.mp4`, added a 15-word caption, removed the now-duplicate ai-fly.mp4 from the body section. Added `.case-hero-image video` and `.case-hero-caption` CSS rules + mobile breakpoint variant. Cache-bust styles.css `?v=24 → ?v=25` across all 8 active HTML files in lockstep.

**What worked:**
1. **Diagnostic-first protocol caught a wrong premise twice on Fix A.** Initial prompt framed the Ghost frame issue as a black browser-chrome wrapper inside the video stream. Diagnostic showed: (i) the dark surroundings in the video are real Ghost product UI (AI ANALYSIS bar, VIEW tabs, AI Recommendation modal at t=6s), not decorative chrome, and (ii) cropdetect found only 4-6px hairline black borders. The user-prescribed crop strategy (target the "thick black frame") and the actual measurable issue (hairline outline rectangle, 4-6px) had to be reconciled before any re-encode. Final answer was a slightly-padded shave (16px each side on demo; 6/3px on thumb) that cleared the outline without losing product UI. **Rule re-confirmed: when a prompt prescribes a fix, treat the prescription as a hypothesis. Verify the actual measurable state before re-encoding.**
2. **`ffmpeg cropdetect=24:16:0` + 30-sample averaging is the canonical "find the black border" command.** The output `crop=W:H:X:Y` value that's stable across 20+ samples is the answer. Less than 5 samples = unstable. Demo result: `1216:688:4:0` stable across 30+ samples. Thumb result: `960:528:0:6` stable. Both findings consistent with my pixel-level Pillow analysis run earlier — independent verification.
3. **Multi-frame test extraction at t=2s/t=6s/t=8s caught the AI Recommendation modal appearance.** Single-frame test would have shown the t=2s state where the right column is empty and made a more aggressive crop look safe. Extracting at t=6s revealed the modal slides in there — confirming a wide crop would destroy the AI Fix narrative beat. **Rule: when test-cropping a video that may have time-varying content, extract frames at multiple timestamps spanning the full duration before approving the crop.**
4. **Hero swap diagnostic caught an invalid premise in the audit.** The audit said pulse.html "leads with three paragraphs of prose before a screenshot." Reading the actual file showed the case study DOES open with a hero image (`compare.png`). The audit was wrong on the symptom but right on the underlying signal — the hero was a static comparison view, not the Living Observatory the audit explicitly named. The fix was a hero asset swap, not a structural reorder. **Rule: read the actual file before accepting an audit's structural claim. Audit recall is often imprecise; ground truth is in the code.**
5. **Hero captions cut from 30 words to 15 on user feedback.** First proposal: "The Living Observatory — one canvas that fuses a procedural wireframe, a thermal heatmap, a time scrubber, and an AI prompt bar that flies a cursor to the issue you ask about." (32 words). User pushback: "Reference portfolios use 8-15 word hero captions, not paragraphs. The Overview section directly below the hero already carries the detailed framing." Final: "The Living Observatory — one canvas that fuses a wireframe, heatmap, time scrubber, and AI prompt bar." (16 words). The shorter version is more memorable AND defers detail to the Overview where it belongs. **Rule promoted to CLAUDE.md: hero captions are 8-15 words max. Anything longer competes with the artifact and bleeds into the role of the Overview prose below.**
6. **`.case-hero-image video` CSS rule was missing — needed to be added before the swap could render correctly.** [styles.css:861-867](styles.css#L861-L867) had a `.case-hero-image img` rule but no parallel video rule. Without the new rule, the video would render at native size and not fill the 16:7 hero box. Added a 5-line rule that mirrors the img rule (minus `object-position: center top` because video framing is typically center-composed). **Rule: when adding a new tag type to an existing styled container, audit the container for tag-specific rules and add a parallel rule before the markup change.**
7. **Lockstep cache-bust pattern held — `?v=24 → ?v=25` across all 8 HTML files in a single edit pass, verified with a single grep.** Pre-flight grep confirmed all 8 at v=24 (no drift). 8 parallel Reads then 8 sequential Edits (Edit's per-conversation Read requirement). Post-edit grep confirmed all 8 at v=25. Total time: ~2 minutes. Without lockstep, half the site would serve old CSS while half serves new — known foot-gun documented in Mobile Video Standing Rule meta-layer (M).

**What failed or surprised me:**
- **Two iterations of the wrong frame-source diagnosis on Fix A before getting it right.** Round 1: I interpreted the audit's "browser-chrome frame" as the AI ANALYSIS / VIEW tabs / right modal column, recommended a content-destructive crop. User corrected: it's a thin outline, use cropdetect. Round 2: cropdetect found 4-6px hairlines — I interpreted those as encoding bleed and recommended NO crop. User corrected: that IS the outline, just slightly under cropdetect's auto-conservative threshold; shave it with a small safety margin (16px instead of 4). The frame turned out to be a real visual — just so thin (4-6px on a 1224px-wide source) that my "is this even there?" instinct was too conservative. **Rule: when cropdetect finds N pixels of hairline border, the user may want N+10-20px of crop to clear it past any ambiguity. The "padded shave" is the safer default than the "exact crop" — outlines that are ambiguous to cropdetect are usually visible to the human eye.**
- **The audit's recall of pulse.html opening structure was wrong.** "Leads with three paragraphs of prose before a screenshot" — actually leads with `compare.png` hero, then title, then 2-paragraph Overview. Following the audit literally would mean restructuring an already-correct opening. The right move was to ask "what specifically about the current opening fails the audit's principle?" — answer: the hero is the wrong artifact (comparison view), not the absence of an artifact. Hero ASSET swap, not structural reorder. **Rule re-confirmed: when an audit prescribes a restructure, verify the current structure against the audit's claim before executing. Don't restructure a structure that's already in the recommended shape.**
- **The Pulse hero swap created an asymmetric situation: the body still had the same ai-fly.mp4 video that was now the hero.** Pre-fix, ai-fly.mp4 appeared once, in the body. Post-swap, it would appear twice — at hero AND at body — without a third edit. Caught this in the proposal phase and added the body-removal as Change 3. The body still has visual rhythm via the compare.mp4 (line 139) and the image-trio (line 147). **Rule: hero swaps must audit the body for the same asset and remove duplicates. New CLAUDE.md rule: hero artifact is unique to the hero, never repeated in the body.**

**What I learned:**

1. **Hero captions are an 8-15 word art form.** The 30-word draft was technically more informative but it doubled the cognitive cost of the hero. The 15-word final defers detail to the Overview directly below. Reference portfolios uniformly do this — the hero is artifact + label, not artifact + paragraph. **Promoted to CLAUDE.md as part of the new Case Study Opener Standing Rule §3.**

2. **Frame-shave crops on existing iOS-compliant videos preserve the codec stack.** Both source videos were already Main / L4.0 / yuv420p / 30fps CFR / faststart from the 2026-04-29 codec re-encode session. The crop pipeline `ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vf "crop=W:H:X:Y" -c:v libx264 -profile:v main -level 4.0 -pix_fmt yuv420p -r 30 -fps_mode cfr -crf <24|28> -preset medium -movflags +faststart -an output.mp4` re-encodes through the same codec settings, producing iOS-compliant output. CRF 24 for the case-study demo (preserves detail), CRF 28 for the thumbnail (smaller file, fine for 16:9 thumb). **Rule: when re-encoding existing iOS-compliant video for any reason (crop, color correction, etc.), preserve the codec settings exactly. Do not change profile/level/pix_fmt/fps — those were tuned for iOS Safari autoplay and the device-test pass should not need to be re-run.**

3. **Frame-source attribution requires evidence at three levels: video stream content (ffprobe + cropdetect), CSS rules (grep on container classes), and rendered DOM (browser inspector or screenshot).** When a "frame" is reported, all three levels need ruling out. On Fix A: cropdetect ruled out a thick frame in the video stream; CSS audit ruled out a frame-adding rule in styles.css; the actual finding was a thin outline at the very edge of the video stream that cropdetect could detect but conservatively undershot. The full diagnostic took 3 rounds because I jumped to "video stream is clean" too early — should have run cropdetect more aggressively (lower threshold) on round 1. **Rule: when investigating a "thin border" claim, run cropdetect with multiple threshold settings (`cropdetect=24:16:0`, `cropdetect=16:8:0`, `cropdetect=8:4:0`) and pick the most aggressive that produces stable output. The default `24:16:0` is conservative and may miss thin-outline cases.**

4. **Case Study Opener Standing Rule (NEW in CLAUDE.md):** Position 1 above-the-fold of any case study MUST be the strongest single visual artifact, not prose. Prefer video over static image when both exist. Hero captions ≤15 words. Hero artifact unique to hero (don't repeat in body). `.case-hero-image` supports both img and video children — each needs its own CSS rule. Audit checklist before shipping: first-scroll-shows-artifact, caption-≤15-words, no-body-duplicate, 7-video-attrs, no-opacity-0-ancestor, cache-bust-if-CSS-changed.

5. **Diagnostic-first reduces total session time even when it produces "no progress" rounds.** Fix A took 3 rounds of diagnostic before the right crop was approved; total elapsed time including all diagnostics + the final re-encode + posters + verification: ~25 min. If round 1 had jumped to a 16px crop without diagnostic, the user would have rejected (correctly — the audit said "thick black frame" and 16px doesn't match "thick" at first glance). The diagnostic rounds were what produced the shared state-vector that converged on "the frame IS thin, padded-shave is the right move." **Rule re-confirmed (Recurring Bug Rule applied to non-bug audit-driven work): when the user's mental model and the measurable state diverge, more diagnostic rounds are cheaper than wrong fix attempts.**

**Files touched:**
- `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo.mp4` (re-encode 1224×688 → 1192×656, crop=1192:656:16:16, codec preserved Main/L4.0/yuv420p/30fps CFR/faststart/no audio; 351 KB → 333 KB)
- `img/ghost/videos/ghost-demo-poster.jpg` (regenerated at t=0.5s; 60 KB → 39 KB)
- `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail.mp4` (re-encode 960×540 → 948×534, crop=948:534:6:3, codec preserved; 59 KB → 60 KB)
- `img/ghost/videos/ghost-thumbnail-poster.jpg` (regenerated at t=0.5s; 41 KB → 24 KB)
- `work/pulse.html` (Change 1: hero `<img src="compare.png">` → `<video src="ai-fly.mp4">` with 7 attrs + poster + aria-label. Change 2: `<p class="case-hero-caption">` inserted between hero and case-content-inner. Change 3: 7-line body video block removed at former lines 110-116. Cache-bust v=24→v=25 line 24.)
- `styles.css` (Add 1: `.case-hero-image video` rule after line 867 — 6 lines. Add 2: `.case-hero-caption` rule with mobile breakpoint variant — 10 lines + 1 line in mobile media query. Net +17 lines.)
- `index.html`, `about.html`, `process.html`, `404.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/ghost.html`, `work/gotefigure.html` (cache-bust v=24→v=25 line 24/40/15 respectively)
- `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (NEW "Case Study Opener Standing Rule" section added before "Cross-Surface Thumbnail Consistency Rule" — 6 sub-rules covering: artifact-not-prose at position 1; video preferred over static; captions ≤15 words; hero artifact unique; CSS rule pair for img+video; pre-ship audit checklist)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)
- `_archive/_backup-ghost-frame-shave-2026-05-06/` (NEW — 4 files: original ghost-demo.mp4, ghost-demo-poster.jpg, ghost-thumbnail.mp4, ghost-thumbnail-poster.jpg)
- `_archive/_backup-pulse-restructure-2026-05-06/` (NEW — 9 files: pre-edit pulse.html, styles.css + 7 other HTML files for cache-bust rollback, preserves work/ subdir)

Explicitly untouched: `main.js` (parse-time .play() pass + IO retry from 2026-04-30 already handle the new video — no JS edit needed), `game.js`, all other video files, all other image files (compare.png is now orphaned but preserved on disk for rollback), `Rotem_Gotlieb_Resume.pdf`, `resume/index.html`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, root `CLAUDE.md`, all other `_archive/_backup-*` directories. Image Layout Protocol respected (no image container class changes — the hero is `.case-hero-image` which already existed). Mobile Video Standing Rule §§1-7b unchanged (the new hero video satisfies all 6 gates + the meta-layer cache-bust). JS-State Classes Standing Rule unchanged. Cross-Surface Thumbnail Consistency Rule unchanged. Recurring Bug Rule applied at meta-level (Fix A's 3 diagnostic rounds before the right crop).


## 2026-05-13 — Phase 1 foundation fixes (impeccable audit prep)

**What I did:** Shipped 7 audit-driven foundation fixes targeting the 14→18+/20 health-score gap before next-week's recruiter review. Each change ran a strict STEP 1 diagnostic → STEP 2 propose → user approval → STEP 3 execute → STEP 4 verify cycle. **(E)** hero subtitle rewrite — killed "AI-native...end-to-end" buzz, replaced with Claude Code + /process route. **(F)** h1 rewrite — killed "the prototypes in between" filler, replaced with "Product designer who ships infrastructure other designers use" (mirrored in resume/index.html summary paragraph). **(B)** swapped broken `aria-labelledby="imageModalTitle"` for `aria-label="Image preview"` on 4 case study pages — WCAG 4.1.2 Name/Role/Value now satisfied. **(C)** bumped `--text-muted` light #888 → #6E6E6E and `--text-faint` light #999 → #717171; dark `--text-muted` #777 → #8E8E8E and `--text-faint` #555 → #8E8E8E — all four pass WCAG AA 4.5:1 (math-verified via WCAG 2.x formula). **(D)** touch-target bumps: `.theme-toggle` desktop 32→40, mobile NEW rule 44×44; `.resume-modal-pdfjs-btn` 28→40; mobile nav-link font 12→14. **(A)** prefers-reduced-motion full handling — JS gate at intro dispatch, custom-cursor block, and load-stagger zero; CSS global override block at end of styles.css. **(G)** footer "Design infrastructure is leverage" + "built with care" cliché replaced with status-bar `12:47pm in San Jose, California ∴` — live San Jose time updates every 60s via `Intl.DateTimeFormat` with `timeZone: 'America/Los_Angeles'`. Cache-bust lockstep `styles.css ?v=25→26` + `main.js ?v=18→19` across all 8 HTML at end-of-session. PDF resume regenerated via headless Chrome — verified new summary text via pypdf extraction.

**What worked:**

1. **Strict STEP-1-through-STEP-4 protocol on every change, even trivial ones.** Each of the 7 changes — including the 30-second copy edits (CHANGE E, F) — ran the full diagnostic / propose / approve / execute / verify cycle. Total session time: ~2.5h for 7 changes. The discipline caught: (a) a Vercel routing finding (`/process` 404s, must use `process.html`) in CHANGE E STEP 1, (b) a `.visually-hidden` utility class is missing on this site in CHANGE B STEP 1 (forcing the aria-label option), (c) a resume HTML mirror update needed in CHANGE F that wasn't in the original brief, (d) 404.html has no footer in CHANGE G STEP 1 (scope shrunk 8→7 HTML). **Rule re-confirmed: the diagnostic step is not protocol theater — it surfaces real findings that change the implementation. Especially valuable in audit-driven work where the brief's prescription is one hypothesis among several.**

2. **3-way branch pattern for runtime-flag-gated dispatch.** CHANGE A's intro dispatch went from a 2-way `if (shouldPlayIntro()) play() else skip()` into a clean 3-way `if (prefersReducedMotion) skip() else if (shouldPlayIntro()) play() else skip()`. Two branches share the same outcome (skip) but for different reasons — the reduce-motion path is explicit and audited separately from the played-recently path. **Rule promoted: when adding a third condition to a 2-way branch where two of the three outcomes are the same, prefer the explicit 3-way over a compound boolean (`if (prefersReducedMotion || !shouldPlayIntro())`). The explicit form documents intent; the compound hides "why we skipped." Especially valuable for accessibility flags where future maintainers need to know which branch handled reduce-motion.**

3. **Stagger zeroing pattern.** [main.js:283](main.js#L283) `var stagger = prefersReducedMotion ? 0 : 150;` replaced the literal `150` in the JS-driven `triggerLoadAnimations()` `setTimeout(... i * 150)`. Trivial 1-line change, completes the brief's spirit (sequential reveal IS motion even if each element appears instantly). **Rule: any JS-driven sequence-over-time (`setTimeout`, `setInterval`, RAF stagger) should gate on `prefersReducedMotion` the same way CSS animations do. The global CSS override at the end of styles.css doesn't reach JS-scheduled timing — JS owns its own reduce-motion gate.**

4. **`prefersReducedMotion` constant-once pattern.** Declared at the top of the IIFE ([main.js:12](main.js#L12)) and reused at three sites (intro dispatch line 247, stagger ternary line 283, cursor gate line 426). Avoids calling `matchMedia` three times AND lets future code reference the same variable. **Rule: any `window.matchMedia` query used in 2+ places should be lifted to a top-of-IIFE const. Cheaper, also centralizes the feature-query so future maintainers see one source of truth.** The current pattern does NOT live-update on user preference change (the brief did not specify) — added to the pending list as a Phase 2+ candidate (`mediaQueryList.addEventListener('change', ...)` for live response).

5. **Dead-CSS deletion as part of the edit.** CHANGE G removed `.footer-quote` and `.footer-location` CSS rules in the same diff that added `.footer-status-line` and `.footer-status-glyph`. Both old classes had zero remaining HTML references after the markup swap. Leaving them in would have been ~13 lines of dead code waiting for a future "what was this for?" investigation. **Rule: when an HTML markup swap orphans a CSS class, delete the rule in the same edit pass. Don't ship dead CSS. The CHANGE C pre-Phase-1 backup of styles.css preserves the rollback path, so deletion is safe.**

6. **"Do NOT overwrite the original Phase 1 backup" rule.** When multiple changes in the same session touch the same file (styles.css was touched by C / D / A / G; main.js was touched by A / G), only the FIRST change's STEP 3 backed up the file. Subsequent changes preserved that backup untouched. The pre-Phase-1 state of styles.css/main.js is the correct rollback point — re-copying mid-session would lose the rollback target. **Rule promoted: in multi-change sessions, the backup target list for each STEP 3 is "files NOT yet backed up this session." Files already backed up are preserved verbatim. The session's backup folder represents the pre-session snapshot, not a per-change checkpoint.**

7. **WCAG contrast math verified computationally before committing token changes.** CHANGE C STEP 1 ran the WCAG 2.x luminance formula in Python on all 4 proposed values BEFORE any token edit.
