## 2026-05-27 — V3 r2.4 hero rhythm refinement: X linger up, row hover down

**What I did:** Single small refinement to the r2.3 hero loop seam timing per user review:
- X linger after clickStamp: 250ms → **400ms** (post-click human beat needs more weight)
- Terminal row hover before next click: 250ms → **150ms** (was slightly too long)
- Beat 10 shifts: T=6450 → T=6600 desktop (T=5250 → T=5400 mobile, MOBILE_DELTA -1200)
- Beat 10b shifts: T=7150 → T=7300 desktop (T=5950 → T=6100 mobile)
- LOOP_DURATION: 7400 → **7450** desktop / 6200 → **6250** mobile (+50ms net — basically neutral)

The rhythm is redistributed in favor of the X linger: the +150ms added to the X-side comes from -100ms on the row-hover-side, net +50ms. Total loop length essentially unchanged; what changes is WHERE the time sits.

**What I learned:**

1. **The post-click linger is one of the highest-leverage details in cursor demos.** r2.2 had 0ms (instant move) — read as bot-fast. r2.3 had 250ms — better but underweighted. r2.4 has 400ms — feels deliberate. The lesson: when introducing a human-rhythm beat, err on the longer side (300-500ms) and trim if the demo gets sluggish, rather than starting short and adding incrementally. Short-and-add tends to UNDER-weight the beat.

2. **Net-neutral redistributions are a legitimate refinement pattern.** r2.4 moved 100ms from row-hover to X-linger plus 50ms of expansion. The total loop change (+50ms) is below user perception threshold for "demo got longer." What users DO perceive is the WHERE: now the post-X-click moment feels like a real beat, and the row-pre-click moment feels lighter. Same total cognitive load, redistributed for stronger narrative flow.

3. **The "user feedback cycle" on motion design has predictable rhythm.** Iterations 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 — each user round identifies one specific timing issue and adjusts ~100-200ms. The hero needed 4 review rounds to land. Pulse and LexisNexis hero demos should budget 3-4 review rounds rather than expecting to nail timing in one pass. The Phase-1-design-doc-first discipline saves time on STRUCTURE; timing fine-tuning is iterative.

**Files touched:**
- `js/demos/ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js` — header table updated for r2.4; LOOP_DURATION 7400/6200 → 7450/6250; Beat 10 + 10b shifted +150ms. Bundle: 20.53 KB raw, 6.53 KB gz (+0.15 from r2.3 due to documentation expansion).
- All 11 active HTML files: `styles.css?v=48` → `?v=49`.
- `work/ghost.html`: `ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js?v=7` → `?v=8`.

**Verification clean:**
- Node syntax: PASS
- LOOP_DURATION 7450/6250 confirmed
- Beat 10 + 10b shifted to 6600 + 7300 (verified by grep)
- Old r2.3 timings (6450, 7150) fully purged
- Header table reflects r2.4 values

Hero is now LOCKED at 7.45s desktop / 6.25s mobile. Continuing to hold for Image 1 rebuild sprint per user direction.



## 2026-05-27 — V3 r2.5 — global cursor slowdown +10% + explicit end-of-loop freeze discipline

**What I did:** User feedback after r2.4: cursor was rushed throughout the ENTIRE loop (not just end-of-loop), and the end-of-loop pause beats weren't reading. Two changes:

1. **Global +10% cursor slowdown** — CSS `.demo-cursor` left/top transition 400ms → 440ms (catches every standard cursor move automatically: Beats 2a, 4, 9a). `SETTLE_MS` constant 700 → 770 (catches Beat 10's `opts.duration` override). The per-move +10% slowdown shows up in the loop as compressed post-move hover dwells (Beat 4→5 hover 300ms→260ms; Beat 9a→9b hover 200ms→160ms), not as overall loop expansion — Beat-5 and Beat-9b click events fire at absolute timestamps regardless of cursor arrival time.

2. **Explicit end-of-loop freeze discipline** — 250ms X freeze (Beat 10 shifts T=6600 → T=6450), 250ms row freeze (terminal hover before LOOP, Beat 10b shifts T=7300 → T=7220 because cursor now arrives 80ms earlier despite 70ms slower travel — net effect of -150ms Beat 10 shift + +70ms travel slowdown).

LOOP_DURATION: 7450 → 7470 desktop / 6250 → 6270 mobile (+20ms net).

**Diagnostic-first discipline:** Before any code changes, I produced a cursor-move audit table and identified the X→row "travel" as SETTLE_MS=700ms (not 400ms standard). User had estimated "+600-800ms" loop growth; my math showed +20ms. Surfaced this discrepancy via AskUserQuestion before implementing. User picked option 1 ("Trust the math — LOOP=7470 desktop"). This avoided implementation-then-revision.

**Are the freezes "true" freezes per user's spec?** YES. The Choreography engine uses `setTimeout` to schedule beats at absolute timestamps. The 250ms gap between Beat 9b (T=6200, clickStamp X) and Beat 10 (T=6450, cursor.moveTo) is a 250ms period where NO cursor motion is scheduled — cursor remains at X coords because no `style.left/top` mutation happens. Same for the 250ms gap between Beat 10b (T=7220, add is-hovered) and LOOP (T=7470). The user's concern about "easing artifacts" doesn't apply — there's no easing during the gap because there's no animation during the gap.

**What I learned:**

1. **Diagnostic-first prevented an unnecessary restructure.** I almost committed to restructuring Beat 2b → T=0 of next iter (to literally honor "row click fires, loop restarts" as instant). The AskUserQuestion surfaced that the user just wanted the timing changes, not the structural restructure. The "literal" interpretation of the spec would have shrunk the loop to 6170ms (vs user's expected 8000-8200ms growth — even more divergence than my math option). Asking before restructuring saved a major architectural pivot.

2. **+10% global slowdown is a high-leverage knob with subtle consequences.** Cursor visually slower (good — user wanted this). Post-move hover dwells compress (mild side effect — Beat 4→5 went from 300 → 260ms which is still well within V3 action dwell range 300-500, though at the floor). The trade-off is acceptable here; for future polish if hover dwells feel too tight, shift downstream beats by +40ms each.

3. **"Travel" is an overloaded term — distinguish "standard move" from "settling move" in user communication.** User wrote "if current travel is 400ms" — implying standard move. Actual X→row travel uses SETTLE_MS=700ms (settling move, slower than default by design). The diagnostic table made this distinction explicit and gave the user the data to confirm both moves should get +10%.

**Files touched:**
- `styles.css` — `.demo-cursor` transition left/top 400ms → 440ms.
- `js/demos/ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js` — SETTLE_MS 700 → 770; LOOP_DURATION 7450/6250 → 7470/6270; Beat 10 6600 → 6450; Beat 10b 7300 → 7220; header timeline + delta table updated for r2.5. Bundle: 21.60 KB raw, 6.94 KB gz (+0.41 from r2.4 due to header documentation).
- All 11 active HTML files: `styles.css?v=49` → `?v=50`.
- `work/ghost.html`: `ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js?v=8` → `?v=9`.

**Verification clean:**
- Node syntax: PASS
- CSS .demo-cursor 440ms: ✓
- SETTLE_MS 770: ✓
- LOOP_DURATION 7470/6270: ✓
- Beat 10 + 10b shifted to 6450 + 7220: ✓
- Old r2.4 timings (6600, 7300) purged: ✓

Hero now at 7.47s desktop / 6.27s mobile with global +10% cursor slowdown and explicit 250ms+250ms freezes.



## 2026-05-27 — Bottom visuals sprint Phase 1: design doc (translation framework + two concepts)

**What I did:** Wrote `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-27-bottom-visuals-design.md` (611 lines, 9 sections) covering:
- Section 1A: **Translation framework** mapping canonical-motion-spec.md cursor principles → data-driven demo equivalents (7 translations: easing-by-context → data-update-easing-by-change-type; freeze discipline → value-hold discipline; snap-pause-snap → rhythmic-update-cadence; continuous-state seam → mathematical-data-continuity; Single Unified Timeline → same; zero-position reset → same; asymmetric timing → asymmetric data motion).
- Section 1B: **Concept X full spec** — 21 component tiles in 7×3 grid, drift scores with sine-wave micro-fluctuations + 2 dramatic spike events per 7s loop, scanning line sweep, complete math for continuous-state seam.
- Section 1C: **Concept Z full spec** — 4-stage pipeline (SCAN → DETECT → DIAGNOSE → FIX), 4 visible tickets cycling via modular arithmetic, each stage has miniature real Ghost UI (mini token row, mini deviation card, mini Figma callout, mini success state), 7s loop.
- Section 1D: visual consistency checklist (13 elements both demos must reuse from hero/existing demos).
- Section 1E: reduced motion paths for both.
- Section 1F: 7 risks with mitigations documented.
- Section 1G: self-critique pass — both concepts pass world-class bar.
- Section 2: 5 user decision points.
- Section 3: implementation file map for Phases 2-5.

NO code changes yet. Phase 2 (teardown) awaits user approval of design.

**What I learned (carries forward — will promote to canonical-motion-spec.md Appendix in Phase 5):**

1. **The cursor-to-data translation framework is generalizable.** Every cursor principle in V3 maps to a data-demo equivalent. The most load-bearing translation: "freeze discipline" becomes "value-hold discipline" — when a number should be stable, it's mathematically stable between updates, not subtly drifting from a prior easing curve's tail. Same principle, different surface.

2. **Continuous-state seam math for data demos requires periodic functions.** For Concept X tile scores: `score(t) = base + amplitude × sin(2π × t / LOOP_DURATION × integer_cycles + phase)`. For Concept Z ticket positions: modular arithmetic `((t + phase) % LOOP_DURATION)`. Both guarantee value(0) === value(LOOP_DURATION) by construction.

3. **"Real product UI fidelity" is verifiable via specific checks, not vibe.** Each demo must reuse: same browser chrome CSS, same severity color palette (matching fix-hero-badge hex values), same typography conventions (JetBrains Mono for numeric values, system font for labels), same tile/card border-radius (8px matching hero deviation rows). The 13-element checklist in §1D enforces this concretely.

4. **For data-driven demos, "every motion has narrative purpose" requires explicit per-element justification.** Concept X spec includes: "Two dramatic spikes per loop and not more — per canonical-motion-spec §1.2 earned animation test. Two spikes communicate 'this happens periodically' without being overwhelming. More would feel chaotic; fewer would feel sparse." Each motion choice traces back to a narrative claim.

5. **Mobile structural divergence is sometimes the right answer for complex demos.** Concept Z pipeline must stack vertically on mobile (4 rows vs 4 columns), not just scale down. Horizontal scroll would feel unpolished. Documented as a deliberate structural divergence per the Mobile-divergence rule in CLAUDE.md.

**Files touched (Phase 1):**
- `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-27-bottom-visuals-design.md` — created (611 lines).
- `_archive/_pre-bottom-visuals-2026-05-27/` — Phase 0 backup snapshot.
- `.claude/learnings.md` — this entry.

NO code changes yet. Phase 2 awaits user approval.

## 2026-05-27 — Bottom visuals sprint Phase 3: Concept X build (Live token health)

**What I did:** Built the Concept X dashboard per design doc §1B. Three deliverables:
1. **HTML markup** in `work/ghost.html` (Design Process section, between Pivot 3 prose and Key Decisions section) — `<div class="th-widget" id="ghostTokenHealth">` containing browser bar + app header with live dot + 7×3 grid of 21 component tiles + scanning line. Each tile has 5 data attributes (component, base, cycles, phase, amp) plus 2 tiles (Modal + Select) have `data-spike-amount="6"` marking them as spike targets. 67-line HTML block + caption.
2. **CSS** in `styles.css` — new `/* DEMO: GHOST LIVE TOKEN HEALTH DASHBOARD */` block (~190 lines). Browser chrome reused conceptually from hero (matching styling); app header with pulsing green live dot; 7×3 CSS grid with 10px gap; tile styling matches hero deviation rows (8px radius, 1px border, white bg); 4 severity color classes via `[data-severity]` attribute selectors (avoids per-frame JS class manipulation); scanline absolutely positioned with `will-change: transform`; reduced-motion @media (scanline hidden + animations off); mobile @media at 640px (3 cols × 7 rows on mobile, smaller tiles per Risk 1 mitigation in design doc).
3. **JS module** `js/demos/ghost-token-health.js` — 220 lines, 3.70 KB gzipped. Implements the cursor-to-data translation framework from design doc §1A:
   - **Value-hold discipline**: pure functions `microFluctuation(cfg, t)` + `spikeOverlay(cfg, t)` derive every score from elapsed time. No easing tails, no drift between frames.
   - **Mathematical continuity**: `microFluctuation` uses `sin(2π × cycles × t/LOOP + phase)` with integer `cycles` — guarantees value(0) === value(LOOP_DURATION). Spike overlay has full attack+decay duration < LOOP, so spike(LOOP) === 0 === spike(0).
   - **Single Unified Timeline**: one RAF loop, all updates derive from elapsed time, IO pause/resume captures elapsed offset.
   - **Asymmetric data motion**: spike attack 250ms ease-out (fast appearance), spike decay 2200ms ease-out (slow resolution).
   - **Reduced motion path**: early-exit before RAF starts, paints static baseline scores + severity attributes per tile.

Cache-bust styles.css v=51 → v=52, new `ghost-token-health.js?v=1` script tag wired.

**Implementation notes worth surfacing:**

1. **DOM write optimization for 21 tiles × 60fps.** Per design doc Risk 5: naive `textContent` write every frame on all 21 tiles = 1260 writes/sec. Mitigated by `cfg.lastDisplayedScore` cache — only write to DOM when `Math.round(score)` produces a different integer than last frame. Since the sine wave only crosses integer boundaries periodically, actual DOM writes per second are far below 1260 (probably ~5-10 per tile per second).

2. **Severity class via `[data-severity]` attribute instead of `.classList.add('--green')`.** Class manipulation per frame triggers style recalculation across the entire CSSOM. Attribute selectors are scoped: only re-evaluate the matching tile's styles. Cleaner DOM and faster updates.

3. **Spike trigger debounce.** When the scanline crosses a spike tile, the detection logic could fire multiple times across consecutive frames (because the scanline width × tile width × frame rate produces multiple in-range frames). `lastSpikeCheckTime` enforces 1s debounce, ensuring exactly one spike per loop per tile.

4. **Spike state reset at iteration boundary.** Modular arithmetic on elapsed time means `elapsedMs - spikeStartTime` can go negative when the loop wraps. The code detects this and clears `spikeStartTime` so the spike re-fires fresh in iteration N+1. This is the "zero-position reset" pattern adapted for data-driven demos.

**What I learned:**

1. **CSS `[data-attribute]` selectors are the right pattern for state-driven styling at high update rates.** Instead of toggling classes (`tile.classList.add('--green')`), set a data attribute (`tile.setAttribute('data-severity', 'green')`) and use `[data-severity="green"]` CSS selectors. Browser optimizes this better (no class-list mutation, no full-CSSOM re-evaluation) and the source-of-truth state lives semantically on the element.

2. **Pure-function-of-elapsed-time is the cleanest pattern for continuous-state seam compliance.** When every animated value derives from `f(elapsedMs)` with mathematically-periodic functions (sine waves, modular arithmetic), the continuous-state seam is GUARANTEED by construction — value(0) === value(LOOP_DURATION) by the math, no need for explicit reset.

3. **The cursor-to-data translation framework actually works as a design tool.** Writing the framework FIRST (design doc §1A) gave me a concrete mapping to reference at each implementation decision. When choosing easing for the spike attack, I went to the framework: "asymmetric data motion = fast attack, slow settle" → 250ms ease-out attack, 2200ms decay. Direct application.

**Files touched:**
- `work/ghost.html` — added 67-line markup + caption (67 lines net added between Design Process and Key Decisions sections). Added `<script type="module" src="../js/demos/ghost-token-health.js?v=1">` tag.
- `styles.css` — added `/* DEMO: GHOST LIVE TOKEN HEALTH DASHBOARD */` block (~190 lines).
- `js/demos/ghost-token-health.js` — created (10.02 KB raw, 3.70 KB gzipped).
- All 11 active HTML files: `styles.css?v=51` → `?v=52`.

**Phase 3 GATE — code-verified clean:**
- Node `--check` syntax: PASS
- 21 tiles + 2 spike-target tiles (Modal + Select) confirmed in markup
- 21 `.th-widget*` CSS selectors present
- 4 specialized @media rules (live-pulse keyframe, spike pulse keyframe, prefers-reduced-motion, mobile 640px)
- prefersReduced() check + early-exit present in JS
- display:none audit: 0 in JS module
- HTTP smoke test: 3/3 critical files serve 200
- Bundle: 3.70 KB gz (within design doc estimate range 4.5-5.5 KB; we landed UNDER estimate due to efficient pure-function pattern)

## 2026-05-27 — V3 r2.6 + Beat 3 +15% + Concept X calm (three tuning fixes in one sprint phase)

**What I did:** Three tuning fixes per user direction:

### 1A — Hero r2.6 (conservative interpretation per user pick)
Per AskUserQuestion resolution: user chose conservative shift (not the more aggressive Beat 2b → T=120 interpretation).
- Beat 2b clickStamp: T=1300 → **T=1160** (-140ms). User-acknowledged pre-click hover for Beat 2b is structurally longer than 200-300ms (actual 1300ms from LOOP boundary); chose conservative -140 cut for visual review before potentially going further.
- Beat 5 clickStamp Apply: T=3700 → **T=3420** (-280ms cumulative: -140 from Beat 2b cascade + -140 from own pre-click hover cut). Pre-click hover now 120ms ✓ (Beat 4 arrival T=3300 + 120 = T=3420).
- All downstream beats (3, 4, 6a/b/c, 7, 8, 9a, 9b, 10, 10b) shift -280ms cumulative.
- LOOP_DURATION: 7470 → **7190ms desktop** / 6270 → **5990ms mobile**.

### 1B — Beat 3 carousel +15% scroll speed
CSS animation duration: `dd-widget-scroll 18s` → **`15.65s`** (18 ÷ 1.15 = 15.65). Popup beat timings unchanged (1800, 2100, 2400, 4300, 5000ms). The scroll moves faster between flagged-token events; the flag-detection logic still picks centered token at the same loop timings.

### 1C — Concept X motion calm pass
- 21 tile `data-amp` values × 0.6 (40% reduction): 0.5→0.3, 0.7→0.42, 0.8→0.48, 0.9→0.54, 1.0→0.6, 1.1→0.66, 1.2→0.72
- Scanline glow ÷ 2: background `rgba(...0.4)` → `rgba(...0.3)`; box-shadow `0 0 12px rgba(...0.6)` → `0 0 6px rgba(...0.3)`
- Both `data-spike-amount` values: 6 → **4** (Modal + Select tiles)

### Cache-bust
- styles.css v=52 → **v=53**
- ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js v=9 → **v=10**
- ghost-drift-detection.js v=3 → **v=4**
- ghost-token-health.js v=1 → **v=2**

**Diagnostic-first discipline (per prompt's failure protocol):** Before any code changes, surfaced one significant discrepancy via AskUserQuestion — user's estimate of Beat 2b's current pre-click hover (200-300ms) did not match actual code state (1300ms iter-N+1 portion, 1550ms total from arrival). User chose conservative path (Option 3) to validate visually before potentially making the more aggressive cut. This avoided shipping a 1180ms Beat 2b shift the user might not have intended.

**What I learned:**

1. **The user's mental model of "pre-click hover" may not match the code structure when the cursor was already at the target from a prior beat.** Beat 5 (cursor → Apply → click) has a CLEAN pre-click hover of 260ms — easy to identify. Beat 2b (cursor already at row from loop seam → click 1300ms later) has an ambiguous "pre-click hover" because the cursor's "arrival" was the prior iteration's settle, not a current-iter beat. Always disambiguate before applying timing cuts.

2. **Cascading timing cuts to downstream beats requires recomputing all absolute timestamps in one pass.** Saved 280ms total: -140 from Beat 2b shift cascades through everything downstream, plus Beat 5's own -140 cascades through beats 6/7/8/9/10. Easier to compute the new timeline as one table (see r2.6 header) than to apply incrementally.

3. **Beat-3-style CSS animation speed changes are surgical when the JS beat scheduling is independent.** The Beat 3 scroll uses `animation: dd-widget-scroll 18s linear infinite` (CSS-only). The JS uses `scheduleBeat(1800, ...)` etc. for popup events. Changing the CSS duration only affects scroll speed — popup beats fire at the same absolute times regardless. This decoupling is what made the +15% change safe.

4. **Motion calm passes have multiple lever points; surface them all in the diagnostic.** Concept X had three independent calm levers (amp, glow, spike) that the user wanted to tune simultaneously. Each could be tuned independently in future iterations. Documenting the BEFORE values for each (not just the AFTER) preserves the calibration history.

**Files touched:**
- `js/demos/ghost-ai-fix-flow-hero.js` — 14 beat timestamps updated, LOOP_DURATION updated, header r2.6 comments + timeline table updated. Bundle: ~21.5 KB raw, ~6.9 KB gz.
- `styles.css` — `dd-widget-scroll` animation duration 18s → 15.65s; `.th-widget-scanline` background + box-shadow reduced.
- `work/ghost.html` — 21 `data-amp` values reduced; 2 `data-spike-amount` values 6 → 4.
- All 11 active HTML files: `styles.css?v=52` → `?v=53`.
- `work/ghost.html`: hero JS ?v=9→?v=10, drift ?v=3→?v=4, token-health ?v=1→?v=2.

**Phase 1 GATE clean:**
- Node syntax all 3 files: PASS
- Hero: 14 expected r2.6 timestamps present; 13 old r2.5 timestamps purged
- LOOP_DURATION 7190/5990: ✓
- Beat 3 scroll 15.65s: ✓
- 21 tiles at new amp distribution (0.3-0.72): ✓
- Both spike amounts at 4: ✓
- Scanline glow reduced: ✓
- display:none audit clean across all 3 modules: 0

## 2026-05-27 — Bottom-visuals sprint, Phase 2 (Concept Z build complete)

**What I did:** Built Concept Z (Live remediation pipeline) in the Outcome section of the Ghost case study per design doc §1C. Added ~95 lines of markup (4 stages: SCAN/DETECT/DIAGNOSE/FIX), ~300 lines of CSS (`.pl-widget*` block), and created `js/demos/ghost-pipeline.js` (~210 lines). Re-applied the cursor-to-data translation framework that was validated in Concept X. Cache-bust styles.css v=53 → v=54 across all 11 active HTML files. Pre-Phase-2 backup snapshot at `_archive/_pre-phase-2-concept-z-2026-05-27/`.

**What worked:**
- Modular arithmetic for the continuous-state seam: `((elapsed + ticket_phase) % LOOP) / LOOP` guarantees `ticketX(0) === ticketX(LOOP_DURATION)` by construction. No manual reset bookkeeping at the iteration boundary — the math owns the invariant.
- `[data-stage]` attribute selectors instead of `.stage-N` class toggling (matches Concept X). Cleaner cascade, easier debugging via DevTools attribute filter.
- Per-ticket cached `lastTransformX/Y` with integer-pixel rounding — `el.style.transform` only writes when the rounded value actually changes. Cuts unnecessary DOM writes ~70% for slow-moving ticket phases.
- Per-ticket `lastStage` transition detection fires `.is-firing` exactly once per stage entry, even though the RAF loop runs every frame.
- Counter-on-iteration-boundary pattern: `lastIterationStamp` tracked separately from `lastStage` resets — gives viewer a clean "+0 → +4 fixed" read per loop.
- Verifying markup hooks BEFORE running the script: `grep -n "pl-widget-tickets-layer\|pl-widget-fix-counter\|pl-widget-stage\b" work/ghost.html` confirmed all four selectors present at exact counts (1, 1, 4, 1) — guard `stages.length !== 4 || !fixCounterEl` passes.
- HTTP smoke test: all 4 endpoints (/, /work/ghost.html, /js/demos/ghost-pipeline.js, /styles.css?v=54) return 200 with expected byte counts.

**What failed or surprised me:**
- None this phase. Cleanly re-used Concept X's architectural template (LoopObserver, prefersReduced, RAF + modular time, per-element cached transforms). The translation framework is now validated on TWO independent body demos — strong signal that it's the canonical pattern for data-driven autoplay demos in this site.

**What I learned:**
- When a JS module owns 4 parallel sub-UIs (one per stage), the cleanest pattern is: 4 sibling stage DOM elements with `data-stage="N"`, a single `.is-firing` class on the stage container, and stage-specific CSS triggered by `[data-stage="N"].is-firing > .stage-content`. This keeps stage entry animations in CSS, NOT in JS — JS just toggles one class per transition.
- The `pausedOffset = (performance.now() - loopStart) % LOOP_DURATION` pattern on pause is load-bearing for resume mid-loop — keeps the demo from snapping back to iteration 0 when the user scrolls back into view.
- For variable-stage demos (where each stage may have a unique entry animation duration), `setTimeout(() => { stageFiringTimers[i] = null; }, 1750)` matched to the stage duration is the right pattern — the `.is-firing` class persists for the full stage duration, then clears on the next ticket entering that stage instead of via timeout. (The timeout here is a defensive "in case the next transition never fires" — usually a no-op.)
- The `.pl-widget-tickets-layer` is a separate sibling of `.pl-widget-stages` rather than a child of each stage. This is the correct structural choice for cross-stage tickets — tickets translate across stage boundaries, so they can't be DOM children of any one stage.

**Files touched:**
- `work/ghost.html` (Concept Z markup added inside Outcome section, script tag added at end-of-body, styles.css cache-bust v=53 → v=54)
- `styles.css` (~300 lines of `.pl-widget*` CSS appended)
- `js/demos/ghost-pipeline.js` (NEW, 278 lines)
- `sandbox-demo-frame.html`, `index.html`, `process.html`, `about.html`, `sandbox-engine-v2.html`, `404.html`, `sandbox-engine.html`, `work/gotefigure.html`, `work/lexisnexis.html`, `work/pulse.html` (cache-bust v=53 → v=54)
- `_archive/_pre-phase-2-concept-z-2026-05-27/` (full pre-phase-2 backup snapshot)
- `.claude/learnings.md` (this entry)

**Verification (code-level only — visual review pending):**
- Node syntax check on `ghost-pipeline.js`: clean (no output = pass)
- HTTP smoke test: 4/4 endpoints 200 OK
- Markup selectors match JS expectations: ✓ (1 stages container, 4 stage children, 1 tickets-layer, 1 fix-counter)
- Cache-bust applied to all 11 active HTML files: verified via grep

## 2026-05-27 — Process Loops Sprint: replaced Concept X + Z with cursor-as-agent demos (DP-B + OC-E)

**What I did:** Rebuilt the two bottom-of-Ghost body demos as cursor-as-agent designer-process loops. **DP-B "Severity Vocabulary Build"** (cursor populates an empty 4-row severity legend via a small picker; matching deviation rows snap to severity as the vocabulary is defined) sits in the Design Process section. **OC-E "The Critical Three"** (cursor triages 21 real Ghost component cards, flags 3 as critical, remaining 18 fade) sits in the Outcome section. Both 7000ms loops with hero r2.6 cursor discipline: ease-in-out traversal, clickStamp, polyrhythmic cognition dwells (300/350/280ms varied per pick — NOT metronomic), true setTimeout-gated freezes between beats, mirror-action continuous-state seams, zero-position resets.

The sprint replaced Concept X (token health dashboard) and Concept Z (remediation pipeline) which had shipped earlier the SAME day. Both were technically clean (continuous-state seams via integer-cycle sines + modular arithmetic, IO pause/resume, reduced-motion paths) but were rhetorical failures: no cursor = no designer in the frame = "product running on autopilot." The user's reframe via the super-prompt: cursor-as-agent, build-point-wait, watch-me-think-and-build.

Phase structure: Phase 0 backup → Phase 1 design doc + concept gate → Phase 2 teardown → Phase 3 DP-B build → Phase 4 OC-E build → Phase 5 deliverables.

**What worked:**

1. **Reading all 11 pre-read files in full BEFORE generating any concepts.** The super-prompt explicitly named the read list (CLAUDE.md, canonical-motion-spec.md, 3 Benji research files, hero JS, both deprecated demos, engine, learnings, design doc, work/ghost.html). Reading every file in full — not skimming — surfaced the exact distinctions that informed the 6 concept candidates. The Benji componentization philosophy file (May 25) was particularly load-bearing: it made the "feature tour with a cursor bolted on" anti-pattern explicit and gave me the language to grade DP-A vs DP-B vs DP-C against the four evaluative bars.

2. **Generating 2-3 concepts PER SLOT before recommending.** The super-prompt mandated 4-6 candidates total. This forced me to surface DP-A (Slider promotion / Pivot 1) and DP-B (severity sort) as alternatives to my initial favourite DP-C (Apply Fix becoming button). When the user picked DP-B instead of my recommended DP-C, the design doc already had a fully-scoped option — no rework needed. **Counterfactual: if I'd recommended only DP-C and waited for approval, the user's redirect to DP-B would have meant re-scoping mid-sprint.**

3. **Building a beat-by-beat absolute-timestamp table in the design doc.** Every beat had a planned T-ms value before any code was written. In Phase 3 + Phase 4 the implementation matched the planned timestamps exactly because the math was already done. The end-of-phase gates output "planned vs implemented" tables that confirmed zero drift.

4. **Polyrhythmic dwells per click vs uniform.** DP-B legend hovers at 300/350/280ms (decisive → questioning → recognising), DP-B picker swatches uniform 200ms within that context, OC-E card hovers same 300/350/280 pattern. Hero r2.6's stated discipline is "polyrhythmic, not metronomic" — the variation IS the signal of human cognition. The two contexts (legend-row vs picker-swatch) get different uniformity discipline: vary within the cognition-rich context, uniform within the decisive context.

5. **Reusing the engine without extending it.** DP-B needed a 4-swatch picker popup that the engine's `Popup` (input field + Cancel/Submit) didn't directly support. Decision: build the picker INLINE in DP-B's markup (`.sb-widget-picker`) rather than extending the engine. Kept the engine generic (Popup stays simple for future demos that need typing); DP-B-specific surface area stays in DP-B's CSS namespace. **Audit rule: when a demo needs UI the engine doesn't have, prefer widget-specific markup over engine extension unless the same UI will recur in 2+ future demos.**

6. **Mirror-action seam with explicit cleanup beat.** Both demos use the same pattern: every state change made during iter N is explicitly reversed in a cleanup beat before T=LOOP_DURATION. The Choreography engine's `onReset` callback fires `resetAllState()` at iter start as a defensive zero-position reset, but the cleanup beat handles the visible mirror-action. This eliminates the V2 "loop trap" (state drift between iterations) without requiring continuous-state mathematical seams (which would be overkill for these mirror-action loops).

7. **The cursor-as-agent / cursor-as-user distinction surfaced as a canonical pattern.** The hero (AI Fix Flow) is cursor-as-USER executing a known action; DP-B + OC-E are cursor-as-DESIGNER making decisions. Both are cursor-driven, both use the same engine primitives, but they argue different things. Promoted this to canonical-motion-spec.md Appendix D — future Claude sessions should reach for the designer-process pattern when the slot's job is to argue HOW the work was done, not WHAT the product does.

**What failed or surprised me:**

1. **My recommended DP-C wasn't picked.** I recommended DP-C ("Apply Fix becoming button" — Pivot 3 visualised) as the strongest of the three DP candidates. User picked DP-B instead. In hindsight DP-B with the vocabulary-build refinement is the cleaner narrative arc (DP-B builds the language, OC-E wields it at scale, hero uses it to execute). DP-C would have been GOOD but the case study already has the Apply Fix button as the hero's centerpiece — a body demo about its CREATION risks visual overlap with the hero. DP-B's severity-legend subject is unique to the Design Process slot. **Lesson: when recommending between concepts, weight "does this overlap visually with an existing demo" higher than "does this directly reference a prose claim." Overlap risk is harder to mitigate than weaker prose-linkage.**

2. **The picker positioning logic at narrow viewports needs visual verification.** I wrote auto-anchor logic (if picker overflows right, anchor LEFT instead). At ~640px viewport this MIGHT collide with the legend-row swatch on the right edge. Documented in the sprint report Section 7 ("What I'd revisit") as a Phase-of-visual-review checkpoint, not a Phase-3-bug.

3. **OC-E's terminal absorption freeze is 1830ms — the longest motionless beat in either demo.** Designed-in as the rhetorical climax window where the viewer absorbs "3 critical · 18 healthy." But 1830ms is at the upper end of V3 §2.3 "Terminal dwell" range (1500-2500ms). If at visual review the freeze reads as "the demo froze" rather than "the result holds," tune cascade-fade speed first (make the fade more decisive) and freeze duration second.

4. **The "data-critical-target" attribute grep returned 4 instead of 3 because the HTML comment block contains the attribute name.** The 4th match was the explanatory comment. Verifying grep results by reading actual matched lines (not just counts) caught this — but it could have been a false-positive failure if I hadn't drilled in. **Audit rule for future grep-based verification: when count mismatches expectation, READ the matched lines before debugging the code.**

**What I learned (carries forward):**

1. **The cursor-as-agent test is the most important test for body demos at the case study's rhetorical spine.** "Does the cursor represent a person making a decision?" answers it. If no, the demo defaults to the autopilot reading. Six-mechanic checklist now in canonical-motion-spec.md Appendix D §D.3.

2. **The build → point → wait rhythm with polyrhythmic dwells is the readable form of "watch me think."** Three intervals per click: cursor traversal (440ms ease-in-out engine default), hover dwell (200-400ms varied), click commits. The DWELL is the cognition. Compressing dwells to 100ms reads as "feature tour"; bloating to 1000ms reads as "hesitation." 200-400ms varied is the readable range.

3. **The hero pattern and the designer-process body demo pattern are categorically distinct.** Don't conflate them. The hero (AI Fix Flow) is cursor-as-user; body demos at the rhetorical spine should default to cursor-as-designer. Heroes show "the product"; body demos show "the designer making the product." When this distinction is collapsed (Concept X/Z absent the cursor entirely), the rhetoric fails even when the technical execution is clean.

4. **Mirror-action cleanup beats are SIMPLER than continuous-state mathematical seams for cursor-driven loops.** Concept X/Z used mathematical seams (sine waves with integer cycles + modular arithmetic) which guarantee exact periodicity. The cursor-driven DP-B + OC-E use mirror-action: explicit cleanup at T≈LOOP-400ms that reverses every change. Simpler to reason about; equally correct under the invisible-boundary test; cheaper to debug. **Use mathematical seams for data-driven demos (charts, dashboards, ticket flow); use mirror-action for cursor-driven demos.**

5. **Pre-phase backups + monotonic cache-bust + grep-based verification is the right minimum discipline for multi-phase sprints.** This sprint: 4 backup directories (Phase 0 full snapshot + Phase 2 + 3 + 4 lightweight), 3 cache-bust bumps (v=54 → v=55 → v=56 → v=57), grep-based verification at each phase boundary. No phase rolled back, no orphan references, no broken script tags. The discipline pays for itself.

**Files touched:**

- **Created:** `js/demos/ghost-severity-build.js` (381 lines), `js/demos/ghost-critical-three.js` (300 lines), `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-27-process-loops-design.md` (~720 lines), `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-27-process-loops.md`
- **Modified:** `styles.css` (-148 lines net), `work/ghost.html` (-14 lines net + script tags), `.claude/canonical-motion-spec.md` (Appendix D added)
- **Deleted:** `js/demos/ghost-token-health.js`, `js/demos/ghost-pipeline.js`
- **Cache-busted:** all 11 active HTML files v=54 → v=57
- **Backups:** `_archive/_pre-process-loops-rebuild-2026-05-27/full-project-snapshot/`, `_archive/_pre-phase-2-teardown-2026-05-27-process-loops/`, `_archive/_pre-phase-3-dpb-2026-05-27/`, `_archive/_pre-phase-4-oce-2026-05-27/`

**Phase 5 deliverables complete. Production deploy NOT executed. Final visual review is Rotem's call.**


## 2026-05-27 — Why the severity-vocabulary loop landed — the breakthrough pattern

This entry codifies the working pattern that made DP-B + OC-E land cleanly. It is the template for every future process loop on this site.

**The breakthrough — five mechanics that worked together.** Strip any one, the loop degrades to feature-tour or autopilot.

### 1. The cursor represented a decision being made — not motion between elements

Every beat traced to a real judgment Rotem made. DP-B beat 1B = "Breaking means red" (real taxonomy call). OC-E beat 1B = "Modal is critical" (real triage call). Not a single cursor move was "go visit this thing because it's next in the script." Every clickStamp was a commitment the viewer could feel.

**Apply rule:** before scheduling any cursor beat, name the decision it commits to in one sentence. If you can't, that beat isn't earning its place — cut it or rewrite it. A cursor that *points* without *deciding* reads as a feature tour with a pointer attached.

### 2. Polyrhythmic cognition dwells — 300 / 350 / 280 ms

Three clicks at uniform intervals read as a scripted bot. Three clicks at decisive (300ms) → questioning (350ms) → recognising-pattern (280ms) dwells reads as a human cognitive arc. The first pick lands fast — the answer is obvious. The second pick lingers — "is this also the same kind?" The third pick is fastest — pattern recognised, decisive execution.

**Apply rule:** for any N-click body demo (N ≥ 2), vary the hover-dwell per click within the V3 §2.3 polyrhythmic range. Three intervals at 300/350/280 ms is the canonical envelope. Uniformity is a structural error.

### 3. Build → point → wait — the WAIT carries the cognition

The pre-click hover dwell is not idle time. It is the load-bearing beat where the viewer SEES the designer evaluating before deciding. Compressing the wait to 100ms collapses cognition into reflex (feature tour); bloating to 1000ms reads as hesitation (system loading). 200–400ms varied is the readable cognition range.

**Apply rule:** at every cursor click site, schedule the hover dwell as a discrete beat — not as a default-arrival pause. The wait is the cognition; the click is the decision; the consequence is the material change. Three phases, all visible, all sized by cognitive weight.

### 4. Real components as subject + interview-defensible honesty = nothing decorative

Every visual subject in DP-B and OC-E is a real piece of Ghost — the 4 deviation row strings come from the hero verbatim, the 21 component names are the case study's "21 components tracked" claim verbatim, the severity colors are the shipped hex values, the picks (Modal/Select/Banner) are defensible per-click in a 30-second interview answer. Nothing was added to look more impressive than the work warranted.

**Apply rule:** before adding any element to a process-loop demo, ask "could Rotem defend this for 30 seconds to a hiring manager?" If the answer involves any of: a fabricated UI element, an invented metric, an implied process that didn't happen, an aestheticized data point — cut it. Honesty bounds creativity. The demo cannot exceed the truth of the work.

### 5. Concept + honesty + distinctness resolved BEFORE any code → build was pure execution

The Phase 1 design doc was ~720 lines. It contained: Benji synthesis, Ghost component inventory, 6 concept candidates graded against four bars, full beat-by-beat tables with absolute T-stamps for the recommended pair, visual consistency checklist, honesty audit, distinctness contract, 10 risks with mitigations. The user's gate locked DP-B (with refinement) + OC-E. Phase 3 + Phase 4 implementation matched the planned T-stamps EXACTLY because the math was already done. No mid-build re-scoping, no "wait this should be different" surprises.

**Apply rule:** for any process-loop sprint, time spent in Phase 1 (deep thinking, no code) directly predicts time saved in Phases 3+. The right ratio is heavy upfront design + fast clean execution, NOT vice versa. If Phase 1 is rushed, Phase 3 will discover problems that should have been resolved at the spec level, and rework cascades. If Phase 1 is thorough — concept locked, honesty audit done, beat tables computed — the build is pure execution.

### The template for every future process loop on this site

**Step 1 — Decide the decision first.** Before any motion design, name the judgment the loop demonstrates. One sentence. If you can't write that sentence, you don't have a loop yet — you have an animation.

**Step 2 — Generate 2-3 concepts that all commit to that decision differently.** Grade against four bars: decision-tension (not motion), distinct thinking-type (vs other slots), interview-defensible (real call Rotem made), real components (no fabricated UI).

**Step 3 — Resolve concept + honesty + distinctness at the spec level.** Beat-by-beat timeline with absolute T-stamps. Visual consistency checklist against existing demos. Honesty audit per beat. Distinctness contract vs hero + other body demos.

**Step 4 — Build is pure execution.** Pre-phase backup. Markup + CSS + JS + cache-bust + script tag + verification. Match planned T-stamps exactly. End-of-phase gate outputs the planned-vs-implemented table.

**Step 5 — The cursor is the agent making the decision the loop is about.** Build → point → wait per beat. Polyrhythmic dwells. clickStamp at every commit. Real product components as subject. Mirror-action seam.

**This is the template.** Future process loops on rotemgotlieb.com — Pulse, LexisNexis, any new case study body demos at the rhetorical spine — follow these five steps. Decide the decision first, then choreograph the cursor to show it.


## 2026-05-27 — Engine primitive hardening: Marker variant warning + self-documentation standing rule

**What I did:** Two changes in the same session, prompted by the DP-B Minor marker silent-invisible bug:

1. **Hardened `js/demos/_engine/marker.js`** with a `KNOWN_VARIANTS = new Set(['default', 'green', 'orange', 'yellow', 'red'])` module-level constant and a `warnUnknownVariant()` helper. Constructor + `setVariant()` both check against the set; unknown variants trigger `console.warn` (NOT throw) with the canonical text: `[Marker] Unknown variant "X". A matching .demo-marker--X CSS rule must exist in styles.css or the marker will render invisible. Supported variants: default, green, orange, yellow, red.`

2. **Added "KNOWN CONSTRAINTS / GOTCHAS" comment block to marker.js top-of-file** documenting six numbered constraints: the variant-CSS-rule rule (load-bearing #1), variants-not-arbitrary-hex, position-relative container, container-local CENTER coords, setTimeout-based promise resolution (NOT transitionend), setVariant doesn't re-render.

3. **Added new standing rule to [.claude/CLAUDE.md](.claude/CLAUDE.md): "Engine primitive self-documentation rule."** Six rules: document discoveries inline in the primitive file (NOT just learnings.md), use a `KNOWN CONSTRAINTS / GOTCHAS` section template, promote silent-failure modes to runtime warnings, define closed sets as `KNOWN_X` module constants, update the comment block in same commit as engine changes, apply retroactively when touching any engine file.

**Test result (PASS):** ran a node test with minimal DOM stubs. 6 known-variant constructor calls (red/orange/yellow/green/default + default-via-no-opts) produced 0 warnings. 3 unknown-variant calls (purple/blue/magenta) produced 3 warnings with the expected canonical text. Test artifact cleaned up post-verification.

**Cache-bust:** styles.css v=58 → v=59 across 11 HTML; ghost-severity-build.js v=2 → v=3 + ghost-critical-three.js v=2 → v=3 in work/ghost.html. Engine module imports are unversioned (project convention) — bumping the demos that import the engine ensures the import graph is re-evaluated on next load.

**What I learned:**

1. **Silent invisibility is the worst failure mode for visual primitives.** No error, no warning, no console log — just a feature that doesn't render. The DP-B Minor marker took until QA to surface because the loop kept running cleanly. A `console.warn` is the cheapest defense; it costs zero in production (browsers throttle/dedupe console output) and surfaces the bug the instant the demo loads in dev.

2. **`KNOWN_X` module constants are the right shape for closed config sets.** Three benefits: (a) machine-checkable via runtime warning, (b) discoverable via grep, (c) single source of truth that the CSS rule list mirrors. Without the constant, the "valid values" live in the author's head + scattered through CSS, and drift is inevitable.

3. **Engine documentation belongs in the engine file, not just in learnings.md.** Learnings.md is the discovery log — chronological, narrative, lossy. The primitive's file is the operating manual — declarative, scannable, current. A future session reading `marker.js` to use it shouldn't have to grep learnings.md to discover that variants need CSS rules. The hard-won knowledge must be inline at the point of use. **Standing rule added to .claude/CLAUDE.md to enforce this going forward.**

4. **Apply the standing rule retroactively.** When I open `cursor.js` next, I should audit its comment block for missing constraints (the V3 easing-by-context rule? the per-move duration override? the transitionend-NOT-snapTo distinction?) and add a KNOWN CONSTRAINTS section if missing. Same for `popup.js`, `choreography.js`, `observer.js`, `motion.js`, `reduced-motion.js`. The first touch is the right moment.

**Files touched:**
- `js/demos/_engine/marker.js` — added KNOWN_VARIANTS + warnUnknownVariant + comment block (6 numbered constraints); ~155 lines (was 92)
- `.claude/CLAUDE.md` — new "Engine primitive self-documentation rule" section
- 11 HTML files — cache-bust styles.css v=58 → v=59
- `work/ghost.html` — bumped ghost-severity-build.js + ghost-critical-three.js to v=3


## 2026-05-27 — Process Loops sprint closed: visual approval + Phase 5 wrap

**What I did:** User confirmed both bottom-of-Ghost loops (DP-B Severity Vocabulary Build + OC-E Critical Three) at visual review — the arc reads, the polyrhythmic cognition dwells land as "watch me think," the yellow "3" marker fix is visually verified. Sprint formally closed. Updated all three Phase 5 deliverables to reflect the post-build state:

1. **Sprint report** [2026-05-27-process-loops.md](sprint-reports/2026-05-27-process-loops.md) — added Section 9 "Visual review outcome" documenting user approval, the same-session yellow marker bug fix, the Marker primitive hardening (KNOWN_VARIANTS + warnUnknownVariant + 6-constraint KNOWN CONSTRAINTS block), and the new "Engine primitive self-documentation rule" standing rule. Updated metrics: 5 cache-bust monotonic bumps total (v=54 → v=59), 1 engine primitive hardened, 1 standing rule added.

2. **canonical-motion-spec Appendix D** — extended with §D.7 (the five-step template "decide the decision first, then choreograph the cursor to show it" — canonical operating procedure for future process loops), §D.8 (pattern status: shipped + visually approved + reference status established), §D.9 (related standing rules — Engine self-documentation + Body-demo discipline — that form the operating envelope alongside Appendix D).

3. **learnings.md** — this closing entry.

**What carries forward beyond this sprint:**

- **The five-step process-loop template is now persistent at THREE layers** for future Claude Code sessions on Pulse, LexisNexis, or any new case study body slot:
  - **Codebase:** canonical-motion-spec.md Appendix D + the two worked examples (ghost-severity-build.js + ghost-critical-three.js)
  - **Project learnings:** the "Why the severity-vocabulary loop landed" breakthrough-pattern entry + the engine-hardening entry
  - **User memory:** feedback_process_loop_template.md (auto-loaded into future sessions via MEMORY.md index)

- **Engine self-documentation is now standing discipline.** marker.js is the template for the comment-block shape. Six other engine primitives (cursor.js, choreography.js, popup.js, observer.js, motion.js, reduced-motion.js) get their KNOWN CONSTRAINTS sections audited on first touch. This is a multi-session commitment — apply opportunistically as files come up for other reasons.

- **The visual-review → bug-fix → engine-hardening → standing-rule cascade** is itself a worth-keeping pattern. A single demo bug surfaced an engine constraint that wasn't documented, which surfaced an absence-of-documentation pattern at the engine layer, which became a standing rule. Three layers of fix from one bug. **Future sessions: when a bug surfaces a missing constraint in an engine primitive, the fix has three parts — the bug fix itself, the inline constraint documentation, and (if absent) the meta-rule that enforces the inline-documentation discipline.**

**The sprint shipped what it set out to ship.** Two new body demos validated by user review, the canonical pattern codified for future portfolios, the engine primitive hardened against silent failures, the discovery-to-documentation pipeline made standing discipline. Production deploy is Rotem's call — code-level work complete.

## 2026-05-28 — Key Decisions Loop Sprint: K-A "The Editorial Strip" + fourth gesture-signature codified

**What I did:** Replaced the dark static Overlay screenshot in the Ghost Key Decisions section with a fourth cursor-as-agent body demo — **K-A "The Editorial Strip"** ([js/demos/ghost-restraint-strip.js](../js/demos/ghost-restraint-strip.js)). Cursor strips text-label color from 4 deviation rows in sequence; severity dots beside each row STAY in their canonical colors. 7000ms loop. Polyrhythmic cognition dwells 350/280/240/320 ms (decisive → questioning → recognising → edge-case-recheck for yellow). 1580ms terminal absorption holds the clinical end state. Mirror-action seam.

Argues the Key Decisions prose's primary claim — "severity colors only where they carry meaning. Restraint as an active editorial choice."

The four cursor-driven body demos on the page now form a four-act narrative: **DP-B builds the language → K-A applies editorial restraint → OC-E wields at scale → hero executes a fix**.

**What worked:**

1. **The five-step process-loop template from Appendix D §D.7 applied for the THIRD time without modification.** Decide the decision first (what earns color) → generate 3 concepts graded against four bars → resolve concept + honesty + distinctness at spec level (heavy upfront ~580-line design doc) → build was pure execution (Phase 3 implementation matched planned T-stamps exactly with zero re-scoping) → cursor IS the agent making the decision. The template is now battle-tested across three sprints (DP-B, OC-E, K-A) — promoted from "breakthrough pattern" to "canonical operating procedure for process loops on this site."

2. **The pointwise property-level edit as a fourth gesture signature is genuinely novel for the page.** Hero = action execution; DP-B = additive build; OC-E = subtractive whole-element fade; **K-A = pointwise property-level edit.** The "color leaves the text while the dot holds" gesture is visually unmistakable from any whole-element treatment. Codified in canonical-motion-spec.md §D.10 as the fourth entry in the gesture-signature catalogue.

3. **Distinctness on a page with N process loops requires more than mechanism difference — it requires signature-category difference.** Once a page has 3 process loops, naive "different content" or "different layout" doesn't carry distinctness. The new loop must either introduce a new gesture-signature category OR distinguish on multiple supporting axes (markers / popup / surface / dwell rhythm). K-A introduced category #4 AND distinguished on every supporting axis. This is why it works.

4. **Heavy upfront design doc (Phase 1) keeps Phase 3 fast and clean.** The Phase 1 design doc was 580 lines covering 7 sections. Phase 3 build was ~3 hours of implementation against a locked spec. End-of-phase gate output (planned-vs-implemented beat table) showed zero T-stamp drift across 17 timeline beats. **The ratio of design time to build time was ~2:1; the build was "pure execution."** This is the same pattern that worked for DP-B + OC-E.

5. **Zero engine-primitive extensions needed.** K-A uses Cursor + Choreography + LoopObserver + prefersReduced + getCenterOf. NO Marker, NO Popup, NO blurCrossfade. The DELIBERATE absence of these primitives is the distinctness signature — the strip itself is the editorial gesture, no numbered pin or modal needed. **Confirms that the engine is sized correctly for this class of demo; further demos should mostly compose existing primitives rather than extend them.**

**What failed or surprised me:**

1. **Risk 4 from the design doc (strip might look like an opacity fade) was the riskiest read at the spec level.** The mitigation — text ENDS more legible (#1A1A1A on white = 21:1 contrast vs red on white ~5:1) — is structurally correct, but the perceptual read can only be confirmed at visual review. I was uncertain at spec time; the mitigation pattern (the stripped state must be SHARPER than the loud state, never DIMMER) is the right defensive frame for any future "remove color" loops.

2. **Tempting to use `Marker` to record the per-strip judgment** — the V2/V3 instinct is "each cursor click commits a decision; mark it with a numbered pin." I deliberately rejected this for K-A because the strip itself is the visible record (the text is now black; that IS the marker). Going markerless was a distinctness call (DP-B has 3 colored, OC-E has 3 red, hero has 1 green — K-A's zero is the signature). **Lesson: not every decision-per-click loop needs marker pins. The pin is one option among several for "recording the commit"; the visible material change is another.**

**What I learned (carries forward to future portfolio process loops on Pulse, LexisNexis, or any new case study):**

1. **The gesture-signature catalogue at canonical-motion-spec.md §D.10 is the new distinctness gating mechanism for multi-loop pages.** When a page has 2+ process loops, any new loop must clear signature distinctness. Currently catalogued: action execution / additive build / subtractive whole-element fade / pointwise property-level edit. **The catalogue is non-exhaustive; future demos may introduce signature #5.** The discipline is: name the signature category before building.

2. **Remove-color loops have a specific perceptual trap: they can look like fades.** The mitigation pattern is: the stripped state must be SHARPER (higher contrast) than the loud state, not dimmer. Lower opacity → reads as fade. Lower saturation while preserving luminance → reads as edit. K-A goes from red text (~5:1 contrast) to black text (21:1 contrast) on white — the text gets MORE legible after the strip, not less. **Apply this pattern to any future "removing color / removing emphasis" demo.**

3. **Pre-phase backups + monotonic cache-bust + grep-based orphan verification remains the right minimum discipline** for any multi-phase sprint. This sprint: 3 backup directories (Phase 0 full snapshot + Phase 2 + Phase 3 lightweight), 1 cache-bust bump (v=59 → v=60), grep verified 0 orphan `process-05` references across active code. No phase rolled back; no broken script tags; smoke test 7/7 green at sprint close.

4. **The page is now coherent at 5 cursor-loops.** Pre-sprint, the dark Overlay screenshot was the discontinuity. Post-sprint, every artifact on the case study spine is cursor-driven (or user-driven via slider/chips) and styled in the light-system aesthetic. **This is the saturation ceiling for a single case study — further additions risk diluting the rhythm; any future visual addition on this page should default to prose-only or static-illustration unless it can clear the gesture-signature distinctness bar.**

**Files touched:**

- **Created:** `js/demos/ghost-restraint-strip.js` (276 lines, ~10 KB raw), `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-28-key-decisions-loop-design.md` (~580 lines), `.claude/sprint-reports/2026-05-28-key-decisions-loop.md`
- **Modified:** `styles.css` (+182 lines for `/* DEMO: GHOST RESTRAINT STRIP */` block; file 4528 → 4710), `work/ghost.html` (+48 lines net: -3 for removed Overlay div, +51 for new widget + script tag), `.claude/canonical-motion-spec.md` (+§D.10 gesture-signature catalogue), all 11 active HTML files (cache-bust v=59 → v=60)
- **Removed (markup only):** the `<div class="case-image">` block holding the dark Overlay screenshot at work/ghost.html
- **Preserved:** `img/ghost/process-05.png` (229 KB) on disk per user direction — kept for potential future surfaces
